Figure 4: Iterative design process.
in on a single design concept at the beginning of the project
and stick with it throughout. But, this precludes the opportu-
nity to come up with a better system that reflects the unique
combination of loads and design integration opportunities for
this specific building.
This better design solution usually evolves during schemat-
ics and early design development in the iterative process—and
the green-savvy mechanical engineer will approach the project
with this in mind. The challenge is to sell the client and the
other members of the design team on the desirability of this
approach, to the point that the client will provide the extra de-
sign time and fee that this requires.
In the author’s experience, it is often possible to make the
case for this on a life-cycle cost basis, as the improvements in
the design usually far outweigh the added design costs. In other
cases, increases in fees or time are not possible, and the itera-
tive design process only becomes achievable if the design team
has sufficient expertise to be able to make up the time and cost
in other aspects of the process.
The roles of all project team participants (owners, design-
ers, constructors, and operators) are critical to the success of
this process, as virtually all disciplines in all project phases are
affected by the sustainability goals. Key specific goals for some
of the major players are outlined here.
Owner’s Role: The owner is the most crucial project par-
ticipant in making a green building happen. If the owner is not
committed to sustainability, the design, construction and op-
erations teams will not receive the empowerment they need
to accomplish it. Specific activities that the owner can perform
in this regard include:
• Establish basic value system:
· “What’s important.”
• Selection of the team;
• Setting budgets and schedules;
• Maintaining commitment throughout project; and
• Demand/encourage integrated thinking among disciplines.
• Loads reduction:
· Reduce every load to the mini-
mum; and
· Evaluate loads implications from
every system.
• Systems efficiency:
· Meet the reduced loads as effi-
ciently as possible; and
· Look for ways to serve loads
from other systems (example:
thermal mass).
• Regenerative Systems:
· Use waste energy for useful pur-
poses; and
· Waste from one system can be
input to another system.
• Renewable Sources:
· Serve loads on-site and renewably (can be energy, waste,
water); and
· Integrate renewable systems into the entire project (cross
disciplines).
• Commission the active systems :
· Document expected performance metrics for every ac-
tive system;
· Test the actual performance of each system; and
· Remediate systems until expected performance is
achieved.
The graph in Figure 3 conceptually shows the impact
of providing design input at the various stages of a project rela-
tive to the amount of cost and effort. It shows with
the potential benefit/impact curve that it is easier to accomplish
major impacts on the performance of the building if you start at
the earliest stages in programming and schematic design. The
available impacts diminish thereafter as you proceed through
design development, construction documents and then through
construction, commissioning and into building operations.
A corollary of this is that the cost of implementing con-
cepts that can improve the performance of the building rise
through each successive project stage. The degree of effort
line is shown as the second element of this graph. The moral
of the story is to do as much up front as you can in planning
and setting of design goals and criteria to have the most im-
pact and least cost to implement.
Beyond the need to start early, this process leads to the con-
cept of iterative design, wherein the design is refined over time,
as shown in Figure 4. Often fee and schedule pressures lead
the designer to want to (or need to, for business reasons) lock
There is compelling evidence from other authors who also regard
commissioning as an intrinsic part of IDP to ensure that the modeled
performance is achieved, especially when technologies such as daylighting
and dimming systems or condensing boilers are installed. These technologies
require proper commissioning to ensure that the energy savings are realized
or the equipment performs as intended.
S26
B u i l d i n g f o r t h e Fu t u r e | A S u p p l e m e n t t o A S H R A E J o u r n a l
S e p t e m b e r 2 0 0 4
Building energy performance simulation is a powerful
tool for integrated design that enables architects,
engineers, and developers to analyze in an integrated
way how the form, size, orientation, and type of building
systems affect overall building energy consumption.
This information is vital for making informed design
decisions about building systems that impact energy
use, including envelope, glazing, lighting, and HVAC.
It is often the case that a few building simulation runs
in the early phases of a project can lead to design
solutions that, though they appear simple, significantly
improve building energy performance.
This was the case for the Public Works Building at
the Naval Construction Battalion Center at Port
Hueneme in Southern California. For this project, a
5,000 ft2 (465 m2) addition was planned in concert with
a major renovation of an existing 10,000 ft2 (929 m2)
Energy Simulation at U.S. Naval Building
S26
B u i l d i n g f o r t h e Fu t u r e | A S u p p l e m e n t t o A S H R A E J o u r n a l
S e p t e m b e r 2 0 0 4
Design Team Role: The role of the design team can be
defined as:
• Setting sustainability goals:
· Energy optimization;
· Materials selections;
· Systems integration; and
· Environmental impact minimization.
• Following the design optimization sequence (described
earlier):
• Documenting design intent;
• Commissioning the building;
• Training the building operators; and
• Post-occupancy evaluation and feedback.
Contractors’ Role:
• Ensure that all trades understand their job-site sustainability
responsibilities;
• Monitor and document sustainability activities;
• Cooperate with commissioning authorities; and
• Control adherence with specifications regarding
sustainability.
Operations Role:
• Involve building operations personnel in commissioning
testing; and
• Train building operators in all sustainability-related
systems.
Applications to ASHRAE Members
To focus this discussion on the roles played by ASHRAE
members, this section drills down in more detail on how to
apply these concepts to a project. This starts with defining the
Engineer’s role as it relates to sustainable design activities, then
looks specifically at integration issues related to the HVAC
system design.
Engineer’s Role: The role of the engineer in the green de-
sign process can be expanded beyond the somewhat constrained
role that the traditional project approach and scope allows. Some
of the ways in which this can occur are outlined next.
First, it recognizes that the design process requires devel-
opment of design alternatives, which must be refined, evalu-
ated, evolved, and optimized collaboratively with other design
disciplines. To allow this, time and budget must be provided
for the iterative process.
Second, it will encourage discussions among the project team
of how to achieve the performance goals: system by system,
with particular emphasis on interdependence between various
systems and design disciplines and on the integrated design
activities needed to accomplish this.
Third, it will lead to rigorous documentation of the design
intent for the systems being designed, as a basis for future ref-
erence during construction, commissioning, and operations.
This includes setting goals for performance relative to energy,
environmental, comfort, and operating cost.
Fourth, it will lead to establishing mechanisms for verify-
ing that design intent is met, in design, in construction,
in commissioning, and in operations. In design, this may in-
clude peer review, system coordination between design dis-
ciplines, and including commissioning processes and criteria
in the design documents. In construction, it can include per-
forming commissioning of building systems. In operations,
it can include:
• Providing training to building operators, so they know how
the systems are intended to operate sustainably;
Figure 5: U.S. Navy Public Works Building.
administrative building. The existing building was long
and narrow, with the long orientations facing north and
south. The owner originally planned to build the
See Naval Building, Page S28
S e p t e m b e r 2 0 0 4 B u i l d i n g f o r t h e Fu t u r e | A S u p p l e m e n t t o A S H R A E J o u r n a l S27
INTEGRATED DESIGN
• Performing post-occupancy evaluation and recommission-
ing of systems, to ensure that systems continue to perform as
intended; and
• Performing measurement and verification of energy and
water use of building, to correlate with design predictions.
Finally, one of the key features of many green buildings is
their use of multiple, innovative systems that require unusual
amounts of coordination and integration between design dis-
ciplines. Examples of these systems include daylighting,
underfloor air distribution, natural ventilation, thermal
displacement ventilation, and radiant cooling. Such systems
virtually demand that the engineer take a leading role in
defining the design requirements and points of interface with
other design disciplines, if a successful design is to be
achieved.
The sustainable design sequence defined in Figure 3 is ide-
ally suited to giving the HVAC engineer a leading role in pur-
suing design sustainability. Steps One, Three, and Four of
that sequence require close integration with other members
of the project team, while Step Two can be largely pursued
within the traditional realm of the HVAC engineer. Each of
these is discussed in detail next.
Step One: Loads Reduction. The reduction of cooling and
heating loads is highly dependent upon other members of the
project team. Many decisions that determine the loads are
made quite early in the project design, so getting involved
early is crucial if this step is to succeed. Mechanical engi-
neers should be involved in helping to make decisions in all
stages of design. Key aspects of this include the following
issues (and the other team members involved):
• Reduce envelope loads.
· Reduce solar loads:
 Building orientation (architect, owner, energy con-
sultant);
 Shading (architect);
 Glass selection and glass area/location (architect,
owner, mechanical designer, modeler/energy con-
sultant ); and
 Passive solar gains through increasing building mass
or using trombe wall designs (architect, owner, me-
chanical designer, modeler/energy consultant).
· Conduction loads: walls and roofs (architect, mechani-
cal designer, modeler/energy consultant).
• Reduce lighting loads.
· Lighting design (architect, electrical engineer or lighting
designer, mechanical designer, modeler/energy consultant);
· Lighting controls (electrical engineer, architect, modeler
energy consultant );
• Reduce power loads.
· Select Energy-Star equipment (owner);
addition perpendicular to the existing building,
creating a T-shaped form.
A member of the design team, however, feared that
orienting the addition in this way would require an
excessively large air-conditioning unit to maintain
comfortable conditions in the morning and afternoon due
to direct sun from the east and west, and would also
reduce the potential for good daylight penetration. Instead,
he proposed to site the addition parallel to the existing
structure and connect the two with a combined hallway
and restroom structure, thus making an H-shaped form.
The team performed a simulation to determine the
energy impact of the proposed H-shaped building. The
model showed that, relative to the T-shape, the building
would be more comfortable, the capacity of the cooling
system would be 25% smaller, and the daylighting
controls would be much more effective. As a result of
this analysis, the client opted for the H configuration,
as well as glazing and wall construction details that
provided maximum daylight harvesting. Even though
the designer’s intuition about the impact was correct,
building simulation made it possible to project both the
construction and operating costs of the two alternatives
with greater confidence.
As this example shows, an architect’s choice of wall
construction, glass type, and building form can have
a profound impact on sizing mechanical and electrical
systems.
Although many designers are qualitatively aware of
the high degree of interaction between different building
systems, it is difficult—and in some cases impossible—
to accurately quantify those interactions without using
building simulation. Typically, many designers address
such issues either by following rules of thumb, by relying
on intuition, or by ignoring them altogether.
By using building simulation to help make decisions,
designers and developers can provide more value to
the building operators who must eventually pay the
utility bills, and they also can protect themselves by
providing a quantitative basis for design decisions.
Both the mechanical designer and modeler need to be involved from the
beginning, so they can advise the architect on the impact of different glazing system
options on mechanical equipment size. They can, for example show that very high
performance glazing can drastically reduce the need for perimeter heating, allowing
the mechanical designer to reduce the size of the boiler piping and radiators.
If daylighting and dimming systems are being considered, the modeler needs
to tell the architect what the benefits are from introduction of architectural features
to promote daylighting. Similarly, the modeler needs to know the control
schemes that the lighting designer proposes in order to model the impact.
Naval Building, From Page S26
S27
B u i l d i n g f o r t h e Fu t u r e | A S u p p l e m e n t t o A S H R A E J o u r n a l
S e p t e m b e r 2 0 0 4
S28
B u i l d i n g f o r t h e Fu t u r e | A S u p p l e m e n t t o A S H R A E J o u r n a l
S e p t e m b e r 2 0 0 4
· Design metering for tracking end-use loads (electrical
engineer).
• Reduce ventilation loads.
· Natural ventilation (architect, owner, energy consultant,
mechanical designer).
Step Two: Optimize System Efficiency. Whatever level of
cooling and heating loads finally are determined, the design of
the HVAC systems to perform efficiently under those loads is
largely up to the HVAC engineer. This requires the selection of
the best combination of peak and average system efficiency.
However, even in this step, the engineer must be an effective
advocate to other team members (whether it is for adequate
space for cooling towers, ducts, or HVAC equipment, or for
using part of the load-reduction savings to buy higher efficiency
equipment or better controls). Some examples of the issues the
engineer faces here are:
• Equipment right sizing (reducing loads allows reductions not
just of the cooling plant and cooling tower size, but also airflows,
air-handling unit size, ductwork, pumps and heating plants):
· Can provide higher A/C efficiency for same cost;
• High-efficiency equipment:
· Systems must be responsive to partial loads;
 Conduction loads: walls and roofs (architect, me-
chanical designer, modeler/energy consultant); and
 80% of year, system operates at less than 50% of
peak capacity.
· Emphasis on “free” cooling and heating;
 Economizers (air, water);
 Evaporative cooling (cooling towers, pre-cooling);
and
 Heat recovery.
• Emphasis on indoor air quality (IAQ).
• Keep control zones small to ensure comfort with reduced
energy use.
• New approaches to air distribution (underfloor air distribu-
tion, thermal displacement ventilation, radiant cooling) are new
techniques that depend heavily upon design integration to be
cost-effective.
Step Three: Regenerative Systems. Using waste energy for
useful purposes is simply said, but what does it mean for HVAC
design? It means looking at the entire energy balance of the build-
ing at once, to determine where energy is being rejected (and the
quality and quantity of that heat). Examples include:
• Heat rejection from cooling and heating systems;
• Enthalpy content of air intake/exhaust systems;
• Heat rejection from process loads (air compressors, etc.);
and
• Cogeneration systems (on-site power production that pro-
duces useful waste heat).
It means then brainstorming the opportunities for using those
waste energy streams to drive other systems. Examples include:
• Precooling outside air;
• Preheating makeup water for hot water heating; and
• Driving absorption cooling cycles.
All of this is well-known in principle, but it is startling how
seldom waste energy streams are used. This design sequence
demands that it be considered, and the result is often an im-
proved thermodynamic and economic performance of the
building systems.
Step Four: Renewable Sources. Using renewable sources
to serve loads on-site and renewably is the final step in this
process. The loads have been driven to a minimum; the sys-
tems serving the remaining loads are as efficient as possible;
possible uses of recovered waste energy have been tapped. Now,
the challenge is to serve as much of the remaining energy in-
put requirements as possible from renewable sources. Again,
the approach is to integrate renewable systems into the entire
project, looking across all technical disciplines and system
boundaries for opportunities for renewables. The potentials can
include energy systems, waste systems, water systems, and
process systems.
Examples include:
• Photovoltaic electric,
• Solar thermal,
• Night-sky radiation, and
• Hydraulic flows.

Cut-off Point
Budget Limit
S e p t e m b e r 2 0 0 4 B u i l d i n g f o r t h e Fu t u r e | A S u p p l e m e n t t o A S H R A E J o u r n a l S29
INTEGRATED DESIGN
Cost-Effectiveness of Integrated Design
One of the common misperceptions of sustainable design is
that it necessarily leads to higher cost of construction than tradi-
tional projects. It is actually much more likely that costs are higher
if an integrated approach to the design is not taken. Likewise,
costs will be higher if changes are made late in the design pro-
cess to incorporate sustainability as an “add-on” to the prior de-
sign. The emphasis of sustainable design tends to be upon
lowering the life-cycle costs; in contrast, many project teams
tend to downplay life-cycle cost and are focused only on first
cost. However, it is possible to achieve overall first cost reduc-
tions as well with the integrated design approach, by taking credit
for savings from one design element (e.g., reduced HVAC ton-
nage from a daylighting system, which reduces internal heat gains
from lighting in exchange for changes in architectural and struc-
tural elements to accommodate the daylighting system).
The traditional integrated design cost-savings paradox is:1
• Upgrade approach (Figure 6):
· Standard design is upgraded with energy-efficient
equipment;
· 30% energy savings with five-year simple payback;
• Integrated approach (Figure 7):
· Design philosophy incorporates energy issues;
· Building designed to meet the budget;
· 50% energy savings at little or no capital cost impact.
Several barriers to achieving cost-effective integration
derive from the typical sequencing of design activities.
Using HVAC design as an example, some of the ways this
occurs include:
• HVAC capacity is usually calculated (and equipment se-
lected) before electrical lighting design is done or glass choice
is finalized. Seldom does this get updated for final design data,
so the equipment is typically oversized.
• HVAC is oversized to accommodate estimated lighting
power densities and plug loads that are determined during
T/I design phase. Of course, the challenge is how to account
for the unknown and provide sufficient capacity and flexibil-
ity without oversizing. This requires integrating the require-
ments of the building’s T/I standards, as well as designing
the HVAC system to perform efficiently at part load if it turns
out to be oversized.
• HVAC and electrical systems are not given adequate space,
so ductwork may have to be undersized to fit, resulting in higher
pressure drops and increased noise levels.
Example: Financial Benefit of “Right Sizing” HVAC Systems
• Reducing load through load minimization and right siz-
ing translates into first cost savings: (e.g., a 1,000 ton peak
load reduced to 600 tons saves 400 tons × $2,000/ton =
$800,000 savings).
To overcome this, several steps can be taken, including:
• Ensure different design disciplines are coordinating on
process as well as on technical details. Specifically, noting
what the critical path is on decisions that affect sustainability
of the building enable the disciplines to work together to avoid
these problems.
• Clarify the performance goals, and make sure that each
discipline understands its role and responsibilities in achiev-
ing these goals.
• Share and communicate the design assumptions made by
each discipline that depend upon other disciplines. Examples
include:
· Lighting power densities set by lighting designer that
affect the HVAC loads; and
· Occupant loads set by interiors architect that affects
HVAC ventilation rates and loads.
• Task someone with the design integration management role;
this can be accomplished by the architect (but should be some-
one other than the project manager, who has too many other
things on his mind) or by specialty consultants such as:
· Energy consultant/modeler;
· Green consultant; or
· Commissioning authority.
Summary and Conclusions
Integrated design expands the design possibilities, creating
buildings that are better performing and more cost-effective.
Achieving this potential requires a holistic and structured ap-
proach to the design process, including setting goals, breaking
through disciplinary boundaries, using iterative analysis and re-
finement of design options, and following-through on details.
For mechanical engineers, it offers opportunities to expand their
horizons and think beyond the traditional limits of HVAC de-
sign; at the same time, it challenges us to take on a broader role
in the projects we work on. It is a process that works, and that
typically produces designs that are inspiring and work better!
References
1. Hawken, Lovins, and Lovins. 1999. Natural Capitalism. Little,
Brown & Co.
Bibliography
ASHRAE SP-102, Advanced Energy Design Guide for Small Of-
fice Buildings.
Grumman, D., ed. 2004. ASHRAE GreenGuide.
Lovins, A. 1992. Energy Efficient Buildings: Institutional Barriers
and Opportunities. E Source.
Tuluca, A. 1997. Energy Efficient Design and Construction for
Commercial Buildings. McGraw-Hill: N.Y.
Whole Building Design Guide. www.wbdg.org/
Los Alamos Sustainable Design Guide
www.eere.energy.gov/buildings/highperformance/lanl\_sustainable\_
guide.html
USGBC Reference Manual.
Malcolm Lewis, D.Eng., P.E., is president of CTG Energet-
ics in Irvine, Calif.
tons × 3.517 = kW
S 5 2 B u i l d i n g f o r t h e Fu t u r e | A S u p p l e m e n t t o A S H R A E J o u r n a l
S e p t e m b e r 2 0 0 4
BUILDING FOR THE FUTURE
By David Gottfried
GUEST COLUMN
I believe the sustainable discussion and impetus is about
value. And, what’s valuable in life? Life itself. Next, is the
ability to sustain life, and ways to enhance life, and so on. I
call these items the value rings, with life as the bull’s-eye. And
yet, our buildings as we’ve made them for the past century
have contributed to problems with our continued ability to sus-
tain life: poor air quality, global warming, water contamina-
tion and elimination of precious limited resources.
So why aren’t all buildings sustainable? Many of our sys-
tems and methods were developed in an
era of different ethics and societal abun-
dance. We didn’t worry about waste and
consumption. We invented an economy
and products and services based on growth and consumption.
Protecting life and the ability to sustain life were not part of
the prevailing ethic and, therefore, not valued higher monetarily.
The New Game
All of that is changing, as a tidal wave washing away ineffi-
cient standard practices. The new game is about supplying high
quality and aesthetic products with the least environmental
impact and highest level of occupant health. Major manufac-
turers, building developers, contractors, architects, engineers,
users and environmental organizations are embracing this ethic.
The new rules comprise of consensus-based leadership stan-
dards that can be third-party verified. Life-cycle assessment
(LCA) methodology will govern future creations. LCA is a
thorough analysis based on a standard approach (ISO and
ASTM standards) that evaluates a product’s environmental
impact over its full life: from material extraction, fabrication,
shipping, assembly, use and ultimate disposal.
Evidence of environmental awareness and practices is the
U.S. and World Green Building Councils’ explosive growth
with about 5,000 organizational members in 10 countries. The
U.S. Green Building Council’s (USGBC) LEED-NC (new
construction) Green Building Rating System is used by sev-
eral hundred million square feet of building space, even
though the system is less than five years old. LEED-EB (ex-
isting building) and LEED-CI (commercial interiors) are fin-
ishing a pilot stage and are launching soon. Many other
LEED® standards are planned.
Green building is becoming its own industry. ASHRAE
clearly understands this by offering its GreenGuide, as well as
its ever-improving standards for air quality, energy efficiency,
thermal comfort and commissioning (all referenced in LEED).
Even though green building in the U.S. is more than a de-
cade old, it is early on in the adoption curve. LEED-NC is only
used by about 5% of new construction, and LEED-EB and
LEED-CI are just beginning. Other sec-
tors such as residential, retail, hospitals
and schools are new to embracing green
building as a core ethic.
However, government has readily adopted green building as
evidenced by dozens of cities adopting green building ordi-
nances, and three states offering tax credits for LEED build-
ings. I can easily imagine within five years, all cities will
mandate LEED for their own buildings, and have an array of
project incentives for the private sector’s adoption of LEED.
Some cities, such as Seattle, already offer LEED-based in-
centives, amounting to $25,000 or more. In California, utili-
ties have a program called Savings by Design (www.savings
bydesign.com) that rewards up to $150,000 to building own-
ers and $50,000 to the design team for achieving energy effi-
ciency in new construction.
As the marketplace becomes more educated and experienced
in the use of LEED and green building practices, costs are com-
ing down for building owners. A recent study showed green build-
ings cost no more than 2% of standard buildings (Capital-E study
for the state of California). Seattle has stated that it is achieving
LEED Silver for almost no extra first cost, based on its large
portfolio of LEED buildings. (Seattle was the first U.S. city to
mandate a level of LEED Silver for its own buildings—setting
off a citywide competition). Many design and construction firms
include green services as part of standard practice.
Accredited Professionals
The USGBC has the LEED Accredited Professional® exam,
which more than 10,000 professionals have passed. Many are
The following article was published in ASHRAE Journal, September 2004. © Copyright 2004 American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-
Conditioning Engineers, Inc. It is presented for educational purposes only. This article may not be copied and/or distributed electronically or in
paper form without permission of ASHRAE.
S e p t e m b e r 2 0 0 4 B u i l d i n g f o r t h e Fu t u r e | A S u p p l e m e n t t o A S H R A E J o u r n a l
S53
noting the accreditation on their business cards and résumés.
Some building owners mandate a LEED Accredited Profes-
sional be included on a bidding team.
I have advocated to the USGBC that they create a GBC
designation analogous to the AIA, CSI or P.E. designations.
The GBC designation would be achieved through passing two
exams (in the area of expertise, demonstrating overall knowl-
edge of green building and its practices, and on the various
LEED rating systems), and a tenure of sustainable building
experience. Continuing education would be required to keep
the license.
Practices
As green building accelerates its penetration into many sec-
tors of our industry, knowledge of its practices is becoming
common. Along with this shift comes a decrease in added pro-
fessional costs for requiring green as part of standard practice.
In the future, I believe we will see:
• Architects including product research, LEED
brainstorms, credit reviews and certification as part
of the standard scope of work.
• Engineers including energy and water model-
ing, commissioning and renewable and closed-loop
systems as part of their base services
• Software tools such as AutoCAD® embedding sus-
tainable building performance tools and a bridge into
the information required for LEED credit analysis; and
• Building owners understanding the value of a
green building, and demanding these services for all future
projects.
Education
The next large areas of education and transformation for green
building practices will be among building owners, developers
and financial institutions.
• As building tenants and brokers are educated about the
benefits of green building, they will require a high level of
green performance from buildings they lease.
• Appraisers will learn green buildings have significantly lower
operating costs (as high as a 15% overall reduction or more).
• Green buildings will be leased faster and at higher rental
rates, as shown by the 543 Howard Street building in downtown
San Francisco (its rents are 10% higher than the market rate).
• Tenant occupancy rates will increase for buildings that are
healthier, have more daylight, a higher level of individual con-
trol and comfort, are adaptable with tenant layout changes (such
as an underfloor air-distribution system and open-space design),
and have lower monthly costs for energy, water and waste.
Financial Trends
All of these items translate into a higher return on invest-
ment for the building owner, and even the opportunity to re-
duce the upfront equity requirement.
Future financial trends I envision include:
• Banks and pension funds understanding that green build-
ings are lower risk, and of higher value to their portfolio of
loans and investments.
• In time, I can see non-green buildings being devalued as
green becomes the norm.
• Banks requiring LEED for their loans, initially starting with
a better interest rate and higher loan to value ratio.
• On Wall Street, green REITs (Real Estate Investment
Trusts) will exist and a Green Exchange may become the
next NASDAQ.
Green Organizations
In addition to buildings, we will have nationally accepted
consensus standards for green building products and green or-
ganizations. These firms will embrace the ethic of life-cycle
assessment. Not only that, they will provide all of their data on
the Web for the public to review as they consider
purchase or investment decisions. Firms that fight
this approach will lose business and market leader-
ship as cooperatives form of those that publicly ad-
vocate and demonstrate this open method of
business and reporting.
An example is the newly formed Alliance for
the
Sustainable
Built
Environment
(www.sustainablebuiltenvironments.org). This is
a business consortium of six mainstream
multibillion dollar product manufacturers. They
intend to be a catalyst in the market for transformation to-
wards sustainability.
The Alliance’s formation is evidence that green is going main-
stream. Some of the leading industry companies see the enor-
mous magnitude of this new market and want to be the dominant
players. A book called The Cultural Creatives identifies this green
tidal wave as comprising 50 million Americans, and as our next
major societal culture.
Conclusion
Green is very much about value and our industry is trans-
forming its reach and impact to embrace the tenets of life and
life-sustaining techniques. The building industry is the world’s
largest at some $3 trillion, and is one of the most destructive.
How can we afford not to understand and embody standard
practices that provide good air quality, energy and water effi-
ciency, health and comfort for all? Adoption of these natural
principles will improve your firm’s bottom line, ensure the
planet’s survival, and contribute towards your own personal
contentment, as the thousands of USGBC members have found
over the last decade.
David
Gottfried
is
president
of
WorldBuild
(www.worldbuild.com), Berkeley, Calif., and is the founder of
the U.S. and World Green Building Councils. He is author of
the book Greed to Green (www.greedtogreen.com).
Gottfried
S 1 8 B u i l d i n g f o r t h e Fu t u r e | A S u p p l e m e n t t o A S H R A E J o u r n a l
S e p t e m b e r 2 0 0 4
BUILDING FOR THE FUTURE
By David Grumman, P.E., Fellow/Life Member ASHRAE
The ASHRAE GreenGuide debuted at the 2004 Anaheim
Winter Meeting and on www.ashrae.org in January. At the
meeting, the copies sold out, and a rush supplementary ship-
ment was required. Strong sales continued, and a second print-
ing was required within the first six months.
I believe its popularity can be attributed to a long-felt need
by the ASHRAE community. The green building movement is
growing, with strong impetus (and publications) from the U.S.
Green Building Council. ASHRAE wanted to participate in this,
and the ASHRAE GreenGuide is one of its first initiatives.
The Origin of ASHRAE GreenGuide
The publication was initiated in 2000
when then-ASHRAE President James Wolf
heard comments from ASHRAE’s
grassroots membership that the Society might be missing the
boat on green building design. (ASHRAE’s purview of techni-
cal expertise, after all, encompasses systems and system de-
signs that account for most building energy use.) He charged
then-President-Elect William Coad, P.E., with developing a
document about green building design.
Coad assigned development of a green building design
document to Technical Committee (TC) 1.10, Energy Re-
sources (now merged into TC 2.8, Building Environmental
Impacts and Sustainability). A small subcommittee was set
up consisting of Jordan Heiman, past chair of Standard Project
Committee 90.1; Sheila Hayter, P.E., then-chair of TC 1.10;
and the author, a retired energy consultant, as chair. (This
role eventually evolved into editor of the book.) The
subcommittee’s first meeting took place during the 2001
ASHRAE Winter Meeting.
Following input from interested parties, within and outside
the TC, on what the document should and should not be, an
outline was developed. The core portion of this outline basi-
cally tracked the HVAC&R design process and covered the
major systems and equipment those systems incorporated. A
significant portion, especially at the conceptual design stage,
was devoted to process — that is, how green design teams are
set up and how they should function.
The next and most difficult task was finding experts to write
on various topics, and then coaxing them to write their assigned
pieces. More than two dozen authors contributed to the ASHRAE
GreenGuide, some as authors of entire or portions of chapters
and some as authors of ASHRAE GreenTips.
One of the unanticipated features of the ASHRAE
GreenGuide evolved from the stated desire to make this docu-
ment practical for the user. Throughout the guide many tech-
niques, processes, measures, or special systems are described
succinctly in sidebars. Called
ASHRAE GreenTips these give the
designer enough familiarity with a
subject to determine whether it might
be suitable for a certain project. Each
GreenTip concludes with a reference list for greater detail.
ASHRAE GreenTips help make this book unique among pub-
lications addressing green design.
At each Society meeting, a progress report was made to the
subcommittee and full TC, with comments received and noted.
This also provided an opportunity to seek authors for unwrit-
ten portions.
As the document neared completion in early 2003, a three-
person review panel was created within the TC. Nearly all changes
or additions suggested by the panel majority were incorporated
in the version provided for approval by the TC at its June 2003
meeting. Further comments and changes were made until the
last minute. In the end, TC approval was unanimous among those
present and voting. (An unanticipated review was initiated dur-
ing the following summer by Tech Council involving other TCs
deemed to have an interest. This also generated considerable
commentary.) The final book appeared in January 2004.
The whole process took nearly three years, and other than
ASHRAE Special Publications staff’s involvement in the last
two months, the entire effort was produced by voluntary au-
thors, editors, and reviewers.
GUEST COLUMN
‘ASHRAE’s Role in Green’
The following article was published in ASHRAE Journal, September 2004. © Copyright 2004 American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-
Conditioning Engineers, Inc. It is presented for educational purposes only. This article may not be copied and/or distributed electronically or in
paper form without permission of ASHRAE.
S e p t e m b e r 2 0 0 4 B u i l d i n g f o r t h e Fu t u r e | A S u p p l e m e n t t o A S H R A E J o u r n a l
S19
Where We Go From Here
The authors, developing subcommittee and TC have always
regarded the document as a work in progress. Some topics re-
ceive no or minimal coverage, due in some cases to the inabil-
ity to find an author with the expertise and willingness to tackle
certain topics. Some areas where I feel the document needs to
be expanded include: selection and use of materials; impact of
system types; diagrams, illustrations and photographs; and fi-
nally, more ASHRAE GreenTips (targeting 50 to 60 total).
Work has begun on a second edition, and a revision subcom-
mittee of TC 2.8, Building Enviromental Impact and
Sustainability, has been formed.
The Concepts of Green and Sustainability
The first chapter of the ASHRAE GreenGuide discusses in
depth the concepts of green and sustainability to clarify the
meanings of these terms as used in the book.
Both general and specific definitions of green are given. The
general definition addresses what is meant by green design,
saying:
“A design that is green is one that is aware of and re-
spects nature and the natural order of things; it
is a design that minimizes the negative human
impacts on the natural surroundings, materials,
resources and processes that prevail in nature.
It is not necessarily a concept that denies the
need for any human impact, for human exist-
ence is part of nature too. Rather it endorses
the belief that mankind can exist, multiply, build,
and prosper in accord with nature and the
earth’s natural processes without inflicting ir-
reversible damage to those processes and the
long-term habitability of the planet.”
Now this is a very broad definition — more like a belief or
credo about the nature of green. However, it does provide overall
guidance on what to consider a contributor to green design.
More specifics are provided in the following:
A green building is one that achieves high perfor-
mance, over the full life-cycle, in the following areas:
• Minimal consumption—due to reduction of need and
more efficient utilization— of non-renewable natural
resources, depletable energy resources, land, water and
other materials as well. (Corollary to this is maximiza-
tion of the effective use of renewable resources to meet
building needs.)
• Minimal atmospheric emissions having negative en-
vironmental impacts, especially those related to green-
house gases, global warming, particulates, or acid rain.
• Minimal discharge of harmful liquid effluents and solid
wastes, including those resulting from the ultimate demo-
lition of the building itself at the end of its useful life.
• Minimal negative impacts on site ecosystems.
• Maximum quality of indoor environment, including
air quality, thermal regime, illumination, acoustics/
noise and visual aspects.”
This definition is very close to those of other organizations
involved in green buildings, and it gives one some definitive pa-
rameters for measuring success.
The ASHRAE GreenGuide also states its preferred meaning
for sustainability, which often is cited in the same breath as
green — and that is the simple and direct “providing for the
needs of the present without detracting from the ability to ful-
fill the needs of the future.”
The common thread running through both definitions is mini-
mizing non-renewable energy resource use, avoiding environ-
mental degradation in its many forms, and reducing a building’s
negative impacts on its surroundings — all of which are di-
rected toward achieving sustainability of the planet over the
long term, leaving enough behind to sustain future generations.
Has Green’s Scope Become Too Broad?
The issue raised here is whether some of the characteristics
that have come to be included within the scope of green have
diverted attention away from green building design’s main pur-
pose: enabling the built environment to make a fair-
share contribution to Earth’s sustainability. A June
2004 Chicago Tribune article on “green architecture,”
for instance, cites such features as median planters,
avoiding inhospitable (interior) environments, uplift-
ing spirits and surroundings of occupants, drawing
people together, and reshaping the human ecosys-
tem, along with features that save energy, or at least
have the potential to do so.
While the foregoing characteristics are laudable
and worthy design goals, the question is whether
they belong as elements of green design or simply
elements of good design? What contribution do those charac-
teristics make toward energy efficiency or toward sustainability
as defined earlier?
Human productivity improvements, which studies purport
result from better indoor environments, certainly are a benefit
for the business or institution with improved worker satisfac-
tion and work output, but does this goal, however worthy, re-
ally contribute substantially to sustainability? (To be sure,
human productivity counts, and if conditions are so bad that
occupants cannot do their job, that could matter in the long
term. But, it is doubtful that most buildings, where no special
effort was made to create a particularly good indoor environ-
ment, are so bad that long-term sustainability is affected.)
The perceptive reader, at this point, may have wondered why
the last bullet of the ASHRAE GreenGuide’s specific green
definition—addressing the quality of the indoor environment—
was included, given the previous discussion. It was included
with reservations, the main reason being simply to maintain
compatibility with the definitions of other organizations.
Grumman
S 2 0 B u i l d i n g f o r t h e Fu t u r e | A S u p p l e m e n t t o A S H R A E J o u r n a l
S e p t e m b e r 2 0 0 4
Does good or even superb indoor environmental quality have
much impact on long-term sustainability? Will it impact a
project’s energy efficiency that much or reduce a building’s
negative environmental impact on its surroundings? (To be sure,
some IEQ-improving features, such as properly executed natu-
ral lighting and its interface with electric lighting, can save
considerable energy.)
Again, good indoor environmental quality should be sought
every time a design is done, just as should all the characteris-
tics of good design. The issue raised here is whether that char-
acteristic, along with some others cited as examples, is more a
good design characteristic than a green one.
And why would this matter? Because the possibility exists
that the attention given to such characteristics, in the name of
green design, may be distracting the design community from
concentrating on the characteristics that really do contribute to
sustainability—and from making some real progress in achiev-
ing it in the long term.
Measuring Success
More building developers and owners are interested in being
seen as green, and more design firms are engaged in producing
what are claimed to be green buildings. The trade and general
press have picked up on this trend and are publicizing these
projects. Many of these accounts cite, in addition to perhaps some
proven energy features, such characteristics as landscaped roofs,
daylighting, automatic shading devices, solar systems, wind gen-
erators, harvested rainwater, or recycled laundry water, etc.
Claims of energy savings are made (usually based on projec-
tions rather than actual historical data), and the capital costs of
the entire project or individual features are cited sometimes.
This all makes for good press. However, a key question for
engineers in particular is whether these green projects really
measure up, especially to the first four bullets of the green
definition cited previously: Do they really make a fair-share
contribution to what we define as sustainability?
This all points to a need for a database of hard information on
green building performance based on actual historical utility use
and cost, and on recorded experience. The author is aware of
several research projects in the planning stage where this kind
of data will be collected and assembled. However, except on a
whole-building basis, it may not be so easy to obtain.
For instance, do landscaped roofs really yield substantive
benefits (beyond providing a “warm, fuzzy feeling” to owners,
designers, and occupants)? Are energy savings that great? Com-
pared to what alternative? What about less obvious factors such
as added weight to the roof structure (especially after a sus-
tained and heavy rain)? What does maintenance entail over the
long term? What if the roof proper develops a leak and must be
accessed?
It may, in many cases, be hard to separate out the energy
savings of a particular green feature from a building’s docu-
mented total energy savings (even with metering techniques),
so there might have to be some reliance here, as a fallback, on
analysis techniques. On the other hand, added capital and main-
tenance costs, and water costs, easily could be tracked and sum-
marized.
In summary, a growing need exists for hard data. What is
needed first is what overall benefits or drawbacks (e.g., en-
ergy; capital cost; maintenance; rental impact, if applicable)
exist for green buildings in operation, based on actual and
verified historical data. Second, some specific data must be
collected or derived, by whatever practical means available,
for the more commonly used green design techniques or fea-
tures. This would enable green building design teams to fo-
cus on what really matters, based on experience.
David Grumman is editor of ASHRAE GreenGuide.
ASHRAE GREENGUIDE
Advertisement in the print edition formerly in this space.
S 4 8 B u i l d i n g f o r t h e Fu t u r e | A S u p p l e m e n t t o A S H R A E J o u r n a l
S e p t e m b e r 2 0 0 4
BUILDING FOR THE FUTURE
I
By Sheila J. Hayter, P.E., Member ASHRAE
t is no secret that making good design decisions early in the
design process ensures energy cost savings throughout the
life of a building. These decisions—such as selecting the best build-
ing orientation, construction materials, and schemes for provid-
ing space heating and cooling, as well as the right amount of glaz-
ing—balance requirements for comfortable levels of lighting, heat-
ing, cooling, and views while minimizing the amount of energy
needed to meet these requirements.
The strategies for maintaining this bal-
ance are not necessarily intuitive, however.
The building’s size, function, and location
are just a few of the many factors affect-
ing the right combination of design solu-
tions to minimize energy consumption and
operating costs and, at the same time, meet
occupants’ comfort needs.
These solutions are unique to every
building design. So, how can a design
team possibly consider the many options
without impacting the overall construc-
tion costs? The answer is threefold—a
collaborative design team, a flexible com-
puter model, and a systematic approach
to evaluating design strategies.
Collaborative design team. A frequent
comment among practicing ASHRAE
engineers is, “Even if I had the time and
budget to provide the kind of analysis
needed to identify aggressive energy-sav-
ing strategies for a project, I don’t have an
opportunity to provide this input early in
the design process.” The reality is that for
many projects, the design team members
are gradually brought on board as the
The design team must identify mea-
surable goals for the project early in
the design process. Design team
members included in the goal-devel-
opment process are more likely to em-
brace those goals throughout the de-
sign, construction, and commission-
ing phases. This goal-development ex-
ercise should include as many design
team members as possible. These are
some sample goals:
• Provide a design that will use 50%
less energy than an ANSI/ASHRAE/
IESNA Standard 90.1-compliant
building;
• Provide daylighting to offset 50%
or more of the electrical lighting loads
(this includes lighting controls);
• Obtain a LEED® Silver rating for
the building;
• Design an energy-efficient build-
ing that costs the same (or no more
than 5% more) than a conventional
building without energy-efficient fea-
tures; and
• Develop and follow commission-
ing and maintenance plans to ensure
efficient operations.
Identifying Goals
project follows a linear development path.
This path, familiar to all ASHRAE engi-
neers, begins when the building owner de-
fines program needs and goals with or
The following article was published in ASHRAE Journal, September 2004. © Copyright 2004 American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-
Conditioning Engineers, Inc. It is presented for educational purposes only. This article may not be copied and/or distributed electronically or in
paper form without permission of ASHRAE.
INTEGRATED DESIGN
S e p t e m b e r 2 0 0 4 B u i l d i n g f o r t h e Fu t u r e | A S u p p l e m e n t t o A S H R A E J o u r n a l
S49
without input from the architect, and the architect next suggests
building shell and interior layout options, after which the engi-
neer is invited to develop strategies for meeting occupant-com-
fort requirements, as well as other operational, mechanical, and
electrical requirements of the building.
In this conventional, linear process, the engineer is “invited”
to participate in later design phases. In most cases, the engi-
neer is selected concurrently with the architect, or shortly there-
after. A proactive engineer embraces the opportunity for early
input and will partner with the architect, starting with the en-
velope and building geometry to develop energy-efficient de-
sign strategies. Engineers who have not yet experienced early
participation in the design process are likely to be pleasantly
surprised by the architect’s eagerness for input, which will ul-
timately result in a better design.
Ideally, the design team can be assembled in its entirety be-
fore conceptual design discussions even begin. Members of
the design team include the obvious ones (e.g., the engineer
and architect), as well as representatives of all those others
(e.g., the energy consultant) who can impact the building de-
sign (Figure 1). This design team will assemble before begin-
ning the conceptual design phase to define program
requirements and set measurable design goals for the project.
Design team members will continue to interact throughout the
design, construction, and commissioning phases to ensure that
design goals are met.
Flexible computer model. A building’s envelope and me-
chanical systems maintain the desired interior space conditions
by resolving the constantly changing interactions between the
building’s internal loads and its exterior environment. Local utility
rate structures—which are likely to include some combination
of time-of-use and demand charges, ratchet clauses, and other
pricing structures—further complicate these interactions. Com-
puter modeling is one practical way to predict how a future build-
ing design and operation influence its energy costs.
Computer modeling, done early in the design process, pro-
vides a direction for selecting design strategies; modeling done
later in the process only verifies anticipated performance. Ac-
curate energy-code compliant, base-case computer models
developed during the conceptual design phase give the design
team typical energy and energy cost profiles for a building that
is similar in type, size, and location to the one they are about to
design. The design team uses this information to develop a
design concept to minimize these energy loads and energy costs
from the very outset.
At this early design stage, the design team can manipulate
the building’s massing, zoning, siting, orientation, internal or-
ganization, and external appearance without adding signifi-
cantly to the cost of the design. Later in the design process,
computer modeling results help the design team establish the
optimal combination of design solutions, predict the building’s
actual performance, verify energy code compliance, and iden-
tify performance problems after construction and commission-
ing are completed.
Systematic approach. A systematic approach to continu-
ously develop and refine computer models during every phase
of the design, construction, commissioning, and operation pro-
cesses is an effective method of ensuring that the building meets
established design goals.
Design Strategies
A computer modeling study of a two-story, 20,000-ft2, of-
fice/light-laboratory building in Los Alamos, New Mexico,
indicated that an energy savings of 25% to 30% could be
achieved without changing any of the building’s original func-
tional or programmatic requirements (LANL Sustainable
Design Guide). The energy-efficient building contained the
same number of offices and laboratory spaces as the origi-
nal design, and each had the same floor area. The type of
HVAC system also remained unchanged. Savings were
achieved through the following design strategies (Figure 3):
• Reconfiguring the building into a rectangle along an
east/west axis;
• Adjusting the size of the windows according to the
wall orientation;
• Adding exterior overhangs to south-facing windows;
• Specifying the optimal window solar heat gain coeffi-
cient;
• Reducing the ambient installed lighting density;
• Incorporating daylighting controls; and
• Adding clerestory windows to daylight second-floor
interior spaces.
These design changes would add little to nothing to the
building’s construction costs. For example, any increased
costs could be covered to some degree by the savings
obtained by being able to decrease the size of the HVAC
system because of the building’s energy efficiency.

S 5 0 B u i l d i n g f o r t h e Fu t u r e | A S u p p l e m e n t t o A S H R A E J o u r n a l
S e p t e m b e r 2 0 0 4
• Predesign—Model a base-case building to identify and pri-
oritize potential envelope-based energy-efficiency strategies.
• Schematic design—Add the building’s massing, fenestra-
tion, and envelope constructions to the model and investigate
design alternatives that best meet the energy targets.
• Design development—Test the performance of the full
building together with the HVAC systems and try system and
control sequence alternatives to optimize the design.
• Construction—Evaluate how design changes proposed
during construction will affect the building’s performance be-
fore implementing the change.
• Commissioning—Model the as-built construction to pro-
vide a baseline building performance that can be used in actual
performance comparisons.
• Post-occupancy—Periodically update the model after the
building is occupied to reflect variations in operations, use
patterns, and unique climate conditions. These conditions may
affect the actual performance of the building dramatically.
Updated models will provide guidance on how the building
should be performing, and they can be used to identify sources
of inefficient operation.
A parametric elimination study conducted during the
predesign phase indicates to the design team where major sav-
ings can occur. Eliminating loads individually from the base-
case building model shows which of those loads have the
greatest effect on the building’s total performance (Figure 2).
The members of the design team should focus their efforts on
reducing the loads having the greatest effect. For example, if
setting the window U-factor parameter extremely low results
in significant energy savings, then the design team should con-
sider strategies for reducing heat transfer through the windows.
Summary
We see that early analysis leads to energy and cost savings
without adverse impacts on the construction schedule and cost
(Figure 3). Decreases in heating, cooling, and lighting loads fre-
quently go hand-in-hand with smaller HVAC equipment capaci-
ties and more compact physical equipment. The HVAC system
components affected may include the heating plant, cooling plant,
air-handling units, distribution fans and motors, chilled and hot
water pumps and motors, and the smaller ducts, pipes, and wires
associated with this mechanical equipment. Cost savings gained
by specifying smaller (thus, less expensive) HVAC systems can
be used to offset the higher costs of the design strategies needed
to achieve a project’s energy-efficiency goals.
Bibliography
The ASHRAE GreenGuide and the soon-to-be-published Advanced
Energy Design Guide for Small Office Buildings provide good infor-
mation on creating a comprehensive design team, setting project de-
sign goals, using computer modeling tools to evaluate design options,
and developing strategies for meeting design goals. For information,
see ASHRAE’s Web site (www.ashrae.org).
Advanced Building Guidelines www.newbuildings.org/index.htm.
Federal Energy Management Program www.eere.energy.gov/femp.

and HVAC systems.” ASHRAE Journal 41(12):46–49.

energy.gov/buildings/highperformance/.

pdf/sbt.pdf.

Sheila J. Hayter, P.E., is a senior engineer with the National Re-
newable Energy Laboratory in Golden, Colo., and chairs the ASHRAE
Research Administration Committee.

S e p t e m b e r 2 0 0 4
BUILDING FOR THE FUTURE
his article demonstrates why designing a building with
stored cooling is a beneficial approach and how oversizing
the chiller plant for safety factor does not make sense. This
article discusses what makes thermal energy storage (TES) a
green technology, TES and safety factor, and benefits from in-
corporating storage.
LEED™ Rating System
One system for rating the “greenness”
of buildings is the U.S. Green Building
Council’s (USGBC) LEED rating system.
Based on this unit of measure, TES is
considered green. The ratings are based
on a point system (10 points are for en-
ergy savings).
LEED points are based on ANSI/
ASHRAE/IESNA Standard 90.1-1999,
Energy Standard for Buildings Except
Low-Rise Residential Buildings, which is
based on energy cost savings, not energy
savings. Cost is the only common de-
nominator for all the different energy-
efficient possibilities, as well as the
common metric that usually drives a
building owner’s decisions. To receive
LEED points, the building must surpass
Standard 90.1-1999 by more than a cer-
tain percentage for a certain amount of
points (20% = 2 points, 30% = 4 points
up to 60% = 10 points).
TES and LEED
The reason TES is a green technology
in the LEED system is that, in most loca-
tions, electricity at night costs less than
half as much as during the day.1 As dem-
onstrated in thousands of installations,
major energy cost savings are realized by
using inexpensive power at night to cre-
ate and store cooling, and using storage
to cool the building during the next day.
These savings provide LEED points,
which was demonstrated in California’s
first LEED 2.0 Gold building built by The
William and Flora Hewlett Foundation in
the City of Menlo Park.
The building had a total of 43 points
(out of 69), of which five were because of
the 35% energy cost reduction. This
project took advantage of four major cost/
energy-saving techniques including exter-
nal shading, natural lighting, natural ven-
tilation and off-peak cooling (OPC) using
ice-based thermal storage. Three of the
four are reducing the amount of mechani-
cal cooling, and the OPC system shifts
most of what mechanical cooling is re-
quired to the inexpensive off-peak period.
Real Reason Thermal Storage Is Green
Many studies, most notably one by the
California Energy Commission,2 have
demonstrated that, for many reasons, it
takes less fuel to make an off-peak kWh.
The main reasons are:
• Off-peak, base-load plants are much
more energy efficient than on-peak
plants, with 7,900 to 8,500 Btu/kW (8335
to 8970 kJ/kW) heat rates typical for
base-load plants. The existing stock of
“peaking” plants, which are comprised
mainly of simple cycle combustion tur-
bine units, are in the range of 9,000 to
12,000 Btu/kW (9495 to 12 660 kJ/kW).
T
By Mark MacCracken, P.E., Member ASHRAE
The following article was published in ASHRAE Journal, September 2004. © Copyright 2004 American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-
Conditioning Engineers, Inc. It is presented for educational purposes only. This article may not be copied and/or distributed electronically or in
paper form without permission of ASHRAE.
THERMAL ENERGY STORAGE
S e p t e m b e r 2 0 0 4 B u i l d i n g f o r t h e Fu t u r e | A S u p p l e m e n t t o A S H R A E J o u r n a l
S 3
• Line losses are less off-peak because that much less power
is transmitted at night.
• Spinning reserve requirements are lower. (Spinning reserve
essentially means power plants are forced to spin turbines at
night, without generating power. So, the plants are ready to
help meet the following day’s peak load). Therefore, lower on-
peak power requirements translate into less waste from spin-
ning reserves.
The results of the Califonia Energy Commission’s study
showed that for the two major California utilities, it required
10% to 30% less energy to create and deliver power off peak.
In addition to the reduction in emissions because of using less
fuel, the peaking power plants that are the last to come on-line
during a hot summer day normally are the dirtiest.
Another report done for California, “The Costs and Finan-
cial Benefits of Building Green,”3 states that the last power
plants to come on-line are “almost twice” as dirty as the base-
load plants. Obviously, the mix of power plants around the
country affects the exact numbers. However, better efficiency
and lower emissions combine for a big environmental advan-
tage from reducing on-peak loads and shifting to off-peak hours
by using storage.
Safety Factor Adds Value at No Extra Cost
At the 2003 USGBC’s GreenBuild Conference, I often
heard HVAC engineers being blamed for oversizing the me-
chanical systems and the negative impact that oversizing has
on a building’s performance.4 The safety factor is necessary
in this litigious world, especially when the engineer’s license
is at stake. However, the simple solution to this predicament
is cool storage.
An article I wrote for ASHRAE Journal 5 essentially con-
cluded with the statement that building owners would be bet-
ter served if the HVAC designer’s paradigm of adding 20% to
30% for safety be changed to decreasing the actual predicted
chiller plant size by 20% and adding storage for the safety fac-
tor at no additional cost. In that article, the building had a pre-
dicted 1,000 ton (3517 kW) peak load and the designer had
planned to install three 400 ton (1400 kW) chillers and related
equipment. The TES system used two 400 ton (1400 kW) ma-
chines and 3,500 ton·hr (12 300 kW·hr) of storage, which pro-
vides excess capacity if the load predictions were incorrect
(Figure 1).
Therefore, the storage system’s chiller plant capacity was
33% less than planned (including safety factor). However, it
was only 20% less than the actual design load (Figure 2).
What surprised one reader of the article is that those two sys-
tems could be constructed for the same cost. Using the costs
from the article of $900/installed ton ($256/installed kW) of
chiller plant and $100/installed ton·hr ($28/installed kW·hr) of
storage the statement is true, as long as each ton of chiller capac-
ity removed requires less than 9 ton·hrs (32 kW·hrs) of storage.
In the previous example, 3,500 ton·hrs (12 300 kW·hrs) of stor-
age is used to replace 400 tons (1400 kW) of chiller capacity or
8.75 ton·hrs/ton (kW·hrs/kW). (The normal range is between 5
and 9 ton·hrs/ton [kW·hrs/kW] based on load shape.)
For each ton a designer is comfortable reducing the installed
chiller plant capacity, storage can be purchased at no additional
cost. In doing so, it creates a system that has the flexibility to
capitalize on the diurnal swings in electric rates—greater value
with little or no extra cost.
Other System Benefits
The additional system benefits are many. Here are a few:
First, it seems many designers believe that absorption chill-
ers are green and inexpensive to operate because they avoid
electric demand charges. Absorption chillers are a good tech-
nology if the energy used to run them is waste heat from some
other process. However, if absorbers are direct fired with a
source fuel, it is apparent from Table 1 that the electric systems
...I often heard HVAC engineers being blamed for oversizing
the mechanical systems....
‘
’

Tons
are the clear choice.
For instance, a centrifugal chiller with a kW/ton of 0.58 (0.165
kW/kW) is equal to a COP of 6.0. If we use a grid electric energy
conversion efficiency of 33%, that equates into a source COP of
2.0. This compares to a COP of 1.0 for a single-effect absorber,
which means the absorber would use twice as much fuel.
In addition, using the electric centrifugal chiller as a baseline,
the cooling towers must be substantially larger and the amount
of site water use is about 70% more. Having an electric cen-
trifugal chiller make ice takes the performance to a COP of
1.66, which is still much better than any absorption chiller.
Therefore, storage gives the same demand avoidance that ab-
sorption chilling does at much higher energy efficiencies.
The other major benefit is the overall demand of the build-
ing and its effect on generation supply (whether from the grid
or on-site). This is most clearly shown by what it does for the
load factor of the building. Figures 3 and 4 show the load pro-
files of a building designed without storage. Figure 3 shows a
building design to meet Standard 90.1. Figure 4 shows a build-
ing with many energy-saving features (better windows, exter-
nal shading, variable speed pumps, etc.) and is predicted to use
20% less energy than Standard 90.1 requires. Even with all
these energy-saving features, the load factor (the average load
divided by the peak load) is about 53% for both designs.
When a full thermal storage system is added (Figure 5) the
load factor moves up to 88%, because the peak load of the
building has dropped from 1,500 kW to under 900 kW by shift-
ing the production of cooling to off-peak hours.
To a generation company, this means that with the same in-
vestment in a 300 MW generation plant, it could supply 300
customers that use full storage and supply only 200 customers
that do not. In the original example in Figure 2, which was
partial storage, we removed 375 kW from the maximum build-
ing demand by having the ice storage handle 500 tons (1760
kW) instead of using chillers. Lower electric demand keeps
energy costs down and minimizes the environmental impact of
cooling for everyone.
If a customer plans to generate its own power or have a backup
generation system, storage can translate into a major initial
cost reduction for a system. Table 2 shows approximate in-
stalled costs of chillers, storage and generation on a $/unit ba-
sis and for the hypothetical 1,000 tons (350 kW) building
project. The most important point is, even if the cost savings
from chiller plant reduction is disregarded, the capital cost of
storage is about equal to the capital savings in site generation
equipment it displaces.
Finally, since the example is limited to only purchasing a quan-
tity of storage that we could pay for by chiller capacity reduc-
tions, the next question becomes should more storage be
considered? The answer is, “yes.” It is based on excess chiller

THERMAL ENERGY STORAGE
S e p t e m b e r 2 0 0 4 B u i l d i n g f o r t h e Fu t u r e | A S u p p l e m e n t t o A S H R A E J o u r n a l
S 5
capacity we still have in the storage design. Eight-hundred nomi-
nal tons (2815 kW) of chillers produce about 550 tons (1930
kW) at ice-making conditions, so in just over six hours we could
charge the 3,500 ton·hr (12 300 kW·hr) of storage. If we assume
10 hours for charging, 5,500 ton·hr (19 300 kW·hr) could be
stored and the peak reduction from storage would be close to
500 kW compared to 350 kW. Therefore, using some of this
additional savings to purchase more storage might be prudent
since we have already installed more chiller than needed.
Conclusion
Storage is a natural method to balance the instantaneous needs
of any system to the average needs. When applied to our indus-
try, off-peak cooling is an efficient and affordable tool that saves
building owners money, as well as saves energy and reduces emis-
sions. Thermal storage should be considered by designers as a
more natural and cost-effective method of adding safety factor,
and not just used in areas where electric rates may dictate. Of-
fices, schools, hospitals, places of worship, arenas and any other
similar building with cyclic loads are possible candidates. With
all these advantages it is clearly a technology that lowers the im-
pact on the environment, which is what green is about.

BUILDING FOR THE FUTURE
By Erik L. Olsen, Associate Member ASHRAE
he Chicago Public Schools (CPS) has begun a major initiative
to maximize the sustainable building potential in its more
than 600 schools. As a first step, it seeks the U.S. Green Building
Council’s LEED®1 certification on new buildings. The design of these
new schools has been approached systematically, involving all as-
pects of design, demolition, construction, and building maintenance.
Although the LEED® rating system
provides one framework for consider-
ation of sustainability, it does not fully
capture the unique challenges faced by a
large, urban school district. CPS serves
more than 430,000 students in hundreds
of schools spread over a city covering
more than 200 square miles (518 km2).
To meet these needs, several major re-
quirements have long been a part of CPS
Capital Improvement Plan objectives:
• Longevity of systems and ease of
maintenance;
• Lower energy consumption;
• Healthy building environment with
daylighting and effective ventilation;
• Optimal educational opportunities; and
• Minimize life-cycle costs.
These standards are parallel with the
goals of sustainable construction, and es-
sentially provide the CPS definition of
sustainability. However, the relative weight
with which they are considered may be dif-
ferent than in other building types.
For example, CPS strongly emphasizes
simple mechanical systems requiring
little maintenance. Although a more com-
plex system may reduce energy con-
sumption, if it requires significantly more
maintenance, it is not practical for CPS
to operate the system properly over time.
Hence, it is not a sustainable solution for
the schools. However, there may be al-
ternative systems that also provide energy
savings without increased maintenance.
A major element of the sustainability
initiative is the development of a new me-
chanical system. A standard system type
has long been used in new CPS build-
ings to streamline design, construction,
and maintenance. Only partial satisfac-
T

S13

’
tion with the current system and the renewed emphasis on en-
ergy efficiency and indoor environmental quality provided the
impetus for a reevaluation of this system, with the goal of imple-
menting a new system that meets all of the objectives listed
earlier. In addition, the systems in all new schools will be fully
commissioned, and a single, standard temperature control pro-
tocol for all schools has been selected.
Current System
The current standard mechanical system has been applied in
all new CPS schools for more than 10 years. Overall, the system
is fairly conventional and similar to systems at many other schools.
Some higher-quality features, such as indoor air-handling units,
are used to meet the district’s demands for durability.
The air-handling system consists of variable volume central
station indoor air-handling units with a mixing box, filter sec-
tion, hot water heating coil, and chilled water cooling coils.
Air-handling units serving single large zones, such as gymna-
siums or auditoriums, are constant volume.
Variable volume air handlers supply 55°F (13°C) air to series
fan-powered terminal units with hot water reheat. The fan-pow-
ered box reheat coil provides all perimeter heat. Interior zones
are served by VAV terminal units with hot water reheat. All air is
delivered to spaces using conventional overhead diffusers.
The hot water system consists of constant-flow primary, vari-
able-flow secondary pumping with 180°F (82°C) design supply
temperature, 160°F (71°C) design return temperature. Heat is
provided by forced-draft water tube boilers sized for two-thirds
of the design heating load for redundancy. The actual supply
water temperature is reset based on outdoor temperature.
Chilled water is provided at 44°F (7°C) by outdoor air-cooled
chillers. Design return temperature is 56°F (13°C). For elemen-
tary schools, a single chiller generally is used, combined with
constant-flow, primary-only pumping. For high schools, mul-
tiple chillers are used, with constant-flow primary, variable-
flow secondary pumping.
Evaluation
A comprehensive study was performed to evaluate several
possible changes to the current system, and using these find-
ings, recommend a new system for new CPS buildings. The
first part of this study was identifying appropriate options for
evaluation. More than 30 total options were developed and
explained to CPS staff, which worked with the authors to re-
duce this list to 15 options warranting further study.
The cases evaluated include various heating and cooling plant
options, primarily consisting of different boiler and chiller types.
Additions to the control system, such as demand-controlled ven-
