in the occupied area). Return air duct detectors
can be used in the kitchen and serving areas of
the restaurant because an area smoke detector
is too sensitive to cooking smoke.
When the ﬁ re alarm system is activated from
any smoke detector, the dining room supply air
system stops. This prevents smoke dispersion
that slows the clearing of the smoke. The alarm
signal opens the gravity inlet path and starts the smoke control
system. The gravity inlet path is the replacement air system for
the smoke exhaust system. The replacement air is introduced
from low sidewall grilles into the exhausted spaces at velocities
not exceeding 200 fpm (1 m/s).
Sprinkler Head Activation
The sprinkler heads are installed to comply with NFPA 135
for Ordinary Group 2 occupancy (0.20 gpm/ft2 [0.14 L/s per
m2] over the most remote 1,500 ft2 [140 m2] area). Restaurant
seating areas are Light Hazard occupancy, and the restau-
rant service areas are Ordinary Group 1 Hazard occupancy.
However, the covered mall is considered the most demanding
hazard classiﬁ cation, which is why Ordinary Group 2 Hazard
is used. Water ﬂ ow in the zoned sprinkler system will activate
the smoke control system.
Kitchen Hood Exhaust System
The kitchen hood system with its replacement air system
operates independently of the smoke control system. However,
when an alarm is in the dining area, the kitchen hood replace-
ment air system will be turned off, as will the other supply air
units. If the hood ﬁ re alarm is activated, the dining area oper-
ates in the normal mode. The hood ﬁ re alarm trips the hood
suppression system, maintains the exhaust ﬂ ow, and stops the
replacement air system to the hood.
Special Inspection
The designed smoke control system requires special in-
spection to review the design of the system, to inspect the
construction, and to test its operation after ﬁ nal construction
(IBC 909.3 & 1704.14). The role of the special inspector be-
gins with a contract between the inspector and the owner. The
special inspector must be qualiﬁ ed in the area of ﬁ re protection
engineering, mechanical engineering and air balancing. The
special inspector also must be acceptable to the authority hav-
ing jurisdiction because the inspector’s test approval is part of
the ﬁ nal certiﬁ cation process.
The duties of the special inspector are to review the elements
of the system design prepared by the restaurant’s design team.
The design elements are:
• Exhaust airﬂ ow required;
• Intake path, gravity or a fan-delivered system;
• Activation devices, including type and location;
• Communication interface with the mall security system,
which alerts the security personnel to call the ﬁ re department;
• Temperature rating of the exhaust fan equipment; and
• Requirement for the ﬁ re alarm wiring to either in conduit
or in plenum-rated wire.
Pretesting
The acceptance testing often is close to the day scheduled for
sign-off for the certiﬁ cate of occupancy. Therefore, pretesting of
the critical coordination parts of the entire system is important.
The following is a list of critical pretesting tasks.
• The ductwork for the smoke control system must be leak
tested to 1.5 times the designed pressure. Test early in the con-
struction before the air inlet or outlets are installed.
• The smoke control system must be supplied and tested with
two sources of power. The primary power source is the normal
building power, and the secondary power is from an approved
standby power source.
Advertisement formerly in this space.
December 2004
ASHRAE Journal
21
• The fan systems must be tested and balanced to their design
airﬂ ows.
• Fan and damper modulation should be tested to allow
for full operation before the space conditions exceed design
smoke conditions.
Acceptance Testing
The smoke control system modes of operation should be
described by means of an event/action matrix as shown in Table
2. The ﬁ re alarm smoke detectors can be activated manually to
verify activation of the smoke control system. The manual trip
mode is either by means of a magnet or a puffer can of smoke.
The sprinkler ﬂ ow switches can be tripped manually to simulate a
sprinkler discharge, which activates the smoke control system.
Communication links to mall security should be veriﬁ ed as
to alarm condition and alarm zone. The type of ﬁ nal test (in
this example), as negotiated with the local code authority, was
by means of burning wet cardboard in a galvanized trash can
to simulate a smoking ﬁ re event. An alternate method is to re-
lease chemical smoke above an electric or propane heater. The
hot rising smoke plume should activate the dining area smoke
detectors. If all goes well, the smoke control system activates,
and the smoke will be cleared within a short time.
Typical Problems
This type of a project is based on mixed occupancies and
the various subcontractors may not be aware of the total smoke
control system design intent. The special inspector should
inform the entire construction team of their roles in the ﬁ nal
smoke control operations system.
The interface between the kitchen exhaust hood system
and the smoke control system should be well deﬁ ned by the
event/action matrix, Table 2. Coordination is required between
the smoke detection system and the sprinkler system because
the zone coverage must be the same.
During review of the equipment shop drawings, it must
be veriﬁ ed that the temperature rating of the smoke control
equipment is appropriate. Also, verify that the smoke control
exhaust fan contains a minimum of two drive belts or 1.5 times
the number of belts required for the design duty.
Verify during pretesting that the HVAC supply devices are
blowing smoke away from the ceiling smoke detectors. The
detectors may have to be relocated.
Summary
A smoke control system for a mall restaurant requires an
experienced engineer to design a typical restaurant HVAC
system, kitchen exhaust system, and a retail mall-type smoke
removal system. All three systems have design intents that
must be integrated.
The trade contractors that install the HVAC, ﬁ re alarm, secu-
rity and sprinkler system may not be accustomed to this degree
of integration. On-site meetings are required to inform all the
contractors about the integrated design requirements.
The local code authorities also may not be aware of the inter-
relationships of the various systems, which is why the special
inspector is required. The codes1 recognize this complex system
and require special inspection and testing. The designer, the
client, the contractors and the code authorities must recognize
the special inspector as a qualiﬁ ed expert.
The systems are complex and require pretesting because the
ﬁ nal acceptance test always closely precedes the signing of the
certiﬁ cate of occupancy, which precedes opening for business.
Good communication between the parties involved and atten-
tion to details is important in this nonstandard project.
References
1. Uniform Building Code-2000, Sections 402 and 905 — by Inter-

C
oncern has escalated over the past decade about the potential
use of weapons of mass destruction either by a nation-state or
terrorists.1 Indeed, concern about the possibility that Iraq had weapons
of mass destruction was the primary attributed cause for the recent
invasion of that country.
About the Author
D.A. Henderson, M.D., is resident scholar at the
Center for Biosecurity in Baltimore of the Univer-
sity of Pittsburgh Medical Center and professor of
medicine and public health.
The Threat of Aerosolized
Biological Weapons
and reported that not only had he been
working on an antibiotic-resistant plague
organism, but that he knew of laboratories
other than his own that were engaged in
biological weapons research.
The fact that a larger program existed
was conﬁ rmed in 1992 by Dr. Ken Alibek,
then ﬁ rst deputy chief of research and
production for the Soviet biological weap-
ons program, who defected and brought
detailed information about a massive pro-
gram then in progress in the Soviet Union.3
As he reported, the Soviet Union, in 1938,
had embarked on a major expansion of its
biological weapons coincident with sign-
ing of the Biological Weapons Convention.
That program grew to involve more than
60,000 persons in 50 different laboratories.
Many of the Soviet Union’s most capable
biologists were engaged.
In 1980, soon after the World Health
Organization announced that smallpox
had been eradicated, the Soviet Union
began an intensiﬁ ed effort to produce
smallpox virus in large quantities for
potential use in intercontinental ballistic
missiles. Eventually, a manufacturing
facility was built that was capable of
producing in excess of 20 tons (18 Mg)
By D.A. Henderson, M.D.
Among weapons of mass destruction,
biological weapons have a preeminent
place because of their potential to cause
casualties and panic at least equivalent
to what might be anticipated following
detonation of a nuclear weapon. However,
biological weapons are of special concern
because they are far easier to produce, to
transport and to disseminate and, thus,
more likely to be used.
Although chemical weapons cannot be
ignored, very large quantities are required
to inﬂ ict substantial numbers of casual-
ties, and dissemination poses a more
difﬁ cult problem. Biological weapons
sometimes are referred to as the poor
man’s nuclear weapon because they can
be produced inexpensively, they require
less technical staff and so little space or
expensive equipment is needed.
The threat of biological weapons large-
ly was ignored until recently. In 1973, a
biological and toxin weapons convention
had been endorsed by virtually all coun-
tries.2 Countries that signed the treaty
agreed to cease research on offensive
biological weapons and to destroy such
stocks as they had. Until the 1990s, it was
believed that all had complied but, as no
provision for veriﬁ cation had been made,
conﬁ rmation was not possible.
Biological Weapons Threat
Complacency about the threat of
biological weapons first wavered in
1989 when a Soviet scientist defected

and Environmental Security under Extraordinary Incidents,” at www.ashrae.org/homelandsecurity.

December 2004
ASHRAE Journal
51
of smallpox virus in a year. The laboratory was a secret facility;
the building is still intact and it is still a secret facility.
Following the breakup of the Soviet Union and the subsequent
Russian economic crisis, personnel in the bioweapons laborato-
ries decreased greatly. Many of those formerly engaged departed
for other countries, including the United States. Some went for
extended periods of time to countries such as North Korea, Syria,
Iran and Iraq to consult and work in laboratories. Whether or not
they carried samples of the pathogenic organisms is unknown
but, certainly, they carried with them what were then the most
advanced knowledge and skills regarding production and use of
the most serious pathogenic agents, such as smallpox, anthrax,
g
,
p
,
,
plague and Ebola virus infection.
Biological Agents Prospects
Many have argued that it would be too difﬁ cult to
obtain cultures of the principal pathogenic or-
ganisms; that they would be too difﬁ cult for all
but the most sophisticated scientists to grow; and
that dissemination would be a problem. None
of these arguments are valid.4
Except for smallpox, all of the
potential agents occur naturally and
infect humans. Thus, many diagnos-
tic laboratories have, and regularly
receive, samples of the organisms.
They are reasonably readily avail-
able. Smallpox virus is known to
be present in two, or perhaps three,
different laboratories in Russia,
and at the U.S. Centers for Disease
Control. Specimens at the CDC
and in one of the Russian labo-
ratories are kept under secure
conditions; little is known about the security of specimens in
other Russian laboratories.
Techniques for growing and genetically manipulating micro-
organisms of all types are taught in schools across the world; the
Internet also provides a great deal of information. In fact, through
2001, explicit instructions were online as to how one could produce
a weaponized form of one of the agents of principal concern.
Based on exercises to test the difﬁ culties of production, it has
been estimated that a competent laboratory could be developed for
no more than $250,000, using equipment that was readily avail-
able and requiring only a handful of staff. It is suspected as well
that material could be obtained for a price from some laboratories
in countries where living conditions are marginal and laboratory
security is slack. Finally, creating a ﬁ ne particle aerosol, the most
effective means for dispersing an agent, has become a technology
that is widely available and widely applied. Paint sprayers, asthma
inhalant devices, dusters to apply insecticides to roses, perfume
atomizers, and insecticide fogging machines are all examples of
technological solutions for aerosolizing liquids and powders.
Given that someone in the United States has already succeeded
in dispersing an anthrax powder that infected some 22 persons,
and presumably has more or could obtain more, has suggested to
most that it is not a question of whether another attack will occur
but only a question of when this will happen.
The U.S. Commission on National Security in the 21st Centu-
ry reﬂ ected this concern in their report of September 1999:5
“While conventional conﬂ icts will still be possible, the most
serious threat to our security may consist of unannounced at-
tacks on American cities by subnational groups using genetically
y
engineered pathogens.”
Agents of Special Concern
concern are termed Class A
The ﬁ ve organisms of greatest
tic group of diseases.
agents and include a diagnost

would be
uld be able to drift for miles,
and it wou

of thousands of individuals.

Not un
d it become apparent that
woul
tack had occurred. The two
an att
agents most greatly feared are
t
smallpox and anthrax. The others are plague, tularemia (rabbit
fever), botulinum toxin and the hemorrhagic fevers such as the
African Ebola, Marburg and Lassa fever viruses.
Education of health-care personnel and public health prepared-
ness initiatives focus on these agents in particular. At the time
of the attack on the World Trade Center, smallpox was a special
concern. It is a viral disease that spreads from person to person
and results in a case-fatality rate of 30%.7,8 No therapy is available.
Isolation of patients and vaccination are the only two weapons
available to deal with the disease.
However, vaccination in the U.S. stopped in 1972, and in the
world in 1980, when it was conﬁ rmed that smallpox had been
eradicated and was then conﬁ ned to research laboratories. Con-
tinuing vaccine use was terminated because of the frequency of
serious reactions to the vaccine; vaccine reactions that were toler-
able when smallpox posed an immediate threat were intolerable
when that threat was removed.
52
ASHRAE Journal ashrae.org
December 2004
As of 2001, it was estimated that 75%
of the population was unprotected against
smallpox; there were then no vaccine
production laboratories anywhere in
the world; the only vaccine available
consisted of limited stocks that had been
produced 25 or more years before and
kept in deep freezers.
Emergency measures were promptly
taken to speed the production of large sup-
cases should they occur and for rapid vacci-
nation. The release of smallpox virus today
would cause considerable devastation, but
control should be rapidly achieved. Indeed,
the entire population could be vaccinated.
Anthrax presents a quite different but
no less serious problem.9 It is caused by a
bacillus and is responsible for many deaths
every year among animals, particularly
ruminants. It exists as hardy spores in the
earth that can remain dormant for many de-
cades before being ingested by animals.
The spores germinate and grow rapidly,
causing severe infection and death. In hu-
mans, anthrax infections are uncommon;
they usually involve only the skin and can
readily be treated. However, if the spores
are inhaled, such as occurred in the 2001
outbreak which involved postal workers
and others, the individuals may experience
a severe, rapidly progressive infection af-
fecting tissues in the pulmonary cavity.
Death may occur in 40% or more of
those infected, many succumbing within
a matter of a few days unless antibiotics
and supportive respiratory therapy are
promptly provided.
Fortunately, anthrax cannot be transmit-
ted from person to person. However, the
organism is reasonably easy to obtain; it
can be grown with little specialized equip-
ment; and can infect persons even if only
small amounts are inhaled. In a powdered
form, it is extremely stable and can readily
be transferred from place to place. Anthrax,
with smallpox, was a preferred agent of
the Soviet Biological Weapons Program,
and is known to have been produced as
a weapon by the Japanese religious cult,
Aum Shinrikyo, as well as Iraq.
Dealing with anthrax poses more dif-
ﬁ cult problems than does smallpox. Only
limited supplies of a vaccine are available
and, for prevention, six inoculations are
necessary. Protection is not long-lasting. A
vaccine that provides more durable protec-
tion with fewer inoculations is well along
in development but still several years away
from being able to be distributed widely.
At present, the strategy for dealing with
an anthrax outbreak is primarily that of
responding as rapidly as possible with
antibiotics and vaccine as soon as an
plies of vaccine; to encourage and support
efforts of hospitals and health departments
to be prepared for the rapid diagnosis of
cases both clinically and in the labora-
tory; and for the isolation of cases and the
vaccination of contacts using the limited
amounts of vaccine available.
Today, there is ample vaccine to be used
if necessary and communities are making
good progress in planning for smallpox
Advertisement formerly in this space.
December 2004
ASHRAE Journal
53
attack has been detected. The incubation
period of anthrax may range from two
to 46 or more days and, thus, as soon as
anthrax is diagnosed, all believed exposed
would be given treatment. This consists of
three doses of vaccine given at two week
intervals and antibiotics to be taken daily
for 40 days if the individual is being vac-
cinated, or 60 days if not.
The logistics of implementing such a
program are formidable, especially if tens
or hundreds of thousands are considered to
be at risk. A further problem is that many
individuals given antibiotics for any period
longer than seven to 10 days begin to expe-
rience adverse reactions to the drug, severe
enough to preclude their continuing it.
Better weapons for dealing with anthrax
are clearly a high priority. A better vaccine;
improved antibiotics; better ways to diag-
nose cases at an earlier time are all under
study. An additional approach—improved
air-ﬁ ltration systems—that are effective
and affordable now are being explored.
Filters rated MERV 13 or 14 ﬁ lter out more
than 90% of all particles larger than one mi-
cron in size.10 The anthrax bacillus is such
a particle. Were it possible to install ﬁ lters
with such efﬁ ciency, most of the anthrax
particles, whether brought in from outside
the building or released in the interior of a
building, could be rapidly removed.
Filtration alone would not totally pro-
tect against the possibility of infection
following the release of an aerosol but
it could markedly reduce the number
of particles being inhaled. This would
be important because the probability of
becoming sick, as well as that of a fatal
outcome, depends on how many spores
are inhaled. Animal studies show that
between 4,000 and 10,000 spores must be
inhaled to infect 50% of those exposed.
Conclusions
The threat of biological weapons being
used is real and inevitably will be with
us for a very long time, perhaps forever.
The threat is especially ominous because
intelligence sources have had great dif-
ﬁ culty in obtaining reliable information
regarding the possible production or
potential use of these weapons.
Efforts are being made to obtain an
earlier warning of their use through
air sensing devices but, for many
reasons, both technological and opera-
tional, it is unlikely that these can or will
prove practical.
Vaccines, better antibiotics and provision
for large-scale treatment of casualties are
receiving attention and research support.
Ideally, one would wish to prevent an attack
but few tools are either available or likely to
become available in the near term.
However, a most promising potential
addition to our armamentarium is air
ﬁ ltration, providing a protection against
anthrax or other bacterial weapons attack
as well as a cleaner atmosphere with few-
er allergens and less dust. This approach
should receive high priority now.

Warfare. Washington: Ofﬁ ce of the Surgeon
General 415–423.

a company’s information technology (IT)
department and software vendors have
embraced XML.
XML uses tags (words surrounded
in angle brackets such as ) to
identify elements. At first glance the
angle brackets make an XML document
seem similar to an HTML (hypertext
markup language) or Web page docu-
ment. However, HTML documents and
XML documents are different and serve
different purposes.
About the Author
Alex Chervet is product marketing manager at
Echelon Corp. in San Jose, Calif.
ated on an Apple Macintosh can be sent to
a computer running Microsoft Windows
or Linux, or to a mainframe, or Unix
server or embedded computing platform
such as a BAS controller. This transport-
ability makes it easy to understand why
XML and
Building
Automation
What is XML?
XML (extensible markup language)
is a text ﬁ le arranged in a speciﬁ c for-
mat, which is easily transported among
computer systems and across operating
systems. For example, an XML ﬁ le cre-
U
sing XML in building automation systems (BAS) has become a
hot topic—but do the results live up to the hype? This article
explains the fundamentals of XML in nontechnical terms and provides
tools to understand how XML related to building automation protocols
such as LonMark® and BACnet®.
By Alex Chervet
The following article was published in ASHRAE Journal, December 2004. © Copyright 2004 American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and
Air-Conditioning Engineers, Inc. It is presented for educational purposes only. This article may not be copied and/or distributed electronically
or in paper form without permission of ASHRAE.
December 2004
ASHRAE Journal
25
HTML tells a Web browser how to render or display a
document. XML describes the data contained in a document.
Consider the following e-mail message.
To: husband@hotmail.com
From: wife@hotmail.com
CC:
Subject: Milk
It’s Friday. Don’t forget to pick up milk
for the weekend.
Coded as HTML this e-mail message might look like this:
Email

**To:**husband@hotmail.com  
**From:** wife@hotmail.com  
**CC:**  
**Subject:**Milk  
  
It’s Friday. Don’t forget to pick up
milk for the weekend.
  
The effect of this coding causes the document to be rendered
in a Web browser with the ﬁ eld labels in bold type.
To: husband@hotmail.com
From: wife@hotmail.com
CC:
Subject: Milk
It’s Friday. Don’t forget to pick up milk for the
weekend.
HTML tags deﬁ ne how to display information and, in this
case, where to use the bold attribute, and where to insert line
breaks.
Coded as XML, the message might look
like:
Email

husband@echelon.com
wife@hotmail.com

Milk
It’s Friday. Don’t forget to pick
up milk for the weekend.
Notice that the XML tags no longer deﬁ ne a rendering
attribute such as bold or underline. Instead, the tags tell us
something about the text (data) that appears between the tag
pair. The tag  tells us to whom the email should be
sent. The tag  tells us what the message
will be about, and the tag  gives us the content
of the message.
What if the text had been in French:
À: husband@hotmail.com De: wife@hotmail.
com
copie:
Objet: Lait
C’est Vendredi. N’oublient pas d’apportez
du lait pour le week-end
Most people, even non-French speaking people, would guess
from the e-mail addresses that this text is, in fact, an e-mail, but
they might not know the sender or recipient, and they might
think that the subject line was left blank instead of the Cc ﬁ eld.
There is the possibility of error. However, coded as XML, all
possibility of error is removed.
Email

husband@hotmail.com
wife@hotmail.com

Lait
C’est Vendredi. N’oublient pas
d’apportez du lait pour le week-end
26
ASHRAE Journal ashrae.org
December 2004
By examining the XML tags, the sender, recipient, subject
and body of the message are obvious — even if you don’t read
French.
The XML tags describe the data but tell us nothing about
how to display the data. How the data is displayed in a Web
browser or a word processor is not described in the XML ﬁ le.
The XML only describes the data.
Thus, XML separates data from presentation. This turns out
to be extremely useful as we’ll see later.
Converting XML
Because an XML ﬁ le contains only data, it can be used as
the source for a ﬁ le, a chart, a Web page, a document, or a hu-
man/machine interface (HMI). In the IT industry, this is often
called repurposing an XML document. Repurposing the XML
ﬁ le into the speciﬁ c format you want is usually done by applying
an extensible style sheet (XSL) transform.1
A transform is something that can be applied against some
XML to translate it into many types of ﬁ les that can be used in
many different software programs. Using a transform, any piece
of XML can be translated into any other text format — including
more XML. Let’s look at a real-world example.
The following XML was retrieved from a building automation
supervisor/gateway widely used with ANSI/EIA709.1-based
device networks. This snippet represents a portion of a data
log (some modiﬁ cations have been made to remove product
references):

ASHRAE Journal
27
TION
By applying the following simple XSL transform, the above
XML converts easily to CSV (a comma-delimited ﬁ le that can
be opened in Microsoft Excel).
Transform

,
,
,
  
Resulting CSV
pml1964, 2002/10/30, 01:15:00, 123.6
pml1964, 2002/10/30, 01:30:00, 124.8
pml1964, 2002/10/30, 01:45:00, 122.4
pml1964, 2002/10/30, 02:00:00, 122.4
pml1964, 2002/10/30, 02:15:00, 120
For each XML element, the transform did the following:
• Selected only the subset of elements we were interested in
(location, date, time, value);
• Inserted commas between values to create the CSV format;
• Converted the date from Uniform Time Code format (UTC)
to yyyy/mm/dd format;
• Converted the time from UTC to hh:mm:ss format;
• Removed extraneous data such as alarm status, engineering
units, source address and point name;
Quick Review
• XML is nothing more than structured text;
• XML is NOT a form of HTML;
• XML describes the data, not how the data eventually is
presented.
· Conversely, HTML describes how data is presented but
does not describe the data itself;
• Transforms convert XML into any other format; and
• Because XML is “just text” it:
· Cannot be owned or controlled by any single company
· Works with any computer
· Is human readable making it easy to debug
· Is optimized to travel over the Web
· Is applicable to any type of data.
HVAC Applications
The protocol wars of the last decade have left many in the
industry confused about the best way to connect systems to-
gether. Over the course of the next decade, the confusion may
increase as HVAC and other building control systems connect
to IT systems.
The reality is that XML is an opportunity for HVAC
professionals and their companies. The beneﬁ ts of using
XML to connect HVAC or building automation systems
is potentially huge, and will beneﬁ t companies seeking to
increase efﬁ ciency, becoming more competitive and offering
expanded services.
Consider the example of a building owner or tenant that
wants to reconcile actual energy used in a building with a
utility bill.
The utility bill is entered into the company’s operational
systems via electronic transfer from a corporate IT application
XML Interface
Field Bus
Field Bus
Ethernet
Figure 1: XML is a perfect ﬁ t at the gateway/supervisory level.
28
ASHRAE Journal ashrae.org
December 2004
or the utility itself. Now, the IT manager needs information
from the BAS. Likely, he would prefer an XML ﬁ le rather
than a LonMark or BACnet object, because IT professionals
are already familiar with XML.
The IT department’s preference for XML and the incredible
marketing push from Microsoft, IBM, Sun, ORACLE, and
others is not lost on manufacturers of building automation
equipment. Major vendors, as well as their suppliers, have
announced products or plans for products based on XML.
Okay, so we have this new data format optimized to travel
over the World Wide Web infrastructure. Does this mean my
occupancy sensor is going to send XML messages to my
lighting controller? Probably not.
Remember, XML is a data format — not a protocol. Even if
the occupancy sensor did provide information in XML format,
the problem of moving the text from the occupancy sensor to
the lighting controller still exists.
Protocols such as BACnet (ANSI/ASHRAE Standard 135-
2004) and LonTalk (ANSI/EIA709.1) have done an excellent
job solving this problem at the device level. For instance, an
ANSI/EIA709.1 packet is optimized to move from device to
device over a simple twisted pair using as little as 9 bytes.
In those 9 bytes are contained source and destination ad-
dress, engineering units, the data value itself and a cyclic
redundancy check (CRC). All of this is implemented in a $4
microcontroller that also handles the device electronics — and
the solution scales to 32385 224 devices. This solution also
allows for low-cost unshielded two-wire twisted pair wiring
further reducing total installation cost.
To do the same thing using XML messaging would require
a larger processor in the occupancy sensor because process-
ing XML is more intensive than processing control pack-
ets. Extra memory is required to host the XML parser and
XML messages, which, being text based, are inherently much
larger. Although it may be possible to implement such a
system over RS-485 or ANSI/EIA709.2 free topology physi-
cal layers, the number of messages per second that could be
sent is greatly reduced. A complete control system based on
device-to-device XML messaging using today’s technology
would simply be too expensive.
XML and BACnet
®
By H. Michael Newman,
Chairman, SSPC 135 XML-WG
The use of XML also has been a hot topic within
ASHRAE. Guideline Project Committee 20, XML for
HVAC&R has been working on ways to establish a com-
mon data exchange format for describing the characteristics
of HVAC&R equipment and controls along with building
performance data using XML. Standing Standards Project
Committee 135 (SSPC 135), the BACnet committee, has
been developing a set of Web services that will enable access
to building automation and control system data via the World
Wide Web. Addendum c to ANSI/ASHRAE Standard 135-
2004, BACnet, recently was in a 45-day public review.
The purpose of Addendum c is to provide a means to in-
tegrate building automation and control systems with other
enterprise computing applications. Web services provide for
computer-to-computer applications many of the same advan-
tages that the World Wide Web provides for human-to-comput-
er information access. Potential uses of the technology include
simplifying access to building energy and performance data
for inclusion in spreadsheets and other management reports;
accessing equipment run-time data for use by maintenance
management systems; allowing tenant access to, and control
of, space temperature setpoints; coupling of room scheduling
with ventilation and comfort control; and many more.
The new addendum is in two parts. The ﬁ rst proposes an
Annex N to BACnet that deﬁ nes the BACnet Web services
interface, BACnet/WS. This interface is intended to be
“protocol neutral” in that the deﬁ ned web services can be
used with any underlying protocol including BACnet, Kon-
nex, MODBUS, LON or legacy proprietary protocols.
This has been accomplished by deﬁ ning an application
program interface (API) to read and write the common ele-
ments of all building automation and control systems such
as values, schedules, trend logs, and alarm information
using services such as ‘getValue’ and ‘setValue’ that use
a simple path such as “/Company HQ/Conference Room
A/Space Temperature” to deﬁ ne the intended data source.
The proposed standard also provides powerful mechanisms
for localization where certain types of data such as time,
date and numbers can be formatted according to local
custom and language. Text names and descriptions may
also be accessed according to the local language. Several
manufacturers will display Annex N gateway products at
the 2005 AHR Expo, February 7–9.
The second part of the addendum contains an addition
to BACnet’s Annex H, Combining BACnet Networks with
Non-BACnet Networks, which prescribes the gateway map-
ping speciﬁ cally to and from BACnet messages.
The combined effect of the BACnet/WS annexes is to
provide a set of generic Web services that can potentially
interface to any building automation protocol, as well as
to describe exactly how this interface would work with
underlying BACnet systems.
December 2004
ASHRAE Journal
29
To keep costs low and to guarantee device-level interoper-
ability, vendors likely will keep the existing device networking
protocols that have been optimized for control at the device
level, but XML is a “perfect ﬁ t” at the gateway/supervisory level
where high-speed Ethernet connections and 32-bit processors
are becoming the norm.
Integration Not Interoperability
BACnet and LonMark are accepted worldwide as the two
protocols for providing interoperability within building control
systems. Will XML ﬁ nally resolve the “war” between LonMark
and BACnet systems or will it be a third protocol?
Most likely, the use of XML and new XML-based standards
will not end these differences. Expect that suppliers will con-
tinue to use LonMark, BACnet, and other open and proprietary
technologies for systems that provide speciﬁ c functions in
buildings. Examples of this would include HVAC control sys-
tems that use LonMark or BACnet, lighting control systems
based on Modbus, LonMark, or DALI, or ﬁ re alarm systems
and security systems that are based on proprietary protocols.
I expect that the protocol used to tie these together could be
based on XML and that when information from these systems
needs to be shared with business or enterprise systems that
XML will also be used.
In short, XML does not replace or compete with other pro-
tocols, rather it provides a bridge that ties them together and
provides a ready point for interaction with business systems.
But, XML also has much broader uses in HVAC than only
for controls. It can be used for building performance data,
modeling, equipment catalog information, electronic com-
merce, etc.
Conclusion
Today we expect access to systems using only a Web browser.
The days of installing a custom application for every system
we need to access are all but gone. Tomorrow, I will expect all
data to be stored as XML. The days of proprietary formats in
the IT industry are fading. The building industry, often con-
sidered slow to adopt new IT standards, will adopt XML even
more quickly than it adopted Web browsers. New XML-based
systems will be driven by customer demand and ubiquitous
access to rapid application development (RAD) tools.
Remember, existing automation systems have value that
XML-based systems simply do not address. Clearly, the correct
use of XML is as an integration layer, sitting between corporate
systems and dedicated device networks, or as an integration
layer between disparate control systems. Used in such a man-
ner, the HVAC industry, integrators and building owners will
surely beneﬁ t from XML.
References
1. W3C Transformations (XSLT), Version 1.0, Nov. 1999.
www.w3.org/TR/xslt.
Advertisement formerly in this space.
December 2004
ASHRAE Journal
115
This is the 20th article based on a recent DOE report cover-
ing energy-saving HVAC technologies.
A
ir-source heat pumps (ASHP) are air conditioners with
a reversing valve and a few additional components that
allow the vapor cycle to pump heat either out of the
t t
conditioned space (cooling) or into the conditioned space
(heating). The resulting cost increase is small compared to
an air conditioner with electric resistance heating and much
less than the additional cost of a gas furnace.
The majority of ASHPs are installed in moderate to warm
areas of the United States, essentially south of the Mason-Dixon
Line. Two major issues make conventional ASHPs unattractive
as a heat source in cold climates. First, buildings in colder
climates often have much smaller design cooling loads than
design heating loads. Second, ASHP heating capacity and
coefﬁ cient of performance (COP) decreases as the outdoor
temperature, To, decreases because the temperature lift across
the compressor increases.
Conceptually, a heat pump for a cold climate would enable
effective use of heat pumps in much colder climates than
current designs, e.g., with design temperatures around To~0°F
(–18°C). This would extend the region in which heat pumps
reduce heating primary energy consumption to over much of
the northern U.S. To achieve this goal, heat pump systems
need to incorporate sufﬁ cient capacity to meet heating loads
without large quantities of electric resistance heat and also
achieve higher primary energy efﬁ ciencies at lower outdoor
temperatures. Several design modiﬁ cations and technologies
have been proposed or introduced (alone and in combination)
for cold-climate heat pumps, including:
• Multiple or modulating compressors address mismatched
loads by sizing compressor capacity to meet heating design
loads at full capacity, while part-load operation efﬁ ciently
satisﬁ es cooling loads.1 Variable-capacity compressors do
not, however, address the problem of reduced heating cycle
efﬁ ciency as To decreases.2
• Geothermal heat pumps, also known as ground- or water-
coupled heat pumps (HP), overcome the problem of reduced
cycle efﬁ ciency in cold ambient air by extracting heat from the
soil or groundwater at more constant temperatures than ASHPs
to achieve near-constant heating and cooling efﬁ ciencies year-
round. Reference 3 discusses geothermal HPs in further detail.
• Increased outdoor coil capacity enables the HP to extract
more heat at a given To.
• Carbon dioxide (CO2 ) refrigerant cycles that exploit the
thermodynamic characteristics of CO2 to provide about 35%
greater capacity at To=17°F (–8°C). This decreases the use of
electric resistance heating and also signiﬁ cantly reduces system
oversizing relative to the design cooling load. CO2 also rejects
heat over a wider temperature range, which enables higher air
delivery temperatures without thermodynamic penalty.4
• Mechanical liquid subcooling increases both capacity (by
g
~10%) and efﬁ ciency (by ~10%).
• Optimization of the indoor and outdoor coil circuiting for
l l
heating mode could also enhance capacity and efﬁ ciency.
The rest of this column focuses on approaches that use
multiple or modulating compressors with R-410A or CO2
refrigerant.
Energy Savings Potential
At moderate temperatures, ASHPs have superior primary en-
ergy efﬁ ciency (e.g., at To= 45°F to 50°F [7°C to 10°C] an ASHP
consumes about half the primary energy of a gas furnace). As
To decreases, however, its primary energy efﬁ ciency decreases
rapidly and, below the balance point (heating load equals heat
pump capacity), requires inefﬁ cient electric resistance heat.3
A simple analysis compared the performance of two 10 EER
ASHP options and a conventional option, an 80% Annual Fuel
Utilization Efﬁ ciency (AFUE) furnace, to heat a small ofﬁ ce
in Chicago. Speciﬁ cally, it evaluated the performance of: 1)
a 5-ton (17.6 kW) heat pump (sized for cooling design load)
with a two-stage compressor, and 2) a 10-ton (35 kW) heat
pump (sized for heating design load) comprised of one 5-ton
(17.6 kW) variable-capacity compressor and one 5-ton (17.6
kW) standard compressor. The analysis found that the 10-ton
(35 kW) unit reduced primary energy consumption for heating
by about 25% relative to the 5-ton (17.6 kW) unit. Relative to
the furnace, the 10-ton (35 kW) HP reduced primary energy
consumption by about 10%. On a national basis, this suggests
that ASHPs sized to meet heating loads can reduce commercial
building heating energy consumption by about 10%.3
On the other hand, a preliminary analysis of a 10-ton (35 kW)
CO2 heat pump suggests that it would consume slightly more
energy than an R-410A ASHP of comparable size. In that case,
the additional energy consumption due to a decrease (~10%) in
By John Dieckmann, P.E., Member ASHRAE, Kurt Roth, Ph.D., Associate Member ASHRAE, and
James Brodrick, Ph.D., Member ASHRAE
Heat Pumps for Cold Climates

116
ASHRAE Journal
December 2004
costs. In this situation, an ASHP sized to meet heating loads
costs In this situation an ASHP sized to meet heating loads
cannot recover the initial cost premium unless it has lower
electricity costs.
References
1. Roth, K.W., et al. 2004. “Modulating compressors.” ASHRAE
Journal 46(10):56–57.
l
2. Biancardi, F.R. and T.H. Sienel. 1997, “Advanced concepts for
cold climate heat pumps.” Proceedings of the Third International
Conference on Heat Pumps in Cold Climates, Natural Resources
Canada, pp. 325–337.
3. TIAX. 2002. “Energy consumption characteristics of commercial
building HVAC systems – Volume III: energy savings potential.” Final
Report to U.S. Department of Energy, Ofﬁ ce of Building Technologies.
Available at www.tiax.biz/aboutus/pdfs/HVAC3-FinalReport.pdf.
4. Richter, M.R., et al. 2000. “Transcritical CO2 heat pump for
residential applications.” Proceedings of the 4th IIR-Gustav Lorentzen
Conference on Natural Working Fluids, Purdue University.
5. Nastro, T. 2002. Personal Communication, The Trane Company.
John Dieckmann, P.E., is a principal and Kurt W. Roth,
Ph.D., is a senior technologist, at the HVAC and Refrigeration
Technology sector of TIAX, Cambridge, Mass. James Brodrick,
Ph.D., is a project manager, Building Technologies Program,
U.S. Department of Energy, Washington, D.C.
COP exceeds the energy savings from reduced use of electric
COP exceeds the energy savings from reduced use of electric
resistance heating from greater heating capacity.3
Market Factors
In heating-dominated climates, heat pumps compete for
market share against furnaces and boilers. According to
Nastro,4 a 5- or 10-ton (17.6 or 35 kW) packaged rooftop unit
(RTU) with an HP sized for cooling loads has about the same
ﬁ rst cost as an RTU with air conditioning and a gas furnace
section (not including the cost of installing gas service).
Thus, the incremental cost for an RTU with an ASHP sized
to meet heating loads amounts to the incremental cost of the
additional (modulating) compressor, refrigerant circuit for
that compressor, additional heat exchanger surface required
for both the condenser and evaporator, and any increase in
RTU box size required to accommodate the additional equip-
ment, less the cost of the gas furnace. Overall, an HP sized
to meet the heating loads of a colder climate likely will cost
appreciably more than a conventional RTU option.
Using the same analysis described for the ofﬁ ce build-
ing in Chicago, the RTU with heat pumps sized to meet
the heating load consumes approximately 10% less heating
primary energy than the conventional option. The gas fur-
nace, however, has appreciably lower (~20%) heating energy

By Evans J. Lizardos, P.E., Fellow/Life Member ASHRAE
HVAC System
For Tunnel Booths
T
he Port Authority of New York and New Jersey (PANY/NJ)
needed to replace the staffed shelter booths that the Port
Authority Tunnel and Bridge Agents (TBAs) use to monitor trafﬁ c
conditions inside the Holland and Lincoln tunnels. Two shelter
booths are in each of the tubes serving the Holland and Lincoln
tunnels for a total of 10, with four booths in the Holland Tunnel
(two tubes) and six in the Lincoln Tunnel (three tubes).
Each tube has the following sectional
conﬁ guration:
• Middle compartment tunnel: houses
the vehicle roadway path;
• Lower compartment tunnel: intro-
duces 100% outside ventilation air to the
middle compartment; and
• Upper compartment tunnel: ex-
hausts the vehicle fumes of the middle
compartment.
Early on, PANY/NJ decided to replace
the booths in lieu of rehabilitating them.
The improvements PANY/NJ sought from
the new booths included an improvement
in air quality for personnel stationed in
each booth and enhanced visibility.
Challenges and Implementation
The design team worked closely togeth-
er to meet the requirements of PANY/NJ.
The ﬁ rst challenge was to transport the
TBAs to the booth with minimal contact
to the air in the tunnel.
• To transport the TBAs to the booths,
a battery-powered car was designed
to ride along a guide rail through the
tunnel to the shelter booths. Once the
car reaches the entrance of the booth
it pushes through the doors (the doors
swing in both directions) and the vehicle
enters the booth. The TBA steps out of
the vehicle into the closed booth, and the
vehicle continues on the track to the next
booth, again pushing through the doors
on the other end. A seat that retracts
into the wall to allow for the vehicle to
pass through can then be lowered so that
the TBA can sit and be able to view the
trafﬁ c in the tunnel as well as be seen
by passing motorists.

About the Author
Evans J. Lizardos, P.E., is president of Lizardos
Engineering Associates, Mineola, N.Y. He received
an honorable mention for the 2005 ASHRAE
Technology Award for Industrial Facilities or
Processes (Existing).
June 2005
ASHRAE Journal
39
Schematic of redundant custom dual air-conditioning unit for staffed shelter booths in the Lincoln and Holland tunnels.
• The next challenge was to design a ventilation system that
could ﬁ t within the booth. The only space available to place
the unit was the curved top of the booth. This portion of the
booth was curved to ﬁ t up against the curved ceiling of the
tunnel. To meet this challenge, the team worked together
to custom design an HVAC unit.
· The air-conditioning system serving the
booths is based on 100% outside air sup-
ply providing more than 100 air changes
per hour, with exﬁ ltration (in which the
supply air leaks out in gaps around the
doors and other openings housing the
booth). This system ensures that the
booths are maintained under positive
pressure and carbon monoxide gases
(which are monitored continuously by
tunnel systems controllers), from the
discharge of the moving vehicles, cannot
inﬁ ltrate the booth.
· This 100% outside air supply unit has
95% air-cleaning ﬁ lter efﬁ ciency. It is capable of
cooling the air to 50°F (10°C) when the outdoor
ambient air is 100°F (38°C). Conversely, when the
ambient air temperature is 0°F (–18°C), the unit can heat the
booth to 80°F (27°C).
· In the event the 100% supply air system fails, a redundant
air-conditioning unit ensures continuous supply air and prevents
inﬁ ltration of the carbon monoxide gases. Upon failure of an
air-conditioning unit, it is capable of being repaired while the
redundant unit remains in operation.
· Both air-conditioning units are located in the ceiling of the
shelter booth. The booths are specially designed to ﬁ t the
contours of the tunnel, creating a small pie-shaped
space for the air-conditioning units. As a result,
the air-conditioning units were custom de-
signed to ﬁ t perfectly into this space with
provisions for maintenance and replace-
ment of a malfunctioning unit without
disturbing the operating unit.
· The supply duct distribution system
with automatic motorized isolation
dampers, housed in the ceiling conﬁ gu-
ration, allows either air-conditioning
unit to supply air to the booth.
· Both air-conditioning units are mount-
ed in separate housings and mounted in
place prior to shipment from the factory. Each
of the units has the following components:
- Supply air fan;
- Electric heating coil;
- Direct expansion cooling coil;
- Air-cooled condensing coil;
- Refrigeration compressor;
- Thirty percent efﬁ cient pre-ﬁ lter;

ASHRAE Journal ashrae.org
June 2005
Indoor Air Quality
The HVAC system serving the booths is based on 100%
outside air supply providing more than 100 air changes per
hour, with exﬁ ltration (in which the supply air leaks out
in gaps around the doors and other openings housing the
booth). This system ensures that the booths are maintained
under positive pressure and carbon monoxide gases (which
are monitored continuously by tunnel systems controllers)
from the discharge of the moving vehicles cannot inﬁ ltrate
the booth. This 100% outside air supply unit also has 95%
air-cleaning ﬁ lter efﬁ ciency.
Innovation
The HVAC was custom designed to replace the residential
window-type unit with a system that provided the following
features: environmental conditions within the booth that could
be maintained on a continuous basis without the outdoor am-
bient conditions impacting the performance; system standby
redundancy; repair of the downed unit while redundant unit is
operational; and the HVAC system be completely contained
within the shelter booth structure so that it can be factory
mounted and shipped to the site for rapid installation.
Operating and Maintenance
Each booth has two custom HVAC units built in to achieve
100% redundancy, thereby if one unit breaks down, the other
unit starts up. In addition, each tunnel has four complete sets
of spare parts in inventory to eliminate downtime waiting for
parts while units are being repaired.
Cost Effectiveness
This project represents the prototype development of a tun-
nel shelter booth. The factory-assembled booth is a one-piece
design and shipped directly to the tunnel for installation. It is
the model design that will be used for all present and future
PANY/NJ tunnels.
Photo of tunnel shelter booth.
- Ninety-ﬁ ve percent efﬁ cient after-ﬁ lter;
- Modulating electric heating coil control (SCR);
- Hot gas refrigeration control for cooling and dehumidiﬁ -
cation;
- A single control direct digital control panel with all relays,
monitoring devices, etc., is mounted in the booth with ac-
cess from inside the booth; and
- Built into the shelter booth is a supply duct that traverses
from the lower compartment supply air tunnel to the ceil-
ing of the booth, which is where the air-conditioning units
are housed.
· Since the car passes through each booth, it is imperative that
the seat be in a vertical position and out of the way of the car.
To ensure this, a proximity sensor controlling a small trafﬁ c
light was designed to let the operator of the car know that the
seat is up and the route is clear.
Energy Efﬁ ciency
The booths have a traditional 100% outside air system using
direct expansion cooling and electric heating. They also use
energy recovery and/or condenser refrigerant reheat. However,
when a bin-analysis was applied to the minimal 250 cfm outdoor
air requirement with associated costs and space restraints, the
energy-efﬁ ciency gains were not practical or feasible.

About the Author
Jeff Christian is director of the Building Technol-
ogy Center at Oak Ridge National Laboratory,
Oak Ridge, Tenn.
T
he ﬁ rst attempt at low ﬁ rst-cost, near zero-energy houses (ZEH)
was in June 2002 in Tennessee as part of the U.S. Department of
TwT
Energy’s Building Technologies Program. The goal is to create technologies
for low-cost net-zero energy residences by 2020. The initiative is to lead
new homeowners and builders toward houses integrate on-site power.1
This article addresses affordable housing in a mixed, humid climate.
The features in the ﬁ rst attempt at zero
energy houses (ZEH1) are listed in Tables
1 and 2
d . The small, efﬁ cient houses were
constructed for under $100,000, which
includes the cost for solar photovoltaics.
The ZEH1 all-electric house uses
about 10,000 kWh/year. The heat pump
and ventilation require about 30% of the
total energy, and the heat pump water
heater (HPWH) requires 15%. Local
electric rates at the time were $0.063/
kWh. This was the ﬁ rst house in Ten-
nessee to sell solar energy to the grid.
The solar system generates about 2,000
kWh/year. The net energy cost to the
homeowner is less than a dollar a day.2
House Descriptions
This article focuses on the ﬁ rst of a
series of four near net-ZEHs (ZEH1).
A full year’s data during the ﬁ rst year of

i
th ﬁt
f
occupancy is available for ZEH1, ZEH2
and ZEH3. This article focuses primarily
on ZEH1 although some comparisons are
made to the other houses (ZEH2, ZEH3
and ZEH4). Table 1 shows envelope
features, and Table 2 shows mechanical
features.
Structural insulated panels (SIP) were
used in the four test houses. These panels
were premanufactured with either ex-
panded polystyrene or polyisocyanurate
insulation, blown with pentane, sand-
wiched between two layers of orientated

site with rough openings for windows
and doors and channels for running

ASHRAE Journal
21
(ZEH1) beyond those listed in Tables 1 and 2
d
are compact
thermal distribution system, controlled mechanical supply
ventilation of 40 cfm,\* (meeting ANSI/ASHRAE 62.2-2003,
Ventilation and Indoor Air Quality in Low-Rise Residential
Buildings), compact ﬂ orescent lights, Energy Star® appliances,
heat recovery shower, insulated water pipes in the crawlspace,
and extended roof overhangs.
The ZEH1 three-bedroom, 1,056 ft2\* house sits on an un-
vented crawlspace with 6 mil black polyethylene ground cover.
The ﬂ oor of the house is 6 in.\* thick SIPS as shown in Figure 1.
The ﬂ oor SIPS have 22 mil white aluminum sheets laminated
to the bottom.
The unvented crawlspace remains above 50°F\* throughout
the winter. The coldest ambient temperature experienced in
2003 was 0°F. This earth-coupled space not only minimizes
wintertime ﬂ oor heat loss but also eliminates the risk of pipes
freezing and provides a heat source for the HPWH during the
heating seasons.
On July 3, the crawlspace’s relative humidity for several days
was near 80%. The highest wet-bulb temperature in July was
approximately 71°F. The average daily interior temperature was
76°F. This indicates that even if the bottom of the ﬂ oor reached
an average inside air temperature, it would still be well above
the average wet-bulb temperature experienced in this crawlspace
during the worst part of the year. Vented crawlspaces during this
time in the mixed humid climate of east Tennessee are frequently
saturated and experience lengthy periods of near 100% RH.
Figure 2 shows the hourly average humidity ratio for a warm
moist day in July of the crawl space air and the outside air. The
humidity ratio in the crawlspace remains slightly less than the
outside air. Conditioning the crawlspace by providing a very
small amount (~20 cfm) of supply air might be considered if
the ﬂ oor did not have the metal laminate to restrict moisture
uptake into the ﬂ oor assembly. A second consideration would be
to place a dehumidiﬁ er in the crawlspace from June – August.
Envelope
The cross section is shown in Figure 3. The house walls are
4.5 in. thick SIPS with 1 lb/ft3\* density expanded polystyrene
between 7/16 in. OSB. The wall panels were fastened together
using well-sealed surface splines.
The 4/12 pitch cathedral ceiling consists of 8 in. thick full
length 4 ft wide panels, from ridge-to-eave, connected with
surface splines. The roof system R-value is 28 h·ft²·°F/Btu at
75°F.\* The windows are wood, vinyl-clad, double-hung with
National Fenestration Rating Council (NFRC) labeled U-factor
of 0.34 and solar heat gain coefﬁ cient of 0.36.
Prior to selection of the opaque walls and roofs for ZEH1,
two 12 ft by 12 ft by 8 ft\* high test rooms were constructed
in a laboratory. One room was constructed using 4.5 in. thick
SIP walls with 10 embedded electrical boxes and connecting
wires. The second room with identical inside ﬂ oor space was
constructed and tested in exactly the same manner. This room
had 2 by 6 at 16 in. on center frame walls with R-19 ﬁ berglass
batt insulation, a ﬂ at insulated roof with a layer of R-19, plus
a second R-11 layer perpendicular to the R-19, the same win-
dow, door, and electrical wiring system. Airtightness tests were
conducted on both test rooms.
The data for the SIP test room is shown in Figure 4 as the
bottom line with the triangles “” pointing upward. The frame
test room is displayed as the line in Figure 4 with the triangles
“” pointing downward. This data is consistent with tightest
and the leakiest of six nearly identical stick frame houses all
built by the same contractor as all the houses described in Tables

Table 2: Mechanical features for ZEH1, ZEH2, ZEH3, ZEH4 and a base house.
1 and 2
d . The tightest and leakiest frame house lines are shown
in Figure 4 with “+” and “×” symbols.
This conﬁ rms that the frame test room represents wood-frame
ﬁ eld conditions. At 50 Pa of pressure across the envelope the
SIP test room had an air leakage of 0.078 ft3/ft2\*\* of ﬂ oor area.
The 2 by 6 test room had an air leakage value of 1.06 ft3/ft2 of
ﬂ oor area. The 2 by 6 frame test room was 14 times leakier than
the SIP structure. These airtightness measurements encouraged
the selection of a SIP envelope for ZEH1.
A blower door analysis was run on ZEH1 prior to installation
of the drywall, after the house was ready to be moved into and
six months after occupancy. The line with the “o” shows the
ZEH1 whole house leakage prior to drywall. The ZEH1 leakage
per unit of ﬂ oor area measured out at 0.15 cfm/ft2\*\* at 50 Pa.
This can be compared to the wood frame base house of 0.87. The
base wood frame house was six times leakier than ZEH1.
Domestic Water Heating (DHW)
ZEH1 is equipped with a domestic HPWH. The warmer the
air source, the better the HPWH’s thermal performance. Also,
to take advantage of the space cooling and dehumidiﬁ cation
available from the HPWH, the unit is connected to the airspace
behind the refrigerator. When the house thermostat is set for
\*\* ft2/ft3 × 0.02832 ÷ 0.0929 = m3/m2; cfm/ft2 × 0.4719 ÷ 0.0929 = L/s/m2
January 2005
ASHRAE Journal
23
cooling, motorized dampers allow the HPWH fans to pull air
from behind the refrigerator into the evaporator coil of the
HPWH. This airstream is cooled, dehumidiﬁ ed and directed
back into the conditioned space.
When the thermostat is in the “off ” or “heating mode,” the
ducts connecting the HPWH to the kitchen are closed and
other ducts are opened to pull in earth tempered crawlspace air
and reject unneeded cold air
outside. Since the crawlspace
is unvented, it will go under
a slight negative pressure
when the HPWH is operating
during non-cooling periods.
The crawlspace access is not
weather stripped and this
crack allows makeup air into
the crawlspace. The crawl-
space remains above 50°F the
entire winter.
The water heating usage
measurements show the occu-
pants used 72% of the DHW
for showers and baths. The
average daily usage was 40
gallon/day.† The measured daily HPWH energy consumption
is 3.8 kWh. The average HPWH COP was 1.62. With lower
hot water usage, the standby losses are a higher percentage of
energy usage resulting in lower COPs.
Because the HPWH and refrigerator were located on inside
walls of the kitchen, long duct runs were required to vent to
the outside and into the kitchen. The installed ﬂ ex ducts gener-
ated excessive static pressure, restricting air movement away
from the HPWH. The optimized coupling with refrigerator,
crawlspace, space cooling and dehumidiﬁ cation was not at-
tained in ZEH1.
In ZEH2, the HPWH COP was measured closer to 2.0. This
would save 337 kWh/year ($0.06/day [$0.063/kWh]). ZEH2
has a second generation hook up that consists of the refrigera-
tor and the HPWH located adjacent to the outside wall and has
all hard ducts, and short runs to and from the unit to minimize
static pressure. The COPs in this unit from March 2004 until
September 2004 averaged almost 2.0.
Thermal Comfort
The conditioned space average hourly temperature and rela-
tive humidity for one year are shown in Figure 5. The thermo-
stat temperature on average is
kept around 75°F. No setback
or setup schedules were pro-
grammed in this house. The
occasional spikes in RH are
due to window openings and
24/7 mechanical ventilation.
The homeowners generally
were not home during the
summer weekday time pe-
riods. Even though the RH
would tend to drift above 60%
on some hot summer days
the owners had no thermal
comfort complaints. Because
of the airtight envelope, well-
shaded low solar heat gain
windows, and continuous mechanical ventilation the ther-
mostat would occasionally not call for sensible cooling until
after the RH rose above what would be considered acceptable
in some situations. Later designs of the HVAC systems in
ZEH2, ZEH3 and ZEH4 all tried to address this potentially
unacceptable high relative humidity levels during the summer
cooling period.
Total Electric Energy Usage and Cost
The ZEH1 all-electric house, from March 1, 2003, until Feb.
29, 2004, used 10,216 kWh, as shown by Table 3. The heat pump
and ventilation fan power required 2,759 kWh or 27% of the

total energy. The HPWH used 1,549 or 15% of the total. The
rest of the energy loads in the building required 5907 kWh or
58% of the total. Prior to construction, the Home Energy Rating
System (HERS) rating was calculated to be 90.2, which converts
to 50% better than the International Energy Conservation Code
(IECC). The electric rates in this area during the monitoring
period were $0.63/kWh. This house sells green power to the
electric utility. The utility pays the homeowner $0.15/kWh for
all the solar power produced by the 2kWp PV system for 10
years. During this one year monitoring period, the solar system
generated 2006 kWh and a solar credit of $300. The net energy
cost to the homeowner is $343/year, or $0.94/day. In the winter
of 2002 – 03, the homeowners used a bit less energy than the
winter of 2003 – 04, during this annual period starting from
November 2002 the cost would have been $0.82/day.

base house. The local electric utility certiﬁ ed the base house as

Energy Use
Table 3 shows monthly measured electric energy usage for
ZEH1. The columns labeled “space heat,” “space cool,” “hot
water” and “other” equal the values shown in “total electric.”
The annual energy cost for space heating totals about $100,
space cooling $74, domestic hot water $98, the “Other” equates
to $372. The total cost is calculated by subtracting the solar
credit at $0.15/kWh or $301 from the total used, $644/year.
Table 3 shows the 2kWp
PV system monthly genera-
tion in the column “solar ac
generated.” The total solar
annual generation was 20%
of the total energy used. The
last column shows the solar
power generated on-site but
at the time was not needed in the house and, therefore, sent to
the grid. The estimated ﬁ rst cost of the ZEH1 solar system was
$22,388, as shown in Table 3. The total installed solar system
cost with the same capacity installed in ZEH2 and ZEH3 was
$16,000. The installed cost in ZEH4 was under $15,000 and
had 10% more capacity. Solar system ﬁ rst cost will have to
continue to drop and photovoltaic utility incentives continue to
rise. The buyback incentives could be going up to $0.20 kWh,
and at market costs for this size solar system in late 2004 of
around $13,700, the simple solar payback for this location and
current residential electric rates is around 30 years. The total
cost to build these three experimental houses is shown in Table
4 along with the base house.
About 40% of the solar power generation was not coincident
with the house energy demand. This solar power for the most
part is available during hot summer afternoon hours when
utility electric grids values not only the reduction in load but
the power to help meet peak period cooling demands. The PV
system on this house on average reduced summer peak loads
by 40% in the three summer months.
Construction Cost
These houses were all constructed by the Habitat for Human-
ity Loudon County Afﬁ liate. Most of the construction was done
with volunteer labor. Subcontractors were hired for plumbing,
HVAC, foundation installation and site work, concrete pave-
ment, and drywall installation. Volunteer hours were kept track
of by a sign in/out book. The
value assigned per volunteer
hour was $5.50. The Habitat
for Humanity afﬁ liate’s indi-
cated that this rate represents
local market value.
Table 4 shows a spreadsheet
for construction cost of ZEH1,
ZEH2, ZEH3 and the base house. The good news is that these
near ZEHs cost only $100,000. Because of the research aspects
of these test houses, the costs are higher than production units.
Production units will result in lower material and installation
cost reductions. Additional utility incentives are anticipated for
future PV systems. It is hoped that this research will also result
in incentives for solar water heating and geothermal systems,
which could be converted to green labeled energy and made a
part of the local utility green power program.
Lessons Learned
Table 3 shows that more than 75% of the heating energy for
an air-to-air heat pump is consumed in the three coldest months,
December, January, and February. ZEH2 and ZEH3 research
houses used better envelopes, and better heat pumps. ZEH3 used
a direct exchange geothermal system for January and February
2004 demanded 19% less total house energy and produced 12%

26
ASHRAE Journal ashrae.org
January 2005
more solar power than ZEH1. The orientation of this house is a
bit better and has a 6/12 pitched roof compared to ZEH1’s 4/12.
By the end of September 2004 and nine months of measured
performance the solar system on ZEH3 had generated the same
amount of AC solar as that on ZEH1. The biggest energy sav-
ings in ZEH3 is believed to come from the geothermal heat
exchanger, drawing heat from the surrounding earth, which
stayed above 50°F in the winter and below 73°F in the summer,
compared to the much colder night time winter ambient air, and
higher ambient PM cooling periods. The additional features of
this second generation of near ZEH are listed in Table 1 and 2.
The local electric utility auditors rated ZEH3 with a HERS =
93.9. Of course, the DOE 2020 affordability cost goal has not
been met in these ﬁ rst prototypes.
Conclusions
ZEH1 had a rated HERS of 90. It used about 40% less energy
than the base house with electric utility certiﬁ ed performance
of HERS 84. The total cost to build ZEH1 including the mar-
ket value for all the donated time and materials came to about
$100,000. The total energy costs are under $1/day. The annual
heating cost of ZEH1 using the local electricity rate of $0.063/
kWh, was under $100, cooling under $75, and domestic hot
water under $100.
The solar PV system generated about 2,000 kWh over the ﬁ rst
full year. This amounted to 20% of total energy load and 74%
of HVAC load. The lowest average total energy cost less the
solar credits came to $0.82/day. This was the ﬁ rst local electric
generation utility’s Green Power Switch Generation Partner.
With the electric utility green power offering $0.15/kWh and
the energy saving features this house experienced an annual
energy cost savings of 65%. ZEH3 had been occupied for only
nine months and with normalized occupancy the daily average
energy cost is extrapolated for a full year to be $0.68/day for
ZEH3. The use of geothermal heat pump in the winter was
making a substantial step closer to net-zero. However, it did
add ﬁ rst cost. Ongoing research and development is leading
to lower ﬁ rst cost reductions in the envelope, HPWH and geo-
thermal heat pump.
References
1. DOE Building Technologies goal for zero energy buildings can
be found at www.eere.energy.gov/buildings/program\_areas/zeroen-
ergy.html.
2. Christian, J.E., D. Beal, P. Kerrigan. 2004. “Toward simple afford-
able zero energy houses, performance of exterior envelopes of whole
buildings.” IX International Conference Proceedings CD.

ASHRAE Journal ashrae.org

Standard 62-2001 Addendum 62n
Single-Path
Multiple-Zone
System Design
By Dennis Stanke, Member ASHRAE
About the Author
Dennis Stanke is a staff applications engineer
with Trane, La Crosse, Wis. He is vice chair of
SSPC 62.1.
NSI/ASHRAE Standard 62-2001, Ventilation for Acceptable Indoor
Air Quality,1 as modiﬁ ed by Addendum 62n,2 prescribes new
minimum breathing-zone ventilation rates and new calculation proce-
dures to ﬁ nd intake airﬂ ow for different ventilation systems. Previous
articles3,4 discussed the design of “simple” ventilation systems (single-
zone, 100% outdoor-air, and changeover-bypass VAV) in compliance
with Addendum 62n requirements. Here, we examine the design of a
more complex set of ventilation systems, namely single-path, multiple-
zone recirculating systems.
zone, recirculating ventilation systems.
For instance, constant-volume systems
with terminal reheat, traditional constant-
volume multizone systems, single-duct
VAV systems, and single-fan dual-duct
VAV systems all provide ventilation from
a single source or path. (A single-fan,
dual-duct system supplies air to each
space using two different ducts, but the
air in each duct contains the same fraction
of outdoor air, because one fan—a single
source—delivers the same air mixture to
each duct.) Other systems have multiple
ventilation paths, including dual-fan,
dual-duct VAV systems and VAV systems
with fan-powered or induction terminal
units. Single-duct VAV systems with
series fan-powered boxes are always
dual-path ventilation systems, but those
with parallel fan-powered boxes are
single-path with the local fan off and
dual-path with it on. Although any of
these HVAC systems may be used in vari-
Although the Ventilation Rate Pro-
cedure in Standard 62 has required
speciﬁ c calculations (Equation 6-1) for
multiple-zone systems since 1989, the
calculation procedure was sketchy at
best; consequently, it was widely misun-
derstood and largely ignored by design-
ers. Addendum 62n includes a detailed
calculation procedure for multiple-zone
system design. Use of this procedure is
expected to increase consistency among
designers and reduce the tendency to de-
sign multiple-zone systems—especially
VAV systems—that provide inadequate
ventilation for some fully occupied zones.
Addendum 62n also includes operational
control options that can be used to modu-
late ventilation capacity as ventilation
load and/or efﬁ ciency varies, but these
options are left to a future article. The
following discussion covers only design
calculations.
Many HVAC systems are conﬁ gured as
“single-supply” or single-path, multiple-
A
N
A
N
© 2005, American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers, Inc. (www.ashrae.
org). Reprinted by permission from ASHRAE Journal, (Vol. 47, No. 1, January 2005). This article may not
be copied nor distributed in either paper or digital form without ASHRAE’s permission.
January 2005
ASHRAE Journal
29
outdoor air in its primary airstream. In the past, many designers
simply added the zone outdoor airﬂ ow requirements and set the
intake airﬂ ow to match this sum, which resulted in a very low
outdoor-air fraction and many underventilated zones.
Some designers went to the other extreme, ﬁ nding the highest
fraction of outdoor air needed by any zone in the system and
setting the intake airﬂ ow to provide this fraction at all times.
This approach considers only ﬁ rst-pass outdoor air, giving no
credit for unused recirculated outdoor air, and results in a very
high outdoor-air fraction and overventilation in all zones.
Proper design in compliance with Addendum 62n calculation
procedures strikes a balance between these extremes, appro-
priately accounting for both critical-zone needs and unused,
recirculated outdoor air.
Let’s look at an example ofﬁ ce building (Figure 2
(F
(
). We as-
sumed that thermal comfort can be achieved using only eight
VAV thermostats, with each thermostat control-
ling one or more VAV boxes. We considered each
of these “comfort zones” (or “HVAC zones” per
ASHRAE Standard 90.1-2001) as a separate “ven-
tilation zone.”
According to Addendum 62n, a ventilation zone
is “one occupied space or several occupied spaces
with similar occupancy category, occupant den-
sity, zone air-distribution effectiveness, and zone
primary airﬂ ow per unit area.”
Most (but not all) HVAC zones qualify as
ventilation zones. The area and population for
each zone in this example were selected to help illustrate the
calculations rather than to reﬂ ect typical zone sizes or popula-
tion densities.
To comply with Addendum 62n, our design calculations
begin by ﬁ nding the ventilation needs at the zone level and
conclude by determining the required intake airﬂ ow at the
system level.
Zone Ventilation Calculations
Following the procedure under “zone calculations” in Sec-
tion 6.2.1, we found zone outdoor airﬂ ow (Voz) for each zone

Demonstrating Compliance by Example
Our example system (Figure 1
(F
(
) includes a central air handler,
with a modulating outdoor-air damper that may be controlled
as an economizer; a variable-volume supply fan to deliver
primary air; cooling-only, throttling VAV boxes in the interior
zones; throttling VAV boxes with electric reheat in the perim-
eter zones; a central return fan; and a central relief damper for
building pressure control. Although we won’t discuss system
control details here, it’s important that we share the same mental
“picture” of the VAV system we’re designing:
• Intake airﬂ ow is sensed and maintained by adjusting the
intake damper position. (Often, the return- and
outdoor-air dampers are linked such that closing
the outdoor-air damper opens the return-air damper
proportionately. Alternately, these dampers can be
controlled separately to reduce fan energy while
maintaining proper intake airﬂ ow, but this has
no impact on ventilation requirements at design
conditions.)
• Primary air temperature is sensed and main-
tained by sequentially adjusting the heating-coil
control valve, economizer dampers, and cooling-
coil control valve.
• Duct pressure is sensed and maintained at setpoint by ad-
justing the primary fan capacity (via fan speed, for instance,
or inlet guide vane position).
• Zone temperature is sensed and maintained at the cool-
ing setpoint by adjusting the setpoint for VAV-box primary
airﬂ ow.
• VAV-box airﬂ ow is sensed and maintained at setpoint by
adjusting the position of the VAV-box damper.
• For zones that need reheat, zone temperature is sensed and
maintained at the heating setpoint by adjusting reheat capacity
(electric reheat or a hot water valve) and, thereby, discharge air
temperature.
• Return air plenum pressure (at the central air handler) is
sensed and maintained by adjusting return fan capacity.
• Building pressure is sensed and maintained between set lim-
its by adjusting the relief (central exhaust) damper position.
Since multiple-zone systems provide the same primary air
mixture to all zones, the fraction of outdoor air in the primary
airstream must be sufﬁ cient to deliver the outdoor airﬂ ow needed
by the “critical” zone—the zone needing the greatest fraction of

earlier article3 covered population-averaging calculations in
detail. See www.ashrae.org for the most current version.)

System Ventilation Calculations
As in Standard 62-1989, -1999, and -2001, Addendum 62n
recognizes that multiple-zone recirculating systems must
overventilate some zones to properly ventilate all zones. It
also recognizes that “unused” outdoor air recirculated from
overventilated zones reduces the required intake airﬂ ow, but
that unused outdoor air that leaves the building (by exhaust or
exﬁ ltration) increases the required intake airﬂ ow. Proper ac-
counting results in a ventilation credit for recirculated outdoor
air and a ventilation debit for exhausted outdoor air.
Addendum 62n makes this accounting straightforward by
requiring a speciﬁ c calculation procedure to determine the
minimum outdoor-air intake ﬂ ow based on the system ventila-
tion efﬁ ciency inherent in every multiple-zone recirculating
system.
Earlier versions of the standard required use of the “mul-
tiple-space” equation, Y = X / (1 + X – Z), to ﬁ nd the fraction
of intake air needed. This approach resulted in about the same
intake airﬂ ow as Addendum 62n for single-path systems; but
without a clear procedural explanation, the equation was widely
misunderstood and largely ignored by designers.
Designs based on the 62n procedure result in proper ventila-
tion for the critical zone at worst-case design conditions while
allowing credit for “good” outdoor air that recirculates from
all other overventilated zones.
From the zone calculations that we completed earlier, we
know how much outdoor airﬂ ow must reach the diffusers in
each zone. Now, let’s ﬁ gure out the minimum required intake
airﬂ ow for the system at design conditions.
Before we start, we should recognize something that Ad-
dendum 62n implies but doesn’t explain: The “worst-case” or
highest required intake airﬂ ow may or may not occur at the
design cooling condition (when system primary airﬂ ow is high-
est). In some cases, it may actually occur at the design heating
condition (when zone primary airﬂ ow values are very low). With
In earlier versions of the standard, only “intermittent oc-
cupancy” zones (at peak population for three hours or less)
could be designed for ventilation at the average population
(but not less than one-half of the peak population). Now, any
zone may be designed for average population. According to
the “short-term conditions” section of Addendum 62n, the
system must be designed to deliver the required outdoor
airﬂ ow to each occupied breathing zone.
However, if occupancy or intake airﬂ ow varies, the ventila-
tion system design may be based on average conditions over
a speciﬁ c time period rather than on peak conditions. The
averaging time T for a given zone is determined according to
T T
Equation 6-9 (T = 3
T T
v/v/v Voz) using zone volume and the breath-
ing-zone outdoor airﬂ ow that would be needed at peak popula-
tion. T equals three zone time constants, the time it takes for
T
contaminant concentration to achieve a nearly steady-state
value in response to a step change in contaminant source.
When applied to population, this averaging approach replaces
the population-averaging option for “intermittent occupancy”
spaces, found in previous versions of the standard,
Averaging time may be applied to make design ad-
justments when changing conditions in the zone can be
predicted. For instance, if zone population ﬂ uctuations are
predictable, then the design breathing-zone outdoor airﬂ ow
may be calculated based on the highest average population
over any T-minute period.

For our example, we tried to use “reasonable” values for zone
primary airﬂ ow (Vpz) at design cooling load. We arbitrarily
set all minimum primary airﬂ ow settings (Vpz-min) to 25% of
design cooling airﬂ ow. We assumed that each reheat box enters
reheat mode after its primary airﬂ ow decreases to the minimum
setting and the zone temperature drops below the heating set-
point. Reheat operation continues until
the zone temperature exceeds the heating
setpoint.
