by solving Equation 6-5 (Zp = Voz /Vpz ) with the zone outdoor
For single-path VAV systems, the worst-case condition for
ventilation (that is, the lowest system ventilation efﬁ ciency
and the highest required intake airﬂ ow) in the cooling mode
usually occurs when the VAV primary airﬂ ow for the system
is at its highest value. Since almost all VAV systems exhibit
load diversity (all zones don’t require peak cooling airﬂ ow
simultaneously), the critical zone can be assumed to be
delivering minimum primary airﬂ ow with the central fan at
cooling-design or “block” primary airﬂ ow. In some cases,
worst-case ventilation in the cooling mode may actually
occur at a central fan airﬂ ow that’s slightly lower than block
airﬂ ow. If a system doesn’t have much load diversity (all
interior zones, for example)—and if the critical zone requires

a lot of primary airﬂ ow—then the central fan may or may
not be at block airﬂ ow when the critical zone is at minimum
primary airﬂ ow.
How can you ﬁ nd out the system primary airﬂ ow at the
worst-case ventilation condition? Simply assume that
primary airﬂ ow at the fan is the sum of all noncritical-zone
peak airﬂ ow values plus the minimum primary airﬂ ow for the
critical zone. At this condition, the difference between XsXsX and
Zp
Zp
Z will be greatest, so system ventilation efﬁ ciency will be at its
lowest value and outdoor-air intake ﬂ ow will be at its highest
values—the worst-case condition. (Operationally, this worst-
case condition may not actually occur, since it assumes that
the critical zone requires minimum primary airﬂ ow even when
fully occupied; this might be the case for some perimeter
zones, for example, during cold weather.)

\*\* For zones with terminal reheat, discharge air temperature can be either cool or warm when the zone is occupied, so Ez drops from 1.0 when cooling to 0.8 when heating.
z
Figure 3: Zone ventilation calculations.
In multiple-zone recirculating systems, such as
constant-volume reheat systems and all varieties of VAV
systems, one air handler supplies a mixture of outdoor
air and recirculated return air to two or more ventilation
zones. The required outdoor-air intake ﬂ ow only can be
determined by properly accounting for system ventilation
efﬁ ciency. Why?
These ventilation systems include an unavoidable
“built-in” inefﬁ ciency. This inefﬁ ciency exists because the
intake airﬂ ow must be sufﬁ cient to ventilate the critical
zone—the zone that requires the highest fraction of
outdoor air in its primary airstream. Since a multiple-zone
system delivers the same primary air mixture to each
ventilation zone, proper minimum ventilation in the critical
zone overventilates all other zones. As a result, some
outdoor air leaves the building via the relief, exhaust, and
exﬁ ltration airstreams without performing useful dilution.
This inefﬁ ciency isn’t necessarily “bad;” it simply must
be recognized and accounted for in system ventilation
calculations.
Multiple-Zone Systems

we’ll see in future articles.

of these two intake values is the worst-case intake airﬂ ow.

by solving Equation 6-8 (Vot

V =
2,800/0.87 = 3,230 cfm at the design heating condition.
The system is more efﬁ cient at the design heating condition
than it was at the design cooling condition (system ventilation
efﬁ ciency of 0.87 in heating vs. 0.65 in cooling). So, using
the “calculated” approach (Appendix G), worst-case/highest
outdoor-air intake ﬂ ow occurs at the design cooling condition
(Vot
V = 4,310 cfm).
t
Reviewing our previous calculations, if we simply use the
default table to ﬁ nd system ventilation efﬁ ciency (Cases 1 and
2), our example design needs outdoor-air intake ﬂ ow of 4,670
cfm, which occurred at the design heating condition. If we use
the more complicated but more accurate calculations in Appen-
dix G (Cases 3 and 4), our example design needs outdoor-air
intake ﬂ ow of 4,360 cfm, which occurred at the design cooling
condition. Since either approach is allowed, the designer can
comply using either of these intake airﬂ ow values.
Assuming that our system controls can maintain the minimum
required intake airﬂ ow, we can now size both the cooling coil
and the heating coil for worst-case outdoor-air intake ﬂ ow.
What About Part-Load Operation?
To comply with Addendum 62n, we need to ﬁ nd the highest
minimum outdoor-air intake ﬂ ow (Vot
V ), which we’ve called
“worst-case” intake airﬂ ow. We could apply optional adjust-
ments (averaging) for “short-term conditions” in our worst-
case calculations, but we chose not to do so in the preceding
discussion. In some cases, averaging adjustments can lower
the worst-case intake value. In others, averaging can be used
to assure proper ventilation when either supply-fan capacity or
outdoor-air intake ﬂ ow varies.
Adjustments for short-term conditions can help the
designer ﬁ nd the appropriate worst-case minimum intake
ﬂ ow. Having found this value, the system can be designed
to maintain this intake airﬂ ow during all occupied hours. In
VAV systems, where both primary airﬂ ow and mixing-box
pressure change in response to zone demands for cooling,
this usually requires some means for sensing intake airﬂ ow
and modulating the outdoor-air damper to maintain the
minimum airﬂ ow setting.
But, do we really need to treat the worst-case outdoor
airﬂ ow at all operating conditions, without regard to current
ventilation needs? No.

In multiple-zone recirculating systems,
system ventilation efficiency almost
always increases as primary fan airﬂ ow
decreases—provided, of course, that de-
sign efﬁ ciency is properly calculated at
the worst-case condition (that is, with low
primary airﬂ ow to the critical zone).
Although we must design the system
with sufﬁ cient capacity for worst-case
intake airﬂ ow, we could operate it at many
conditions with less-than-worst-case
intake and still comply with Addendum
62n. To do so, our design could incorpo-
rate one of the optional “dynamic reset”
approaches presented in Addendum 62n,
using a control approach that resets intake
airﬂ ow to match current requirements at
part-cooling load.
In a future article, we’ll examine part-
load operation and optional dynamic reset
in detail. For now, we simply note we
always must design for worst-case intake
ﬂ ow (as discussed earlier), regardless
of any “dynamic reset” control options
we may choose to implement. In other
words, dynamic reset does not alter the
worst-case outdoor-air intake ﬂ ow needed
to comply with the standard.
Summary
Historically, Standard 62 required both
zone- and system-level calculations for
the design of single-path, multiple-zone
ventilation systems (like throttling VAV
systems). Unfortunately, the calculation
procedures were unclear and frequently
misinterpreted or ignored by designers.
As a result, many multiple-zone systems
were improperly ventilated.
Addendum 62n clariﬁ es the multiple-
zone system calculations to reduce both
underventilation and unnecessary over-
ventilation. It allows a simple “default”
approach, as well as a more accurate
“calculated” approach for determining
system ventilation efﬁ ciency.
As shown here, either calculation
procedure can be readily applied to
single-path VAV systems at the design
conditions for both cooling and heating,
to provide a compliant determination
of worst-case minimum outdoor-air
intake ﬂ ow.
References
1. ANSI/ASHRAE Standard 62-2001,
Ventilation for Acceptable Indoor Air
Quality.
2. ANSI/ASHRAE Addendum n to
ANSI/ASHRAE Standard 62-2001.
3. Stanke, D. 2004. “Addendum 62n:
single-zone and dedicated-OA systems.”
ASHRAE Journal 46(10): 12–21.
4. Stanke, D. 2004. “Standard 62-2001
Addendum 62n: ventilation for change-
over-bypass VAV systems.” ASHRAE
Journal 46(11):22–32.

January 2005
By Terry Brennan and Harriet Burge, Ph.D.
M
old colonizes buildings when a spore ﬁ nds the coincidence of
moisture, nutrient and shelter. Susan Doll1 conducted a series
of experiments at the Harvard School of Public Health demonstrating,
of experiments at the Harvard School of Public Health demonstrating
among other things, that building materials from the retail supplier
arrive already inoculated with mold spores. Other portions of the
research support the idea that mold growth can be expected in shel-
tered locations where materials containing carbon (especially sugars
and starches) are exposed to liquid water for several days or relative
humidity levels of 95% for several weeks.
build-
and dirt. A number of locations in b
er are
ings exist where both food and wate
stored
unavoidably present—drain traps, s
s, etc.
food, soap covered grout in showers
These problems are controlled by routine
These problems are controlled by ro
housekeeping and are not the concern of
this article. Rather, we address problem
mold growth that results from inadvertent
water intrusion or chronic humidity levels
close to saturation.
It is best to design, construct and
operate buildings so that problem mold
growth is avoided. Mold that doesn’t
grow is the easiest sort to deal with. The
Materials commonly found in build-
ings that provide nutrient for mold growth
include paper products, composite
wooden materials, wooden laminates,
lumber, adhesives, plant based ﬁ bers,
food, and many particles found in dust
About the Authors
Terry Brennan is a building scientist with
Camroden Associates in Westmoreland, N.Y.
Harriet Burge, Ph.D., is adjunct senior lecturer
on environmental microbiology, Department of
Environmental Health, at the Harvard School of
Public Health, Boston.
The following article was published in ASHRAE Journal, January 2005. © Copyright 2005 American Society of Heating,
Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers, Inc. It is presented for educational purposes only. This article may not be
copied and/or distributed electronically or in paper form without permission of ASHRAE.
January 2005
ASHRAE Journal’s Official Product & Show Guide
S159
following three actions will make a building resistant to mold
growth:
• Keep mold food dry (manage the rain and plumbing water,
surface temperatures and humidity);
• Make items that will get wet out of materials that are not
mold food and are easy to clean (e.g., ceramic tile, stainless
steel, enameled steel, porcelain china, glass, glass ﬁ ber com-
posites); and
• Routinely inspect mold food that might get wet, especially
hidden mold food (rats, bats and roaches do not complain about
mold growth).
A document containing details for implementing these strat-
egies in residential buildings, Read This Before You Design,
Build or Renovate,2 can be downloaded at no charge. It’s often
far easier and less expensive to prevent moisture problems
and mold growth than to retroﬁ t moisture control and clean
up contamination.
In the last eight years, several guidance documents for the
assessment and remediation of mold problems have been pub-
lished.3–12 With the exception of the The Institute of Inspection,
Cleaning and Restoration Certiﬁ cation (IICRC) S520 standard
for remediation of mold, comparative summaries of these docu-
ments can be found in the report of the National Academy of
Sciences (NAS) Committee on Dampness and Health in Build-
ings.13 The S520 standard, arguably the most comprehensive
and practitioner-oriented guidelines, had not been published
at the time the IOM report was completed. The following list
of actions is required at some level when responding to mold
contamination in buildings:
• Take emergency actions to stop water if needed (e.g., turn
off the water line6);
• Identify the extent and location of water intrusion, the mois-
ture dynamic and nutrient source supporting mold growth;
• Plan remediation using appropriate guidance (e.g., those
listed above);
• Establish containment as needed and plan for worker pro-
tection during remediation;
• Control the moisture so that it does not recur and dry the
materials;
• Remove the mold;
• Pass clearance inspection; and
• Reassemble components in a way that makes regrowth
unlikely.
Sometimes these steps are easy to apply. Perhaps, the water
source is a leaky supply line to a toilet and can be stopped
quickly by turning a valve. Maybe the mold is a dinner plate-
sized patch on a ceiling tile resulting from a transient leak.
Most of the remediation guidance suggests vacating the room.
An ordinary worker wearing gloves and an N-95 respirator then
may gently remove the tile, place it in a garbage bag, seal the
bag and remove it from the building.
Sometimes responding is difﬁ cult and tedious. For example,
the building may smell of mold but none is readily visible. Mold
growth may occur inside a wall cavity, in a crawlspace, on the
bottom of a dresser, or behind a bed headboard. Removal may
require demolition. Protecting workers, occupants and pass-
ersby may require containment and personal protection similar
to lead paint or asbestos abatement procedures.
Obvious mold growth on painted gypsum board.
S160
January 2005
Technical Feature
Mold growth hidden in a wall cavity.
\* (°F – 32) ÷ 1.8 = °C; (pounds of water per pound × 453.6) ÷ 0.4536 = g/kg
Water-Focused Mold Assessment
The most effective and straightforward way to ﬁ nd mold is
to seek microclimates where mold is likely to grow, that is,
where water and nutrients co-exist. Look in the places water
problems are most likely to appear to see whether mold there.
A water-focused mold inspection is intended to answer the
important questions:
• Where is the water damage and mold growth?
• How extensive is the problem?
• How is water getting in?
• What is the nutrient source?
and
• Will remediation cause a huge
release of mold-related air contami-
nants?
The answers to these questions
can be used to develop a remediation
plan. Coincidentally, a water-focused
investigation will provide many of the
clues needed to identify the moisture
dynamic and nutrient issue underly-
ing the problem. Most of the guidance
documents do not include information on resolving moisture
issues (the IICRC, International Society of Indoor Air Quality
and Climate [ISIAQ] and American Conference of Govern-
mental Industrial Hygienists [ACGIH] documents provide the
most information along these lines).
A water-focused mold investigation includes these steps:
• Talk to people;
• Seek out the combination of water and nutrient;
• Look for obvious mold;
• Look harder. Move stuff, open doors and hatches, make
holes; and
• Take measurements and perhaps collect samples.
Talk to the occupants. Listen to the occupants. They are a
source of clues. They may know that the building always feels
cold and clammy; that rainwater
comes in under the west wall when
it rains heavily and there is a west
wind; that the rec room ﬂ oor sweats
if the windows are open in August;
that the ﬁ rst ﬂ oor smells musty but
the upper ﬂ oors do not; that there
was a rainwater ﬂ ood a year ago. It’s
okay to listen to health complaints,
but not to give health-related advice.
People with health symptoms should
see medical professionals.
Look for obvious mold, wet mate-
rials and evidence of water damage:
damp materials, stains, peeling paint, and efﬂ orescence. Look in
the places that commonly get wet such as attics, roofs, dormers,
ﬂ ashing, windows, skylights, doors and the foundation. Trace
the water lines: both the supply side and the drain system.
Check places that get unavoidably and routinely wet—bath-
rooms and kitchens—carefully. Look for areas of condensa-
In most cases direct examination provides the most
useful information with the least amount of effort and un-
certainty. Measurement of moisture-related variables can
be very useful in understanding moisture dynamics and
determining whether or not a material is dry. Surface and
air sampling can be used to determine whether a surface
is contaminated with mold spores or hyphae and can be
helpful in determining whether there is mold hidden behind
furnishings or in cavities.
Moisture-Related Measurements
Moisture meters are useful for determining whether or not
a material is wet and for back tracing water to its source.
Moisture meters are available that are calibrated for wooden
and concrete materials. They may be intrusive, depending
on penetrating the material with needles or non-destructive,
only penetrating the material with electromagnetic ﬁ elds. A
number of nondestructive meters have several scales that
provide relative readings for a number of materials. These
scales are useful for tracing water pathways.
Air temperature and relative humidity measurements
can track sources of water vapor. For example, imagine a
building during heating conditions that is experiencing mold
growth on the ﬁ rst ﬂ oor. Outdoor air is 25°F\* and 50% relative
Sampling Useful in Assessing Mold Growth
humidity. The ﬁ rst ﬂ oor air is 72°F and 42% RH. The basement
air is 65°F and 45% RH. There is 0.00138 pounds of water
per pound\* of dry air and the dewpoint is 8°F. On the ﬁ rst
ﬂ oor there is 0.00686 pounds of water per pound of dry air
and the dewpoint is 48°F. In the basement there is 0.00577
pounds of water per pound of dry air and the dewpoint is
43 degrees F. From this information it can be induced that
there are sources of water vapor in the basement, but not
enough to account for the water vapor levels on the ﬁ rst
ﬂ oor. There must be additional water vapor sources on the
ﬁ rst ﬂ oor. The surfaces growing mold on the ﬁ rst ﬂ oor are
likely to be near 48°F.
Mold-Related Methods
This article is focused on methods useful for determin-
ing whether or not a material is contaminated by mold. For
information on sampling to characterize the bioaerosol in a
building see the ACGIH Bioaerosol Handbook.
Surface samples may be useful during a mold assessment
of a building to determine whether a surface is contaminated
by mold or not. Surfaces can be assessed by direct micro-
scopic examination of the surface using a stand microscope.
Samples may be collected from a surface using clear tape
or sterile swabs. The tape may be mounted and examined
January 2005
ASHRAE Journal’s Official Product & Show Guide
S161
Technical Feature
A hole saw vibrates materials less than a hand saw
or recipricating saw. This one is equipped with a
homemade shroud.
tion—sources of humidity and chilled surfaces. Look at showers,
damp foundations, pools, spas and brick or masonry claddings.
Check air-conditioning equipment, foundation materials in con-
tact with soil, parts of the enclosure that may be exposed to cold
outdoor air, night sky radiation losses and cold air-conditioned
indoor air. Check places that take a long time to dry once they do
get wet; places that are covered (double vapor barriers, beneath
baseboards), isolated or made of materials that hold water like a
sponge (gypsum board, insulation, fabric, concrete, wood).
Inspect the exposed surfaces ﬁ rst—walls, ceilings, ﬂ oors.
Sketch the location and size of mold using simple ﬂ oor plans
and elevations. Photographs and video provide very useful
ways of documenting conditions before remediation. A bright
light is critical for ﬁ nding mold on surfaces. Sometimes mold
that is barely visible becomes obvious when a light is shone
across it. Once the visible mold has been located begin the
search for hidden mold.
In addition to direct observation, moisture meters and humidity
ratio (pounds of water per pound of dry air) measurements can
help to ﬁ nd moisture problems. Moisture meters are useful for
tracing water sources and ﬁ nding hidden moisture. Temperature
and relative humidity measurements can be used to calculate
humidity ratios. Comparing the humidity ratio of outdoor air,
basement, and rooms can help locate sources of humidity.
Sometimes unaided visual examination of a surface will not
reveal whether a stain is mold growth or not. For example,
microscopically. Swabs samples can be cultured using direct
plating or dilution techniques.
The authors have found that directly examining surfaces
using stand microscopes is a very useful ﬁ eld method.
Stand microscopes originate in the graphic arts and printing
industry. They have a built-in stand that holds the objective
lens above a surface. Small battery powered illuminators
provide reﬂ ected light for direct observation. Stand micro-
scopes are available from 20X to 300X. As magniﬁ cation
increases, depth of ﬁ eld decreases. The most useful ranges
for examining surfaces to determine whether there is mold
growth are in the 100X to 200X range.
Stand microscopes allow quick examination of numerous
surfaces in the ﬁ eld. It is relatively easy to distinguish fungal
growth from other deposits. Sometimes organisms can be
identiﬁ ed to genus level by the habit of growth.
For example, fan-like Penicillium spore bearing structures
often can be distinguished from the spherical heads char-
acteristic of Aspergillus. Stand microscopes do not provide
enough power to identify organisms easily by examining
spores, or indeed to distinguish a 2 micron spore from a
2 micron bit of dust from sanding joint compound. Using
stand microscopes for ﬁ eld work in assessing mold is a new
endeavor—there are as yet no training programs for doing
it. Competency is developed by examining deposits with
the stand microscope and examining collected samples by
transmitted light microscopy. To that end training in foren-
sic microscopy is very useful in learning to use the stand
microscope.
Tape Lifts
Clear plastic tape can be used to collect a sample of mate-
rial from a surface. The tape can be mounted on a slide and
examined microscopically using transmitted light.
Generally tape lifts are made in the following way:
• Cut a small piece of transparent cellophane tape (no
more than 1 in. [25 mm] long).
• Lightly press the sticky side on the material using a
gloved ﬁ nger or a stick (e.g., a tongue depressor).
• Place the sample sticky side down on a microscope
slide or other clean smooth surface (e.g., inside a ziplock
bag).
For analysis:
• Mount the tape sticky (and sample) side up on a micro-
scope slide.
• Cover with dilute potassium hydroxide or other mount-
ing medium and a cover slip.
• Examine at 400X to 1,000X.
A tape lift can always tell you whether or not discoloration
is mold. Often the organism can be identiﬁ ed to genus and
sometimes to species. Tape lifts document that mold is grow-
ing on the surface sampled, and may allow identiﬁ cation of
the mold. As with any of the methods in this section, proper
use requires good training.
Sometimes unaided visual examination will not reveal whether a stain is mold
growth. The left photo is cladosporium on paint and the right photo shows hyphae
and spores on gypsum board.
S162
January 2005
Technical Feature
ceiling tile is frequently stained by water but dries before mold
grows; soot deposits on walls or ceilings may look as if they
are mold growth. Occasionally an unstained surface that ap-
pears to be clean actually is covered with mold growth that can
only be detected by augmenting the ability to see. Microscopic
examination of the suspect surface or of samples collected
from the suspect surface can reveal whether a stain is mold
or not or whether an apparently clean surface is covered with
microscopic mold. Note that most apparently clean surfaces
are not moldy. Hard-to-see mold only is expected if moisture
problems have been identiﬁ ed.
Mold often grows in sheltered places, microclimates where a
small source of water causes high humidity or doesn’t dry well
because it’s cool. Look behind or beneath:
• Furniture (especially surfaces facing cool exterior walls or
concrete ﬂ oors on grade);
• Pictures;
• Chalkboards;
• Wall coverings;
• Bookcases; and
• Floor coverings: carpets, carpet padding, vinyl ﬂ ooring.
All of these items can make an accidental cold side vapor bar-
rier during cooling conditions or may provide enough insulation
to cool a surface below dewpoint during cold weather.
If the building or portions of the building are at lower air
pressure than the outdoor air during the cooling season, con-
densation and mold growth may occur beneath vinyl or foil
wallpaper on exterior walls (or even interior walls if the wall
cavity is depressurized). Under these conditions, wallpaper
often is peeling in corners and around windows. Carefully peel
back small sections of wallpaper to examine beneath.
Below grade walls ﬁ nished on the interior may provide similar
microclimates. The space is enclosed, the foundation wall chills
the cavity and may be exhaling water vapor. Paneling and paper
covered gypsum board provide nutrient. Summertime ventilating
air wets the space. Polyethylene vapor retarders reduce drying po-
tential. A layer of foam board against the foundation wall greatly
reduces the potential for problems by warming the cavity and keep-
ing room-side water vapor from the earth-chilled foundation.
When you move things around, do so gently. It sometimes
is easy to knock hundreds of thousands of spores loose from a
patch of mold. Gently lift carpets, carefully move the chest of
drawers away from the wall. Don’t slam and bang things.
Look inside things that have access doors or panels, especially
if they have a potential moisture source:
• In closets (exterior walls, plumbing, in basements);
• In cabinets (particularly cabinets containing plumbing)
and boxes;
Air Samples
Collecting and interpreting air samples requires special-
ized knowledge. Many of the air sample interpretations the
authors have reviewed over the years draw conclusions
that cannot be defended with the sample results. This is
largely due to the complexity and dynamics of bioaero-
sols, and uncertainties in the methods. When sampling
for bioaerosols many organisms will be collected, outdoor
levels may dominate indoor levels and source terms vary
over the course of minutes, hours and days by orders
of magnitude. Interpretation of indoor samples requires
sufﬁ cient indoor and outdoor samples to account for
variability, and comparisons of individual types of fungi.
Also, the uncertainties introduced into the collection and
identiﬁ cation of samples must be understood and accom-
modated. The following section focuses on differential
interpretation and uncertainties rather than methodology.
For methods see the ACGIH
and AIHA references.
As a relatively straightforward
example, consider an outdoor
spore trap sample that contains
900 Cladosporium spores and
150 Penicillium spores per
cubic meter of air. An indoor
sample taken immediately
after this sample contains 200
Cladosporium spores and 1100
Penicillium spores per cubic
meter of air. This is evidence
that Penicillium may be or may
have been growing inside the
building. It does not help to ﬁ nd the growth, however.
Suppose a third sample taken in the crawlspace im-
mediately after the ﬁ rst two contains 700 Cladosporium
spores and 29,000 Penicillium spores per cubic meter of
air. This provides evidence of Penicillium growth in the
crawlspace and evidence, but not proof that the spores
are being transported to the upstairs
Suppose that the samples were cultured and the Penicil-
lium colonies isolated and speciated. The outdoor sample
was found to contain 150 colony forming units of Penicil-
lium pinophilum per cubic meter of air, the crawlspace
contained 29,000 Penicillium citrinum colony forming units
per cubic meter of air, while the house contained 1,100
colony forming units of Penicillium oxalicum per cubic
meter of air. Now the evidence is that Penicillium may be
growing in both the house and the crawlspace.
Mold growth in glue layer is apparent under
the stand microscope.
Glue beneath carpet has no visible mold.
January 2005
ASHRAE Journal’s Official Product & Show Guide
S163
Technical Feature
• Above ceiling tiles (beneath plumbing, baths, toilets, ﬂ at
roofs);
• In attics, crawlspaces and basements;
• In utility tunnels, trenches and chases;
• Inside wall cavities (sometimes there are access panels for
plumbing in walls or ceilings); and
• Inside ductwork and HVAC equipment.
During hot, humid times of the year, condensation on surfaces
chilled by air conditioning may lead to mold problems. Inspect
the cooling coil, the inside of air handlers, the drain pan and the
interior of ducts for water and mold growth. Look in cavities
with no planned access.
There are times when mold growth is suspected in cavities
that do not have access doors or panels—wall and ceilings cavi-
ties, utility chases and trenches, large portions of ductwork and
air handlers (many residential central air-conditioning coils do
not have access for inspection and cleaning), crawlspaces and
attics. A cavity may seem suspicious if:
• A room smells musty and mold has not been discovered
during the previous steps.
• Snifﬁ ng electrical outlets reveals a musty odor (note: if air
is entering the room from the cavity through the outlet this is
a good test; if air is leaving the room through electrical outlets
this is a poor test).
• Evidence exists that the cavity is wet (peeling paint, joints
in wooden materials have expanded and contracted, water stains,
high moisture meter readings).
There are a number of ways to assess the presence of mold
hidden in these locations. The most direct is to make an opening
that provides visual access. Spore samples collected in room
air or from inside cavities (e.g., through electrical outlets or
small holes made by the inspector) may provide evidence of
hidden mold. Analysis of air samples for VOCs associated with
the presence of mold has been experimented with as a way of
locating hidden mold.
Making openings into hidden places is the most direct method
of detecting mold growth. Doing so may release many mold
spores by vibrating materials where mold is growing and by
providing much larger openings for airﬂ ow from the cavity into
the building. Minimize vibration and be prepared to quickly
reseal the opening. See the precautions listed later.
The following factors increase the chances that mold will be
found growing in a cavity:
• Cavities with water or drain lines;
• A cavity beneath the corner of a window or door;
• Cavities near skylights;
• Cavities with wooden or paper products in contact with
concrete foundation materials;
• Cavities with moisture meter readings indicating elevated
moisture content;
• Cavities behind interior or exterior walls with stains, peel-
ing paint or efﬂ orescence;
• Cavities with carpenter ant or termite nests;
• Cavities where the exterior is exposed to heavy wind-
driven rain;
• Cavities with complex, defective or missing ﬂ ashing detail
on exterior; and
• Cavities beneath baseboards (where water ends up by grav-
ity and is likely to remain wet).
Holes provide a direct look into a cavity, so hidden mold and
moisture becomes visible. They provide observation of exact
construction details. They provide access for using boroscopes
for deeper inspection. And last but not least, they expose the
backside of the interior ﬁ nish materials (e.g., gypsum board
and paneling) for inspection.
Make Holes Safely
It is unpredictable how much mold will be found inside a cavity.
Be prepared to reseal holes quickly and effectively. Use low im-
pact tools like utility knives and hole saws to make holes. Collect
dust using HEPA vacuums. If tools that create a lot of dust are
used—reciprocating jig saws, blitz saws, Rotozip tools—use them
in containment. Some tools have collection shrouds that can be
used with HEPA vacuum cleaners to dramatically reduce dust.
Making Quick Containments
If a signiﬁ cant hole must be made, it is a good idea to make
containment. Containment can be quickly established by using
spring loaded poles and polyethylene ﬁ lm or plastic coated nylon
sheets. It is handy to be able to quickly make containment for a
small section of ceiling, ﬂ oor or wall. If added protection is desired
a containment can be depressurized using a HEPA vacuum.
Close it Up, Clean it Up
Holes must be resealed after the inspection is complete. It
is best to make neat holes and to reseal with the material that
came out of the hole. Backing to reattach the removed piece can
be made by inserting small pieces of plywood or lumber and
screwing them in place. The piece can be sealed using taping
compound. Holes made through walls behind baseboards are
easy to repair. Nailed baseboard can be removed by driving the
ﬁ nish nails through the wall with a drift pin or punch. Plastic
cove baseboard attached with adhesive often can be removed
using a thin pry bar (of the type used by beekeepers). A hole
saw can make a clean opening. A HEPA vacuum collects the
particles. The hole is resealed with the removed plug. Use a
HEPA vacuum to clean all the exposed surfaces around and
below the opening. Seal the gaps with joint compound and
reattach the baseboard. Leave no dirt or debris behind.
The Report
The purpose of the investigation and report is to establish how
much mold is present, sources of moisture and recommenda-
tions for remediation. The report should include:
• A description of the investigation including observations
of moisture source and dynamics, visible mold growth, signs
S164
January 2005
Technical Feature
of water damage and the results of the
hidden mold inspection;
• Results of the occupant interviews;
• Drawings showing where moisture,
mold growth or both were found;
• Recommendations for containment,
clean-up and worker protection;
• Speciﬁ c directions for achieving de-
contamination and moisture intervention;
• Clearance criteria; and
• Results and speciﬁ c interpretation of
sample analysis.
One of the difﬁ culties investigating
and mending mold problems is that the
knowledge needed cuts across many
ﬁ elds. The antidote is continued learning
and ﬁ nding people who have a depth of
knowledge in your weak areas.
It always seems to take longer than
you’d think to write a useful—not neces-
sarily long—report. In fact the shorter a
report while still conveying the important
information identifying the problems and
remedies, the better.
References
1. Doll, S.C. 2002. Determination of Limit-
ing Conditions for Fungal Growth in the Built
Environment. Doctoral Dissertation, Harvard
School of Public Health, Boston, MA.
2. Lstiburek, J. 2004. Read This Before You
Design, Build or Renovate. Building Science
Corporation. www.buildingscience.com/
resources/mold/read\_this\_before\_you\_de-
sign\_build\_or\_renovate.
3. ACGIH. 1999: “Bioaerosols—assess-
ment and control.” American Conference of
Governmental Industrial Hygienists, Cincin-
nati, Ohio.
4. AIHA. 2001. “Report of the microbial
task force.” American Industrial Hygiene
Association. AIHA Press, Fairfax, VA.
5. Health Canada. 1995. “Fungal contami-
nation in buildings: a guide to recognition
and management.” Health Canada, Ottawa,
ON, Canada.
6. IICRC. 1999. Standard and Refer-
ence Guide for Professional Water Damage
Restoration S500. Institute of Inspection,
Cleaning and Restoration Certification,
Vancouver, Wash.
7. IICRC. 2003. Standard and Reference
Guide for Professional Mold Remediation
S520. Institute of Inspection, Cleaning and
Restoration Certiﬁ cation, Vancouver, Wash.
8. ISIAQ. 1996. Control of Moisture Prob-
lems Affecting Biological Indoor Air Quality.
International Society of Indoor Air Quality
and Climate.
9. MNDOH/ 2001. Recommended Best
Practices for Mold Investigations In Min-
nesota Schools. Minnesota Department of
Health.
10. NYCDOH. 2000. Guidelines on As-
sessment and Remediation of Fungi in Indoor
Environments. New York City Department
of Health.
11. EPA. 2001. Mold Remediation in
Schools and Commercial Buildings. United
States Environmental Protection Agency,
Washington D.C.
12. OSHA. 2003. A brief guide to mold in
the workplace.
13. IOM. 2004. Damp Indoor Spaces and
Health. Committee on Damp Indoor Spaces
and Health; Institute of Medicine, National
Academies Press.

About the Author
Todd Lash is product manager for Siemens Building
Technologies in Buffalo Grove, Ill.
Operations and Maintenance
Tapping into Remote
Building Intelligence
By Todd Lash
merging technologies and services are enabling engineers to tap
intellectual property outside their organization, remotely and
affordably, and add signiﬁcant value to their processes and bottom
lines. A new trend in remote facility monitoring and analysis is gaining
wider acceptance among building owners, managers and engineers
throughout the U.S. as they stretch their already-thin resources and
budgets to new limits.1
A form of “remote intelligence” is
garnering particular attention among
facility professionals. Customized ser-
vices provide continuous commission-
ing to HVAC equipment that can reduce
energy consumption, and prioritize and
track equipment maintenance issues.
Some call it remote building solutions,
or ongoing performance monitoring and
improvement, while others give it such
names as remote monitoring and analy-
sis, networked building systems.
The Federal Energy Management Pro-
gram (FEMP) calls it Continuous Com-
missioningSM (CCSM), deﬁned as “a com-
prehensive and ongoing process to resolve
operating problems, improve comfort,
optimize energy use, and identify retroﬁts
for existing commercial and institutional
buildings and central plant facilities. Ac-
cording to FEMP, CCSM has produced
typical savings of 20% with payback under
three years (often one to two years) in
more than 130 large buildings.”2
FEMP is clear about its process going
beyond traditional operations and main-
tenance (O&M) programs by including
a comprehensive engineering evaluation
and an integrated approach that ensures
local and global system optimization and
the persistence of improved schedules.3
No matter what the service is called
or how it is contracted, one common
denominator exists: facility performance
data are gathered or “mined” continu-
ously from a building automation system
(BAS) using open protocols and software
algorithms, then downloaded to a large,
remote database. The data are analyzed us-
ing a combination of artiﬁcial intelligence
and advanced diagnostic methods man-
aged by experienced, off-site energy and
mechanical engineering professionals. The
service uses proprietary applications, in-
dustry benchmark comparisons and rec-
ommended ASHRAE/National Institute of
E
The following article was published in ASHRAE Journal, March 2005. © Copyright 2005 American Society of
Heating, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers, Inc. It is presented for educational purposes only. This
article may not be copied and/or distributed electronically or in paper form without permission of ASHRAE.
March 2005
ASHRAE Journal
63
Standards and Technology (NIST) methods to expand the BAS
capabilities for the end user.
As a generic description for this article, we will call it simply
RBAO (remote building analysis and optimization).
A key component of RBAO is the intelligence gathered
during a facility performance assessment. This comprises an
initial site survey reinforced with ongoing analysis, tailored to
the user’s speciﬁc needs along with a quantiﬁable and tracked
improvement plan.
Remote Origins
The concept of RBAO was inspired by the shortcomings of
packaged software products that tried to meet advanced ap-
plication needs. While packaged products can collect data and
provide reports, signiﬁcant technical and cost barriers exist to
a “shrink-wrapped” software solution that can combine user
needs, custom facility operational and mechanical informa-
tion, and data for meaningful and actionable improvement
and diagnostics. Most packaged software still requires on-site
expertise and time to interpret their outputs.
Over the past ﬁve years, the need for remote intelligence
was further driven by the owners’ desire to get more value
for the dollar from their systems by moving them into more
customizable conﬁgurations that offer greater ﬂexibility and
opportunities for performance improvement.4
Over the next ﬁve years, advancements in networking, ap-
plications, interfacing and the Internet will be the primary
drivers for change in the BAS industry. These changes will
become enablers for signiﬁcant new product developments in
HVAC control. Engineers will be moving toward Web-based
and networked solutions that can create new levels of efﬁ-
ciency in their automation systems and leverage their current
infrastructure investments. Additionally, the next ﬁve years
will most likely drive the BAS industry into a technolgical
catch-up mode as demand intensiﬁes.
RBAO promises to deliver a much-needed bridge between
the value of data sufﬁciency and the resident, but usually
untapped, capabilities of the BAS. Heretofore, the BAS has
been used primarily to generate basic reports that have been
interpreted by the user for problem diagnostics. Now, owners
can better leverage their investments in their automation sys-
tems and their infrastructure by reducing energy and operating
costs, boosting equipment and manpower efﬁciency, avoiding
major costs and increasing asset values. Fault diagnostics de-
Figure 1: Typical delivery process of remote building analysis and optimization.
64
ASHRAE Journal ashrae.org
March 2005
tection, energy conservation, and
continuous commissioning and
improvement are the goals.
Once the experts uncover the
problems and opportunities re-
motely, they can make speciﬁc rec-
ommendations for improvements.
Additionally, remote intel-
ligence provides engineers with
the “vision” to see the longer-term
problems that set in from season
to season and from year to year, as
the facility goes through different
cycles. They can see more clearly
how the mechanical systems truly
behave over time.
For example, the most common
phenomena that the RBAO ana-
lysts look for are wasteful condi-
tions or where equipment is not
being used to optimum efﬁciency.
But more importantly, they look
for issues that relate more to the optimal design and operation
of the building, not simply the control system.
Emerging remote services are moving well beyond alarm
response and troubleshooting. They detect broader trends
such as ﬂaws within current operation, opportunities for more
optimal operation and key information used to make better
design decisions in the future.
One RBAO customer found an immediate cost savings of
more than $20,000 a year by correcting a leaking valve and
some simultaneous heating and cooling. Midterm beneﬁts
for the same customer totaled $44,000 a year—achieved by
optimizing the staging of his plant to avoid repeating several
costly design mistakes that were found in his air-handling units.
Using the RBAO reports as a guide, the customer was able to
plan future building projects on campus.
Once experts can assess performance and uncover problems
and opportunities remotely, they can make speciﬁc recommen-
dations for improvements and/or changes in operation.
The Value of Intelligence
Applying sophisticated analytics to the mining and evalua-
tion of facility performance data can pay signiﬁcant dividends.
Certainly, lower energy costs due to improved control and
performance optimization top the list and attract the most at-
tention from building owners and engineers. However, many
other beneﬁts exist of equal or greater value.
Most users will realize signiﬁcantly lower operating and
maintenance costs due to more efﬁcient allocation and deploy-
ment— and shorter durations—of on-site manpower resources,
which often results in the reduction of overtime expenses.
Operations and Maintenance
Figure 2: Key components of the advanced data analysis stage.
Here is a partial list of examples of the types of problems and
opportunities that the RBAO process can uncover and/or assess:
Optimal Plant Staging,
Global Plant/AHU Optimization,
Plant Efﬁciency Declines/Predictive Maintenance,
Optimal Plant Scheduling,
Indoor Air Quality Issues,
Optimal Mixed Air Control,
Demand Control Ventilation,
Ventilation Code Compliance,
Optimal Setpoints and Setpoint Tracking,
Optimal VAV Control, Including VAV Plus Variable Pressure,
Energy Benchmarking,
Peer Benchmarking,
Enterprise Information Rollups and Analysis,
Equipment Lifecycle Analysis,
Optimal Scheduling and Tracking,
Optimal Boiler Staging and Preheat,
Degree Day Baselines,
Comfort/Productivity,
Valve Fault Detection,
Loop Performance, and
Optimal Start/Stop Analysis.
Remote Intelligence in Action
March 2005
ASHRAE Journal
65
Because the goal of RBAO is to operate equipment as close to
optimal conditions as possible, repair and replacement costs will
be reduced and malfunctions can be diagnosed and remedied
to preclude failure. Equally important, the longevity of most
mechanical equipment can be extended substantially. Although
RBAO-type services are relatively new on the HVAC scene and
do not yet offer long-term operating data, extended life cycle
estimates can be developed for budgeting and management
approval purposes.
Further, since the collection of information and delivery of
certain services can be managed effectively via the Internet and
intranets, integration with existing information technology (IT)
systems becomes simpler and more cost effective.
Finally, through improved control and a more consistently
maintained environment, users also should realize increased
levels of indoor occupant comfort and satisfaction, with re-
duced risk of air quality problems.
Applying sophisticated analytics to the mining and evaluation
of facility performance data can pay signiﬁcant dividends.
Most RBAO programs provide engineers and building
managers with a consolidated (usually monthly) report that
serves as an important management tool for tracking results.
Typically, details on energy consumption, use patterns, trends,
and comparisons of actual vs. budget vs. baseline are included.
The larger role of the report, however, is to provide data in-
terpretation and action plans, not simply depict performance
charts and graphs.
How the Process Works
Figure 1 provides a diagram of the steps involved in a typical
RBAO process and illustrates how survey, static, dynamic data
are integrated into the analysis.
The ﬁrst step is site assessment and qualiﬁcation of the facility
to ensure that remote intelligence makes sense. This phase involves
a thorough needs analysis and a comprehensive assessment of all
facilities, including gathering all relevant, existing performance
data and site histories. In addition, the assessment must ensure
that the BAS at the facility has the resident tools and the advanced
capabilities to facilitate the complex tasks of mining, compiling,
transmitting and monitoring large amounts of real-time data.
This survey differs from traditional models, in that standard
energy audits and retro-commissioning surveys usually take a
short-term, static view of a building and recommend changes and
modiﬁcations that should be pursued. Once the survey is complete,
any changes to building operation (overrides, change in use/occu-
pancy, etc.) and changes to equipment (failures, excessive cycling,
suboptimal operation, etc.) will no longer be reﬂected.
Once the survey is complete, a road map or master plan can
be crafted to pave the way for improvements and for continuous
monitoring and commissioning. The plan must detail all of the
technical and procedural changes in the form of recommenda-
tions to optimize the value of the remote intelligence.
The next phase is establishing non-intrusive, secure BACnet
connectivity between the user’s BAS and the remote database
to mine and transmit the facility’s data on an ongoing basis.
This step can be performed by the local BAS service ofﬁce.
The process involves installation and conﬁguration of BACnet
routing devices as gateways (when the BAS platform is non-
native) that will provide the communications interface for
continuous, real-time data. Security and ﬁrewall issues with the
organization’s corporate network usually can be addressed by
using a ﬁrewall-friendly remote communications device.
BACnet technology has grown in appeal for this type of
application because it enables remote systems to connect to a
variety of different control systems that often vary in technol-
ogy, capability, age and conﬁguration.
Although each building is unique, the functions that are com-
monly targeted for monitoring include chiller and boiler plants,
chilled and hot water distribution, air handlers, exhaust fans,
VAV boxes, and ﬂow and utility meters. (See sidebar, “Remote
Intelligence in Action.”)
Once connectivity is established, information is transmitted
on an ongoing basis to a secure, off-site analytical lab where
it is captured, stored and analyzed. This is the place where en-
gineers avail themselves to the service’s intellectual property.
The goal of RBAO analysis is to uncover faults, diagnostics and
value—through the eyes of expert, unbiased professionals—that
cannot otherwise be reasonably detected or uncovered from a
one-shot survey of a building or addressed with an off-the-shelf
software product.
An RBAO analysis should provide users with a key beneﬁt:
a timely management report (usually monthly) with recom-
mendations. Ongoing recommendations are based on analyses
and observations made during the continuous monitoring and
evaluative processes. An effective analysis must take both static
and dynamic factors into account for continuously commis-
sioned buildings. Figure 2 illustrates the RBAO data analysis
and reporting process.
Following implementation of the immediate recommenda-
tions, the monthly reports should indicate the facility’s target
energy consumption and operational characteristics. Variances
from predicted results should be analyzed, problems identiﬁed
and appropriate measures recommended as needed.
Hospital Example
As an example of how an analysis might work, let’s use a
series of variable frequency drives (VFDs) in a hospital wing.
The RBAO service has been monitoring 18 points to determine
efﬁciency of both fan energy and cooling energy. The VFD
signals were used to determine the fan energy loss based on
66
ASHRAE Journal ashrae.org
March 2005
the afﬁnity law where Hp is a cubic relation to speed. A 10%
VFD loss is factored into the equation.
In this example, experts spot the potential for signiﬁcant
savings that can be derived from altering operation of a supply
fan and a return fan, and from the reduction of excess cooling.
One kW/ton is used to convert BTUs of cooling to kW. Using
$0.065/kWh, the analysis reveals that a total of approximately
$473 of excess air-handling unit (AHU) operations occurred
during one summer month. Over an eight-month cooling period,
that would amount to $3,788. Additional savings would occur
during the heating season.
Taking this example further, the experts also spot a potential
problem in a different section of the hospital. Being monitored
are the operating characteristics of one of the main AHUs.
Experts notice that on some hot summer days, the discharge
air temperature setpoint varies from 55°F up to 58°F (13°C to
14°C). This, in turn, causes the chilled water valve signal to
experience wide swings to maintain the correct discharge air
temperature. More revealing is that similar trends also appear
on other AHUs in other hospital sections.
The report concludes that these wide swings in temperature
setpoints (and corresponding actuator and valve oscillations)
Operations and Maintenance
can lead to premature equipment failure, system inefﬁciencies
and comfort issues. In this case, the recommendations are to
investigate each AHU control strategy to determine the source
of the wide swings in the discharge temperature setpoints and
to make the appropriate software or hardware adjustments.
A useful report should also prioritize all of the recommenda-
tions so the user understands which measures or changes require
immediate attention, which issues should be addressed in the
short or near term, and which issues require future attention.
In the hospital example earlier, the report identiﬁed one of
the problems as a control issue that could be easily corrected,
while the other problem uncovered a design ﬂaw that required
a change to the AHU. The staff was able to use this new intel-
ligence in the design of a new hospital wing to direct future
AHU mechanical designs and prevent such incidents from
recurring. The two recommendations were given a priority
rating of medium: action needed within the next 30 to 45 days
(and continue to be monitored).
Checklist for Success
Before embarking on a journey into RBAO services, the fol-
lowing considerations should be taken into account.
Statistics show that existing college buildings that are commis-
sioned and stay commissioned consume an average of 15% less
energy than non-commissioned buildings (according to the Cali-
fornia Commissioning Market Characterization Study).
American University in Washington, D.C., was convinced of these
statistics when it decided to try a new remote building analysis and
optimization (RBAO) program for three of its buildings in early 2004.
The service—a process of facility data acquisition, remote expert
analysis and reporting—gives building operations personnel unbi-
ased recommendations and management information that enables
them to reduce energy and operational costs while enjoying the
many beneﬁts of continuously commissioned buildings.
The program assists systems’ engineers with fault detection,
troubleshooting and problem solving while prioritizing maintenance
issues and reducing downtime. The overall goals, in addition to cost
reduction, are to improve facility operation and efﬁciency, increase
comfort and reduce risk of indoor air quality issues.
The university operates 50 buildings across its 84-acre campus
in the nation’s capital. A building automation system (BAS) moni-
tors a total of 4,850 points on campus, with 3,000 of those points
currently assigned to the RBAO service.
Director of physical plant operations, Willy Suter, admits he and
his staff have their hands full with the number of operational and
maintenance solutions that the service already has presented. But
he is grateful to have an outside intelligence source that can spot
troubles before they become failures.
“We’re often so busy handling day-to-day symptoms,” he ex-
plains, “that we don’t have time to dig deeper into the root causes
of many of the problems. The RBAO service enables us to see the
larger picture of how our facilities really behave and what we really
should be doing to address the larger issues.”
Harry Thompson, assistant director of building maintenance
operations, is responding to as many of the recommendations as
possible in the monthly reports. “The service has enabled us to
identify what our real maintenance backlog is (for the three build-
ings) and to understand how we can get more productivity out of
our BAS. Unless we increase our staff by 24 people, we simply
don’t have the time to dig up all the information and perform the
type of analysis that the remote intelligence service is able to
provide in just a 30-day period.”
Thompson says that the RBAO monthly reports have identiﬁed
a range of HVAC issues involving software and hardware. “The
reports let us know, for example, if CO2 sensors are accurately
sensing the conditions of occupied spaces and adjusting the
outside air dampers appropriately to keep the CO2 levels within
the speciﬁcations. We know if the discharge temps at the AHUs
are adequate to provide the needed setpoints for the spaces. The
reports give us indications about energy consumption based on
runtimes. The reports even evaluate the efﬁciency of the chillers
in reference to such factors as water temperature, which gives us
clues to other related issues.”
Although it is too soon for quantitative results, the RBAO
program estimates that the university will save more than $70,000
per year in energy alone. However, Suter sees additional beneﬁts:
“We already have qualitative improvements in many spaces
on campus where we previously had temperature problems
that couldn’t be resolved. The reports have enabled us to make
relatively minor repairs in some cases, which has greatly reduced
temperature-related complaint calls. As a result, we’ve experienced
a net reduction in labor cost. At the same time, we’ve improved
occupant satisfaction considerably. It’s hard to put a number
on that.”
D.C. University Taps New HVAC Intelligence Source
March 2005
ASHRAE Journal
67
1. For new or retroﬁt facilities, be sure the building’s design
has adequate instrumentation to accommodate the latest tech-
nologies. Try not to cost-minimize when it comes to building
control and monitoring; the downside could be greater—or
worse, prohibitive—costs down the road.
2. Plan for open-system technology. Ensure that the BAS pro-
vider supports BACnet® technology at the top of their architec-
ture. As the key access point for remote technologies, this will
ensure that remote IP can be leveraged across all systems.
3. Ensure your facility has the best information technology
(IT) infrastructure in place for your organization. For optimum
information management, you will want the remote services
and the BAS tied directly into your IT.
4. Thoroughly evaluate and assess the capabilities and ro-
bustness of your current BAS. Be sure the RBAO supplier’s
services are compatible with your system, and that your system
can handle the increased complexities.
5. Be sure the BACnet routing devices and/or gateways to
be used for gathering data and monitoring performance on a
continuous basis are ﬁrewall friendly.
6. Ensure that your organization operates with a standard
point-naming convention to minimize the cost of expanding
the remote service to various parts of the facility.
These guidelines can help you and your organization better pre-
pare for the future of intelligent buildings so that you can achieve
the highest return from your BAS investment at the lowest cost.
Notes
1. Lee, J. and J. Grifﬁth. 2003. “BACnet remote monitoring and the
bottom line.” Today’s Facility Manager February. www.facilitycity.
com/tfm/tfm\_03\_02\_news3.asp.

gov/femp/operations\_maintenance/commissioning\_guidebook.cfm.
3. “Continuous Commissioning Guidebook for Federal Managers.”

4. From “Business Case Guidebook, Enhanced Automation,”
published by the California Energy Commission. www.energy.ca.gov/
reports/2002-06-20\_400-02-005F.PDF. Covers a variety of strategies
to increase the capabilities of existing building automation systems
to control current, and plan for future, building energy costs while
maintaining the comfort and productivity of all buildings.

ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
November 2005
Rebuilding
After Katrina
W
e learn our lessons from disaster. Hurricane Andrew (1992)
taught us about wind. Hurricanes Charley, Frances and
Wt
W
Jeanne (2004) taught us about rain. The Red River of the North
Basin taught us about ﬂ oods (1997). Hurricane Katrina had it all:
wind, rain and ﬂ ood.
By Joseph W. Lstiburek, Ph.D., P.Eng., Fellow ASHRAE
That we will rebuild in the same place
is not in doubt. This is what we do—for
better or worse.
However, if we are to rebuild and we
rebuild in the same place, how should
we rebuild?
We do know how to address wind,
rain and ﬂ ood successfully. However, we
rarely address all three simultaneously.
The lesson of Hurricane Katrina on this
score is particularly harsh. Rebuilding
must address all three.
To address wind and rain, we should
look to Florida. When you are hit
by hurricanes on a regular basis,
you tend to learn a thing or two. The
Florida Building Code under-
went significant revision after Hur-
ricane Andrew. The success of those
changes was reﬂ ected during the four
hurricanes that hit Florida during August
and September 2004. No one died in
any structure built under the new code
changes. Furthermore, structural
damage due to wind was minor in
Photograph 1: New home (Charleston, S.C.). First level used for parking, storage and building access. Photograph 2: New home (Florida
Panhandle). Note the location of the HVAC unit.
About the Author
Joseph W. Lstiburek, Ph.D., P.Eng., is a prin-
cipal of Building Science Corporation in West-
ford, Mass.
© 2005, American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers, Inc. (www.ashrae.org). Reprinted by permission from
ASHRAE Journal, (Vol. 47, No. 11, November 2005). This article may not be copied nor distributed in either paper or digital form without
ASHRAE’s permission.
November 2005
ASHRAE Journal
13
buildings built under the new code
changes. And, the code is being further
revised as a result of the last hur-
ricane season. Excellent guidance is
readily available.1,2
In an irony that is best appreciated by
engineers and contractors, the success
of the code changes in Florida regarding
wind resulted in signiﬁ cant unhappiness
with rain entry in new buildings. When
your building has blown away, and you
are missing a roof and an entire façade,
you tend not to complain about rain leak-
ing through walls, windows and doors.
New Florida buildings did not blow away
and roofs and façades stayed intact dur-
ing the last hurricane season—they just
leaked rainwater.3 However, addressing
rainwater entry is rather straightfor-
ward4—but only if a decision is made
to address it. As a result of the last hur-
ricane season, control of rainwater entry
is now a Florida priority. It should be a
priority everywhere it rains.
To address ﬂ ood, we should look to
common sense:
• Elevate structures;
• Build with materials that can become
wet; and
• Design assemblies to easily dry
when they become wet.
The Federal Emergency Management
Agency (FEMA) has all of the basics
correct and has had them correct for a
long time.5,6,7
The obvious point is to build high-
er—as high as possible and practical.
In coastal regions such as Charleston,
S.C., and Florida’s panhandle, the ﬁ rst
level in new buildings typically is used
for parking, storage and building ac-
cess. Utilities, services and equipment
also are elevated (Photographs 1 and 2).
This should be the fundamental basis for
construction in high risk areas for ﬂ ood.
Further inland, elevated crawlspaces
and pier foundations can be constructed
(Figures 1 and 2). Slabs should be
avoided. If slabs are used, they should be
constructed as raised slabs (Photograph
3 and Figure 3).
Additionally, we should use non-wa-
ter-sensitive materials. Again, FEMA
has it right.8,9 Recent work has dem-
onstrated the efﬁ cacy of constructing
assemblies from water-resistant materi-
als.10 Not surprisingly, Florida leads
the way. Most commercial buildings in
Florida are constructed from masonry
and concrete (Photograph 4).
Also in Florida, in a modern adapta-
tion of the medieval practice of rustica-
tion, residential buildings typically are
constructed from masonry on the ﬁ rst
level and wood frame on the second
level (Photograph 6 and
6
Figure 3). In
medieval times the lower level was con-
structed stronger to resist the marauding
rabble. In modern times (in Florida),
the lower level is constructed to resist
marauding water.
Materials That Can Become Wet
It is obvious that the most water-
sensitive building materials in use to-
day are paper-faced gypsum board and
ﬁ berglass cavity insulation. The entire
interior of a building typically is lined
with paper-faced gypsum board. Our
shaft walls, our utility and service walls
are wrapped with paper-faced gypsum
board. And, in many commercial non-
combustible building assemblies, the
exterior also is wrapped with paper-
faced gypsum board. We are building
paper buildings.
The easy answer is don’t use paper-
faced gypsum board. Use non-paper-
faced gypsum board on the inside of
assemblies and on the exterior of non-
combustible assemblies.
The answer to ﬁ berglass cavity insula-
tion is equally obvious—don’t use it. In
commercial steel stud assemblies, do not
insulate the cavity. Instead, install the in-
sulation on the exterior of the assembly.
Then, design the cavity to be ventilated
after a moisture event to facilitate dry-
ing (Photograph 5 and Figure 4). The
Photograph 3: New home under construction (Ft. Myers, Fla.). Note the elevated slab relative to the surrounding grade and the non-water-
sensitive structure (masonry wall). Photograph 4: Commercial ofﬁ ce under construction (Orlando, Fla.). The basic structure is constructed
from completely non-water-sensitive materials.
14
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
November 2005
insulation on the exterior can be semi-rigid ﬁ berglass, foam
plastic boards or rock wool.
Insulating steel stud cavities always has been a bad idea due
to the conductivity of the steel studs. An R-19 ﬁ berglass batt
installed in a 5.5 in. (140 mm) steel-frame wall yields an effec-
tive thermal resistance of approximately R-7 when insulation
is installed “with good workmanship” (using the isothermal
planes method [ASHRAE Handbook—Fundamentals
[A
[
, Chapter
25]). Under typical installation practice that rarely sees good
workmanship, why even bother?
I can hear the ﬁ berglass industry lobbies and the steel
industry lobbies gearing up as I write this. Relax. Hurricane
Katrina’s aftermath will make steel and ﬁ berglass shine.
Residential steel framing has always been a problem due to
thermal bridging. When all of the insulation is on the exterior,
steel framing has huge advantages over wood. For one, rust is
less of a problem than mold and rot. Even better, insects don’t
eat it and it doesn’t burn. What’s not to like when the cavity is
empty and the insulation is on the outside? This makes even
more sense commercially.
As for the ﬁ berglass industry, semi-rigid ﬁ berglass has huge
advantages over foam plastic insulation. It is extremely vapor
permeable and will dry well when it is on the exterior of the
sheathing in a vented cavity. It also does not burn. I predict
more money can be made selling semi-rigid ﬁ berglass exterior
insulation than can be made selling low-density ﬁ berglass cav-
ity insulation. It results in a huge improvement in performance
Figure 1 (left): Pier foundation. No cavity insulation, no water-sensitive materials, assemblies designed to dry, cavities can be opened to
dry after moisture event. Figure 2 (right): Elevated crawlspace. No cavity insulation in wall or subﬂ oor, assemblies designed to dry, cavi-
ties can be opened to dry after moisture event (note the interior crawlspace grade is above the exterior grade and crawlspace access/vents
designed to “wash-out” during ﬂ ood).
Figure 3: Elevated slab. No water-sensitive materials on ﬁ rst level.
Cavity insulation on second level is non-water-sensitive and vapor
open. Attic is conditioned and unvented reducing wind uplift forces/
attic pressurization.
compared to current practice, and it is ﬂ ood resistant.
In commercial and residential masonry mass wall assemblies,
non-water-sensitive semi-vapor permeable rigid foam insulation
should be installed on the interior (Figure 6
(F
(
and Photograph 5
d d
).
16
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
November 2005
Wall 1: Masonry
wall with exterior
drainage plane/air
barrier/vapor
barrier. No water-
sensitive materials.
Assembly can dry
to both inte-
rior and exterior.
Exterior cavity
vented to exterior.
Interior cavity can
be opened to dry
to interior after
moisture event.
Wall 2: Masonry
mass wall. No
water-sensitive ma-
terials. Assembly
can dry to both in-
terior and exterior.
Interior cavity can
be opened to dry
to interior after
moisture event.
Wall 3: Steel stud
wall with exterior
drainage plane/air
barrier/vapor
barrier. No water-
sensitive materials.
Assembly can dry
to both inte-
rior and exterior.
Exterior cavity
vented to exterior.
Interior cavity can
be opened to dry
to interior after
moisture event.
Wall 4: Wood frame
wall with house-
wrap drainage
plane/air barrier.
No water-sensitive
materials (wood
frame treated to be
non-water sensi-
tive). Assembly can
dry to both inte-
rior and exterior.
Exterior cavity
vented to exterior.
Interior cavity can
be opened to dry
to interior after
moisture event.
Wall 5: Concrete
mass wall (precast/
tilt-up/cast-in-
place) with spray
foam insulation.
No water-sensitive
materials. Assem-
bly can dry to both
interior and exte-
rior. Interior cavity
can be opened to
dry to interior after
moisture event.
Wall 6: Concrete
mass wall (precast/
tilt-up/cast-in-
place) with rigid
foam insulation.
No water-sensitive
materials. Assem-
bly can dry to both
interior and exte-
rior. Interior cavity
can be opened to
dry to interior after
moisture event.
Flood-Resistant Walls
18
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
November 2005
Photograph 5 (left): Non-paper-faced exterior gypsum sheathing over uninsulated steel cavity. Figure 4 (right): Uninsulated steel frame.
Cavities are empty and can be ventilated to interior after moisture event. No moisture-sensitive materials used in assemblies.
On exterior surfaces, wood and wood-based cladding and trim
materials should be avoided (Photograph 8
(P
(
). Fiber cement and
plastic composite materials should be used for exterior cladding
and trim. All exterior cladding and trim should be back-vented. If
wood trim and cladding is used (grudgingly), it should be coated
on all six sides prior to installation to reduce water absorption.
20
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
November 2005
Photograph 6: Residential rustication in Florida. The ﬁ rst level is
masonry and the second level is wood frame.
Figure 5: Exterior insulation. Open uninsulated cavity. Materials
non-water sensitive. Assembly designed to dry. Egress skylight to al-
low escape from rising water. HVAC system is in conditioned attic.
Designing Assemblies to Dry Easily
Nothing dries like airflow, especially when no power
is available. Cavities should be designed to be ventilated.
Interior cavities should not contain absorptive insula-
tion—or any insulation for that matter. The interior cavi-
ties should be designed to allow vent openings to be eas-
ily provided at the top and bottom to provide ventilation
airﬂ ow.
The materials themselves should be vapor open in addition to
being non-water-sensitive to facilitate drying by diffusion.
22
ASHRAE Journal
ashrae.org
November 2005
Photograph 7 (left): Non-water-sensitive interior insulation. Note the unvented conditioned attic using vapor open spray foam—conditioned
attic is preferred location for HVAC system. Photograph 8 (right): No wood. Non-water-sensitive plastic and ﬁ ber cement exterior trim and
cladding elements installed over a ventilated exterior air space (i.e., cladding is back-vented).
The caveat to consider is that interior vented cavities have ﬁ re
consequences. Cavities should be sealed and compartmental-
ized under normal service conditions and only opened after a
moisture event to facilitate drying.
Conclusion
The rebuilding effort after Hurricane Katrina will have to ad-
dress the three principle disaster functions simultaneously: wind,
rain and ﬂ ood. The good news is that we have experience dealing
November 2005
ASHRAE Journal
23
with each disaster function individually. It
should be straightforward to integrate the
individual disaster functions.
Design and build using the following
approaches:
• Keep them from blowing away;
• Keep the rain out;
• Elevate the structures;
• Build with materials that can get
wet; and
• Design assemblies to easily dry
when they become wet.
Truthfully, these approaches apply to
all structures wherever they are built.
We shouldn’t need a natural disaster to
remind us of fundamentals.

ASHRAE Journal ashrae.org
January 2005
By John Dieckmann, P.E., Member ASHRAE; Bob Zogg, P.E., Member ASHRAE; Detlef Westphalen, Ph.D., Member
ASHRAE; Kurt Roth, Ph.D., Associate Member ASHRAE; and James Brodrick, Ph.D., Member ASHRAE
Heat-Only, Heat-Activated
Heat Pumps
This is the 21st article inspired by a recent DOE report cover-
ing energy-saving HVAC technologies.
M
ost residential and commercial buildings use a furnace,
boiler, or air-source heat pump (ASHP) for heating.
Some parts of the country, notably regions having
small annual heating loads (e.g., the South) or inexpensive
electricity (e.g., Idaho), may use electric furnaces instead. High-
efﬁ ciency absorption heat pumps represent a new alternative
for space heating.
Recently, several organizations have pursued development
of a commercially viable, reversible absorption (typically
ammonia-water) heat pump for residential space heating and
cooling.2,3 Most units provide both cooling and heating so the
consumer does not need to purchase separate heating
and cooling systems. If desired, these units also could
provide efﬁ cient water heating. Reversible absorption
heat pumps typically consume at least 15% more pri-
mary energy in cooling mode than would a comparable
vapor-compression cycle.
In heating mode, however, they consume at least
40% less primary energy than conventional furnaces
or boilers.1,2 Consequently, a heating-only absorption
heat pump could signiﬁ cantly reduce national heating
energy consumption. This suggests an alternative design,
a heating-only absorption system, targeted for regions
with large annual space heating loads and smaller cool-
ing loads, e.g., the northern half of the United States. A
heating-only system would also cost less than a unit providing
heating and cooling.
The heating-only heat pump depicted in Figure 1 operates
with a water-ammonia absorption cycle. Heat ﬂ ows into the
cycle from the outdoor air at the evaporator and from the
burner at the generator. Heat outputs sufﬁ cient to provide space
and/or water heating occur at the water-cooled absorber and the
condenser. Beginning at the expansion device, liquid ammonia
expands into the evaporator and accepts heat from outdoor
air. This causes the ammonia to evaporate, and the refrigerant
vapor ﬂ ows to the absorber. In the absorber, the water-ammo-
nia solution absorbs the low-pressure ammonia vapor, giving
off heat in the process. The solution then is pumped from the
absorber to the generator (at higher pressure), where heat input
increases the solution temperature until ammonia boils out of
solution. The high-pressure ammonia vapor condenses at the
desired heating temperature in the condenser, transferring heat
to the space and/or water heating system(s). Subsequently, the
condensed ammonia passes through the expansion device and
returns to the evaporator, completing the cycle. 2001 ASHRAE
Handbook—Fundamentals discusses several subtleties of am-
monia-water cycles, such as internal heat recovery and manag-
ing water concentration in the vapor leaving the generator.
A heating-only absorption heat pump is considerably simpler
than the reversible counterpart. In contrast to a reversible sys-
tem, the outdoor coil serves as both the condenser and absorber
in the cooling mode and the evaporator in the heating mode. In
a heating-only system, the outdoor coil is only an evaporator.
This enables performance and cost optimization of the outdoor
coil design. Moreover, a heating-only design simpliﬁ es hydronic
loop design and eliminates multiple reversing valves, which
further reduces system complexity and cost.
Energy Savings Potential
Analysis and test data indicate that reversible heat pumps can at-
tain a heating COP of 1.3 to 1.5.1,2,3 Although a basic tradeoff exists
between COP and the cost of major components, a heating-only
unit would likely attain similar performance. Relative to furnaces
and boilers, which typically have a seasonal efﬁ ciency (e.g., an-
nual fuel utilization efﬁ ciency) of about 0.8, a heating-only heat
Figure 1: Heating-only heat pump schematic.
d
The following article was published in ASHRAE Journal, January 2005. © Copyright 2005 American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and
Air-Conditioning Engineers, Inc. It is presented for educational purposes only. This article may not be copied and/or distributed electronically
or in paper form without permission of ASHRAE.
January 2005
ASHRAE Journal
41
pump reduces space heating energy consumption by about 40%
d
h
ti
ti
b
b
t 40%
to 50%. Relative to condensing furnaces and boilers, which have
seasonal efﬁ ciencies usually between 0.9 and 0.95, the energy sav-
ings could approach 40%. Compared to ASHPs, absorption heat
pump capacity and heating COP vary much less with the outdoor
temperature than electric ASHPs, which means they require little
supplemental heating. Placed in a national context, widespread
replacement of conventional boilers and furnaces with heating-
only absorption heat pumps could reduce annual space heating
primary energy consumption by 0.5 and 1.8 quads in commercial
and residential buildings, respectively. Water-heating applications
would realize further national energy savings.
Market Factors
Preliminary cost estimates suggest that a heating-only absorp-
tion heat pump would cost approximately 25% to 35% less than
a reversible cooling and heating heat pump. It would, however,
have a signiﬁ cant end-user cost premium relative to a boiler or
furnace, e.g., roughly $2,000 more than a residential furnace
of 100,000 Btu/h (29 kW) capacity, assuming high-volume
manufacturing (TIAX estimate). Market-optimized absorp-
tion heat pump designs will reﬂ ect tradeoffs among unit cost,
capacity, and efﬁ ciency.
For example, a design that maximized unit use might have a
limited capacity and incorporate supplemental gas heating to
meet the heating load on very cold days. This would moderately
decrease the unit’s effective seasonal efﬁ ciency and its energy
savings potential. On the other hand, if the cost of heating oil
and natural gas continue to escalate, the economic attractiveness
of heating-only absorption heat pumps would be enhanced.
Absorption units face other barriers to widespread use besides
equipment cost. Ammonia refrigerant is toxic and ﬂ ammable,
which necessitates installing units outdoors. Absorption units
also require a hydronic (i.e., forced hot water) connection
between the outdoor package and the indoor heat distribution
system (forced air or hydronic). This differs from the installa-
tion practice for boilers and furnaces and could further increase
the system installed cost. In addition, current manufacturers of
ammonia-water absorption systems are small companies or have
a small U.S. presence, which complicates achieving the larger
manufacturing volumes needed to reduce cost.
References
1. Rockenfeller, U., et al. 2002. “Gas ﬁ red unitary climate control
via advanced ammonia-water absorption.” Presented at the Gas Tech-
nology Institutes 1st Annual Conference and Exhibition on Natural
Gas Technologies.
2. TIAX. 2004. “Review of thermally activated technologies.”
Department of Energy, Distributed Energy, 21 July.
3. van der Stoel, H., et al. 2002. “Development of an absorption heat
pump for family dwellings.” Proceedings of the International Sorption
Heat Pump Conference.
John Dieckmann, P.E., is a principal, Bob Zogg, P.E., is an associate
principal, Detlef Westphalen, Ph.D., is an associate principal, and Kurt
W. Roth, Ph.D., is a senior technologist at the HVAC and Refrigeration
Technology sector of TIAX, Cambridge, Mass. James Brodrick, Ph.D.,
is a project manager, Building Technologies Program, U.S. Department
of Energy, Washington, D.C.
