ASHRAE Journal ashrae.org
February 2005
About the Authors
Ralph S. Pitzer, P.E., is a principal with GPM En-
gineering in Corpus Christi, Texas. Mike Malone,
P.E., is the mechanical delivery team leader at HEB
Grocery Co. in San Antonio.
By Ralph S. Pitzer, P.E., Member ASHRAE, and Mike Malone, P.E., Associate Member ASHRAE
Case Credits
& Return Air Paths
For Supermarkets
Refrigerated display case credits can
be confusing and no solid data exists on
exactly how much cooling spills from
the case and by what method it occurs.
According to the 1982 ASHRAE Hand-
book—HVAC Applications, Chapter 2 (and
repeated in each edition since), “the heat
removed by the refrigeration equipment
must be considered…because this heat is
being removed constantly, day and night,
summer and winter, regardless of the store
temperature” and “…the refrigerating ef-
fect imposed by the display ﬁ xtures must
be subtracted from the building’s gross
air-conditioning requirements.”
So what does this mean? The local cool-
ing you feel as you walk by any open case,
whether air spillage or radiant, adds to the
effective air-conditioning capacity and
reduces the effective heating capacity of
the HVAC equipment. This is the sensible
case credit and it occurs whether or not the
designer takes it into account. This is be-
cause it alters the effective space sensible
load, reduces the air volume necessary
to satisfy the space cooling demand and
increases the heating required to satisfy the
space heat loss. It also creates a need for
specialized air distribution to compensate
for the localized nature of the effect.
The latent heat absorption of these
cases reduces humidity in the store and
is most readily recognized as frost on
the evaporator coils and product or ad-
E
nergy-efﬁ cient HVAC systems are necessary for grocery stores to be competitive. Understanding and
properly using case credits in the system design of refrigerated display cases is critical in meeting this
goal. In addition, implementing different return air paths can have signiﬁ cant impact on system heating/
cooling capacities and comfort for shoppers. This article explains case credits and how they affect HVAC
system design. It also explains the impact different return methods (in conjunction with appropriately ap-
plied case credits) can have on heating and cooling capacities and related energy use.
The following article was published in ASHRAE Journal, February 2005. © Copyright 2005 American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and
Air-Conditioning Engineers, Inc. It is presented for educational purposes only. This article may not be copied and/or distributed electronically
or in paper form without permission of ASHRAE.
February 2005
ASHRAE Journal
43
ditional condensate going down the drain. Again the ASHRAE
Handbook addresses this, stating “The latent heat removed…
is 19% of the total… heavy coil frosting, product zone frosting
in low-temperature cases, ﬁ xtures sweating….”
Regarding comfort of the shoppers, “To take advantage of
the cooling effect of the open display cases and provide an even
temperature distribution throughout the store, the cold air must be
mixed with the general store air” and “To accomplish the neces-
sary air mixing, air returns should be located at the ﬂ oor level;
they should also be strategically placed to ensure removal of cold
air near concentrations of refrigerated ﬁ xtures.” Two commonly
applied solutions are underﬂ oor ducts for return air collection
and fans on the backside and top of cases (1991 ASHRAE Hand-
book—HVAC Applications, Chapter 2, Figures 2 and 3).
While implementing these recommendations will improve the
comfort situation, this information doesn’t address how much
sensible and latent cooling is felt in the occupied zone and how
to account for this heating and cooling impact on the HVAC
system capacities and layout. The ASHRAE Handbook only
quantitatively addresses this in general, not speciﬁ c, terms.
The HEB Grocery Company, headquartered in San Antonio,
Texas, made an effort some years ago to evaluate this phenomenon
and incorporate its impact on HVAC system performance and
comfort conditions into their HVAC design. The engineering team
pursued measurement of typical aisle and return air temperatures in
several stores with varying return air paths. With this information,
the team developed design criteria in 1989, reﬁ ned in 1993 with
a design ﬁ rm’s assistance that was distributed to all of the chain’s
MEP consultants for incorporation into its HVAC designs (see
sidebar for how this was developed and used). These credits can
be applied either to the space sensible and latent loads or to adjust
the return air conditions, depending on the location of the cases
and the return methods incorporated into the HVAC system. What
is important is where and how they affect the space temperature
distribution and impact the HVAC equipment performance.
Many grocery and retail stores are designed with an open
structure ceiling, meaning that the occupied zone takes up only
about one-half to one-third of the volume of the store. With roofs
ranging from 13 ft (4 m) to well over 20 ft (6.1 m), signiﬁ cant
opportunity exists for stratiﬁ cation. While this article will not
deal with the dynamics of that particular subject, sufﬁ ce it to
say that heat rises and taking return air from near the roof will
impact the temperature of recirculated air entering the HVAC
equipment. For the purposes of this article, high conventional
return means taken through return grilles/openings where the
inlet is higher than 10 ft (3 m) above the ﬂ oor (Figure 2a
(F
(
), and
low conventional return (Figure 2b
(F
(
) are grilles near ﬂ oor level,
or lower than 4 ft (1.2 m) above the ﬂ oor.
Energy-efﬁ cient HVAC systems are necessary for grocery stores to be competitive.
44
ASHRAE Journal ashrae.org
February 2005
Taking return air from underneath the cases will produce
greater impact, improving temperature distribution and therefore
customer comfort. See Figures 3a and 3b for the typical refriger-
ated case proﬁ le and suggestions for how this be accomplished as
compared to conventional methods. The return air can be collected
into a plenum above the case or connected to a duct running over
the case, but whichever method is used, the return air is cooler and
drier than with conventional return paths. While the underﬂ oor
ducts (1991 ASHRAE Handbook—HVAC Applications, Chapter
2, Figure 2) achieve similar results, the design presented here is
generally less expensive to construct, cleaner in operation and
more accommodating of the inevitable store remodel. The refriger-
ated case credits, typically assigned to the space load calculations,
are determined by the same methods, and the modiﬁ ed return air
temperature is used to size the heating and cooling systems.
As an example, here is data on a recently constructed HEB
grocery store just outside San Antonio, Texas, with indoor design
conditions of 75°F (23.9°C) and 45% RH (58 grains/lb absolute)
and outdoor conditions of 99.0°F dry bulb/78.0°F wet bulb
(37.2/25.6°C). The 73,000 ft2 (6785 m2) store faces northeast,
which contains the only signiﬁ cant glazing. With good thermal
characteristics for the building envelope, the cooling load breaks
down as follows: the roof is 17.7% of the building sensible load,
the glazing is 12.5% and the walls are 11.3%—the internal loads
(people, lighting and equipment) are the remaining 58.5%. At
1,105 MBtu/h (323.7 kW) (not taking into account any case
credits), the space sensible load for the 54,100 ft2 (5029 m2)
main sales area would require 52,500 cfm (24 780 L/s) at 56°F
(13.3°C) supply air temperature—the latent load is 140 MBtu/h
(41.21 kW). The ventilation requirement, combining occupant
load and positive pressure needs, is 7,820 cfm (3690 L/s).
Assuming 78.0/63.0°F (25.5/17.2°C) for high return air, the
mixed air condition entering the cooling coil would be 81.1/65.2°F
(27.3/18.4°C). The necessary cooling capacity is 1,722 MBtu/h
(504.4 kW) or 144 tons (500 kW) to reach the 56°F (13.3°C)
leaving air temperature. The heating mixed air, assuming a 70°F
(21.1°C) space with an outdoor design of 31°F (–0.6°C), would
be 66.7°F (19.3°C). To satisfy the space heat loss, the calculated
leaving air temperature is 77.1°F (25.1°C) for a nominal heating
requirement of 589 MBtu/h (172.7 kW). We normally assign an

\* 1987 ASHRAE Handbook—HVAC Systems and Applications, Chapter 18.3, Table 1. \*\* Usage factor based on a ﬁ ve-store HEB case study.
In determining supermarket air conditioning load, it is im-
portant to account for the refrigeration effect imposed by the
refrigerated display equipment in the store. This load must be
subtracted from the gross air conditioning requirements and
must also be added to the gross heating capacity.
The information presented in Tables 3 and 4 is a result of
applying the 1987 ASHRAE Handbook—HVAC Systems and
Applications, Chapter 18.3, Table 1 combined with trial and error
and case study in the ﬁ eld. The main differences between the
ASHRAE table and how HEB Grocery applies “case credits” are
that HEB uses the manufacturer’s ﬁ xture data for the particular
equipment it installs in its stores and HEB has adjusted the con-
tribution from case spillage based on performance information
gathered in a ﬁ ve-store sample case study.
According to ASHRAE Handbook Table 1, depending on
the display ﬁ xture, 80% – 85% of the refrigeration load is
sensible heat and 15% – 20% is latent heat. For most upright
single and multideck frozen refrigerated display ﬁ xtures,
70% of the refrigeration load is spilled into the sales area by
the operation of the cases—65% for medium temperature
multideck ﬁ xtures. For cofﬁ n or island cases, a load factor
of 50% is applied. This is because the contribution to under
case return and the contribution to the zone are about equal,
based on how most supermarkets are laid out. The island
cases are some distance from the actual returns located under
the case, so only about 50% of the case credit contribution
is applied to the return air. The other 50% is contributed to
reducing the zone load. Door cases are factored by 35%
based on the number of door openings occurring per hour
(HEB performed a time-motion study to determine this). This
research, conducted with the manufacturer’s assistance, was
not only to determine the contribution these cases make in
reducing the HVAC load, but also for the manufacturers to
develop reliability in their door design.
To allow engineering consultants to make sense of this
information and for ease of use, HEB created a standard-
ized template to determine case credit application factors.
This table represents the calculation method used. The only
data that appears in the HEB design manual is the ﬁ nal ap-
plication factors.
Example:
48 ft of multideck produce cases with a total refrigeration
load of 20,400 Btu/h
Sensible Case Credit: 20,400 Btu/h 0.52 = 10,608 Btu/h
Latent Case Credit: 20,400 Btu/h 0.13 = 2,652 Btu/h
The heat removed by the refrigerated cases must also be
considered in the heating system design. When calculating
heating capacity, case credits must be added to the heating
load in the same manner as they are credited to the cooling
load. If not, the heating system will not have sufﬁ cient capacity
to maintain the desired space temperature at design outdoor
weather conditions.
It is important that the ﬁ nal air-conditioning case load be
determined correctly. After tabulating the load, refer to 1987
ASHRAE Handbook—HVAC Systems and Applications, Chap-
ter 18.3, Figure 1. There are considerable ﬁ xture (case load)
heat removal differences as the relative humidity and tempera-
ture in the store vary by relatively small increments. Increases
in the relative humidity above 55% add considerable load,
while lower relative humidity substantially decreases the load
as shown in Figure 1. The case load total should be further
factored to reduce the case credit total by that amount shown
on the graph. HEB Grocery does not use any safety factors
when calculating HVAC loads in its stores, so this ﬁ nal case
credit factor is sometimes not applied. The difference would
be an implicit safety factor.
Calculating Case Credits

Table 4: Latent HVAC case credit factors.

Figure 2a: High conventional return. 2b: Low conventional return.
85°F (29.4°C) minimum discharge temperature for comfort pur-
poses, even though it is higher than the calculated requirement,
for a scheduled heating capacity of 1,037 MBtu/h (303.8 kW).
This is the base case for comparison purposes.
With low conventional return (75.0/61.0°F or 23.9/16.1°C), the
mixed air condition is 78.5/63.5°F (25.8/17.5°C), which requires
only 1,422 MBtu/h (416.5 kW) or 119 tons (418 kW) cooling. No
difference exists in heating between high and low return depending
upon the designer’s assumptions for stratiﬁ cation and willingness
to reduce capacity for this condition when using high return.
Now with the case credits: of the approximately 350 hp of refrig-
eration, the refrigeration case credits, applicable to the occupied
zone, are 319 MBtu/h (93.50 kW), a near 30% reduction in the
1,105 MBtu/h (323.7 kW) space sensible load. The latent credits
are 76,285 Btu/h (22.34 kW), which is slightly over half of the
space latent load. Subtracting the space sensible credit from the
space sensible load gives 786 MBtu/h (230.2 kW)—this reduces
the airﬂ ow requirement from 52,500 to 38,500 cfm (24,780 to
18,170 L/s), using the same 56°F (13.3°C) supply air temperature.
This provides a signiﬁ cant reduction in fan motor requirements,
from 42.3 to 31.0 bhp (31 to 23 kW) (for the same static pressure)
and 50 to 40 hp (37 to 30 kW) motor nominal. For a store that
operates 24/7, this is nearly $6,000. What more could an operator
ask than simply taking credit for what is happening anyway? And,
every designer who tells the customer about this “design feature”
looks great to the client because these savings don’t have an up-
front cost to amortize like most energy-reduction measures!
You could stop there and be satisﬁ ed with these “free savings”
—but why? Let’s discuss return air. This particular store has 240
linear ft (73.2 m) of cases available for undercase return. These
are separate from the cases where we applied the space credits
noted earlier. At 100 cfm per linear ft (155 L/s per m), they can
accommodate 24,000 cfm (11 330 L/s) of return air. Applying
the appropriate factors based on the style of case, they provide
180 MBtu/h (52.94 kW) sensible credit—this reduces the return
air temperature from 75.0°F to 68.0°F (23.9°C to 20.0°C). The
corresponding latent credit is 45,450 Btu/h (13.31 kW), which
lowers the wet bulb from 61.0°F to 57.8°F (16.1°C to 14.3°C)
for the 24,000 cfm (11 330 L/s).
These credits are not taken against the space loads because the
undercase return feature essentially eliminates these cases’ impact
directly into the space. While you can calculate lower return air
temperatures than this in many cases, the experience at HEB has
been that 66°F to 67°F (18.9°C to 19.4°C) seems to be a lower
limit, and we recommend caution in using lower temperatures for
selecting the HVAC equipment. Whether this is due to radiant cool-
ing as opposed to air spillage would be a good research project.
Continuing our analysis, the layout for this particular store did
not allow the remaining return air to be taken from underneath
cases or even at ﬂ oor level—the architects retained control over the
ﬂ oor plan at the expense of optimal HVAC design. Therefore, the
coil entering air condition was the mixture of three streams: 7,820
cfm (3690 L/s) OA at 99.0/78.0°F (37.2/25.6°C), 24,000 cfm (11
330 L/s) undercase return air at 68.0/57.8°F (20.0/14.3°C) and

The more important result is in the operating cost. For a
simpliﬁ ed energy comparison, we generally assume an annual
average cooling load of 60% of peak. At 8 cents per kWh, the
annual cooling savings are projected to be $32,300. When you
add the fan motor savings of $5,900, the total energy savings are
an impressive $38,200. Since the difference in heating is only to
overcome the temperature drop of under case return, the annual
heating penalty is minimal at $770 (assuming $3.25 per MCF
[1,000 cubic feet] of natural gas). See Table 2 for more detailed
system comparisons and projected savings. Once again, energy
savings were produced with no upfront cost to amortize—in
fact it was less expensive. Even if the ﬁ rst cost were higher, the
savings are impressive and crucial to the operator.
In conclusion, the HVAC designer needs to understand the
impact open refrigerated cases have on the occupied zone of the
grocery store. Mixing cool air from the cases will not only alter
the HVAC system capacities, but it impacts customer comfort.
By properly applying case credits and using a more effective re-
turn air method, the designer can reduce ﬁ rst cost and operating
cost for a grocery store while improving comfort, which usually
leads to increased sales per customer according to various market
research reports. These are all beneﬁ ts for the store operator and
a win for the design, construction and operations team.
6,680 cfm (3,150 L/s) high return at 78.0/63.0°F (25.5/17.2°C).
This produces air at 76.0/62.8°F (24.4/17.1°C), 2.5°F (1.4°C)
cooler than the low conventional return. The new cooling capacity
is only 954 MBtu/h (279.5 kW) or 80 tons (280 kW). The heating
entering air would be 58.2°F (14.6°C). Needing a 90°F (32.2°C)
discharge to meet the larger heating requirement (due to includ-
ing the space case credits) with the lower supply air volume, the
heating capacity of the unit must now be 1,322 MBtu/h (387.2
kW). These ﬁ gures are summarized in Table 1.
Taking into account the case credits and using the optimum
return air method reduces the cooling capacity requirement from
144 to 80 tons (44% reduction) and reduces the fan motor from
50 to 40 hp. At the same time, it increases the heating capacity
from 1,037 to 1,322 MBtu/h (303.8 to 387.2 kW). The store
used a dual path air-conditioning unit with a contractor’s cost
of approximately $700 per ton. In the San Antonio area, this is
a ﬁ rst cost savings in the range of $70,000 when you include
the unit capacity, downsizing the supply duct and electrical
connections and other related installation efforts. Construct-
ing the return plenum over the cases or installing duct above
them can vary from $15,000 to about $40,000, depending on
the conﬁ guration and overall length. The net ﬁ rst cost savings
could be as much as $55,000 to as little as $30,000.

By Ralph F. Goldman, Ph.D., Fellow/Life Member ASHRAE
For a more complete discussion of ASHRAE’s views, see its position paper on homeland security,
“Risk Management Guidance for Health, Safety and Environmental Security Under Extraordi-
nary Incidents,” at www.ashrae.org/homelandsecurity. Also, the information presented here
may not constitute the opinion of the Alfred P. Sloan Foundation.
Personal Protective
Systems for
First Responders
About the Author
Ralph F. Goldman, Ph.D., is chief scientist at
Comfort Technology in Framingham, Mass.
Background
The original ASHRAE thermal com-
fort zone, established about 75 years
ago based on responses to an ASHRAE
seven-point thermal comfort vote scale
(Table 1), was the range of air tempera-
tures from 68°F to 74°F.\* Subsequent
studies involving thousands of subjects
some 25 years later moved the comfort
range up to 72°F to 78°F. This was largely
accounted for by a change in clothing,
from the deﬁ ning 1 clo1 unit for the busi-
ness suit and long underwear typically
worn during the 1920s, to the 0.6 clo\* of
the long sleeved shirt and trousers over
T-shirt and shorts worn in the 1950s.
In addition to attaching a clo value for
clothing to the deﬁ ned comfort range, four
other factors were speciﬁ ed. The thermal
comfort range was accepted to be an air
temperature (Ta) of 72°F to 78°F if, and
only if, air motion (WV) was 40 fpm,\*
relative humidity (RH) was 40%, mean
radiant temperature (MRT) Ta, the cloth-
ing provided 0.6 clo of insulation, and the
individual was at rest ( metabolic heat
production [M
[M
[ ] of 1 Met\* = 50 kcal/m
M]
M
2
m2
m ·hr).
The clothing moisture permeability index
[Im
2] was ignored; it was assumed that the
individual was not sweating (skin relative
humidity [s] was <20%).
It is now recognized that comfort based
on the ASHRAE seven-point predicted
mean vote (PMV) is, at best, a useful
engineering approximation as shown by
MacIntyre (Figure 1
(F
(
). The variability in
comfort perception is enormous. The
standard deviation of PMV is 1 full vote
unit. Thus, the 95% probability range for
a “comfortable” PMV (= 4), ± 2 standard
deviations includes the range from cool
(PMV = 2) to warm (PMV = 6).
This seven-point scale may have origi-
nated with the thermoscope developed
in Germany by Otto von Guericke about
1650. His scale used “great heat, hot air,
warm air, temperate air, cool air, cold
air and great cold.” This PMV approach,
although often used, may force judgments
(votes) exceeding the human capacity to
discriminate.
F
irst responders, such as ﬁ reﬁ ghters, to chemical, biological and
radiation (CBR) incidents need improved protective systems that
can provide comfort as well as protection. This article describes three
approaches to CBR protection: chemical barriers to destroy agents; totally
impermeable barriers; and ﬁ ltering and/or absorbing barriers. It also dis-
cusses why providing comfort using these systems is such a challenge.
\* (°F – 32) ÷ 1.8 = °C; 1 clo= 0.155 K·m2/W; 1 Met = 58.2 W/m2; fpm × 0.00508 = m/s

Air-Conditioning Engineers, Inc. It is presented for educational purposes only. This article may not be copied and/or distributed electronically
or in paper form without permission of ASHRAE.
February 2005
ASHRAE Journal
51
An informal ASHRAE working group, consisting of A.P.
Gagge, a physicist, Ralph Nevins, an HVAC engineer, and
the author, an environmental physiologist, reviewed the four
environmental (Ta, WV, RH and MRT) and two behavioral (clo
and M) factors in thermal comfort and, based on human physi-
M)
M
ological studies beginning in the 1940s, suggested trade-offs, as
shown in Table 2. Examination of Table 3, which uses typical clo
and M for ﬁ rst responders CBR threats, clearly establishes why
M
M
comfortable CBR protective clothing is an oxymoron without
auxiliary cooling.
Using these suggested trade-offs, the major cause of heat stress
for ﬁ rst responders becomes much clearer as shown in Table 3.
Clearly, ﬁ rst responders have a heat stress problem, primar-
ily caused by:
1. Any sustained activity requiring high metabolic heat
quiring high metabolic heat
production;
2. Difﬁ culty in losing this heat through multilayered protec-
tive clothing;
3. Number 2 is complicated by elevated ambient
temperature and/or humidity.
The Human HVAC System
Heat Production Capacity
p
y
Human life depends on metabolic heat
production. The 0.8 Met produced while
asleep reﬂ ects 0.1 Met used for circulation
and respiration, 0.2 Met for the nervous
system (including the brain) and 0.5 Met
for the gut at rest. Awake, but seated,
the Met for circulation and respiration
increases to 0.3 Met, making up the
total of 1 Met unit (50 kcal/m2/h = 90
kcal/h = 105 W for the average adult
male with 1.8 m2 of body surface
area) for seated ofﬁ ce work.
Increasing Met reflects the
demands of working muscles,
which are only ~20% efﬁ cient, so non-sedentary work requires
transport of most of the metabolic heat from the muscles, by one
side of the circulatory system pump (the heart), which pumps
the heat transfer ﬂ uid (the blood) to the skin where it can be
transferred out of the body by radiation, convection and, when
required and possible, by sweat evaporation. Light ofﬁ ce work
requires ~1.25 Met, and very light physical work ~1.75 Met,
with the other half of the heart pumping blood to the lungs
to pick up oxygen from respiration (10 L of air per minute)
and deliver it to the other side of the pump for delivery to the
working muscles, as well as to the nervous system (including
the brain) and gut.
Light physical work is ~3.25 Met (requiring 20 L/min. of
inspired air). Moderate work (about 450 kcal/h—the typical
maximal voluntary rate for tasks that may take three to four
hours) is ~5 Met (35 L/min. of inspired air).
Heat Transfer Demand
Even at moderate work i.e., 5 Met [35 L/min.], at 20% efﬁ ciency
~360 kcal/h) of the 450 kcal/h produced must be eliminated from
the body. Considering that the respiratory muscles are only ~6%
efﬁ cient, the increased demand to 35 L/min. for inspiration of
enough oxygen (1 L of inspired oxygen  4.85 kcal) contributes
signiﬁ cantly to the need to eliminate heat from the body. Heat
that cannot be eliminated must be stored in the body. Since the
average speciﬁ c heat of body tissue is 0.83 kcal/kg·°C, the mean
body temperature of an average (i.e., 72.5 kg [160 lb]) young adult
male will increase 1°C for every 60 kcal of heat stored. Heat stor-
age of 1.15 kcal/kg of body weight is usually the limit for opting
age of 1.15 kcal/kg of body weight is us

kg man this is <240 kcal

Cold Clim
g
HVAC systems are not
Human
gned for cold, although
well desig
s are provided with more
some of us
nsulation (hair) and inter-
external in
nsulation (fat) than others.
nal in
ll experience goose ﬂ esh,
We a
caused by tiny piloerector
muscles attempting to pull these hairs upright and thus increase
the thickness of our insulation but, unlike furred animals, not
even the hairiest human has enough for this vestigial defense
mechanism to be of much beneﬁ t. Thus, we attempt behavioral
temperature regulation; when awake, by adding clothing and,
when asleep, by minimizing the body surface area exposed to
heat loss by curling into a fetal position.
Our ﬁ rst useful physiological line of defense against cold is
vasoconstriction, a reduction of the normal skin blood ﬂ ow that
delivers ~72 kcal/m2·hr to the skin when at rest in a comfortable
environment. Initially, this circulatory heat ﬂ ow (q) to the skin
is reduced to ~23 kcal/m2·hr, except for the feet which appear to
immediately cut their circulatory heat input to the “full vasocon-
striction” level of 7 kcal/m2·hr. Of course, being relatively thin
52
ASHRAE Journal ashrae.org
February 2005
cylinders, with little mass to hold heat, little subcutaneous fat to
conserve heat and a disproportionately large surface area to mass
for heat loss, our ﬁ rst perception of cold is usually cold toes,
shortly followed by cold ﬁ ngers. Toe temperatures fall if we even
approach the lower boundary of the thermal comfort zone.
Our second line of defense against cold is to increase heat pro-
duction (M
(M
( ). Again we can use behavioral temperature regulation,
M)
M
becoming more active to do this but, unless properly insulated,
this usually increases our heat loss more than the extra heat pro-
duced, as a result of the reduction of the external surface still air
layer, the pumping of air trapped within the clothing (resulting
in greater intra-clothing ventilation with ambient air), and the
override of vasoconstriction as the body attempts to move the
extra heat being produced in the body core to the skin so that it
can be eliminated to ambient air.
Physiological temperature regulation, i.e., increasing heat pro-
duction by shivering, suffers from the same additional increases
in heat loss as behavioral regulation. The measured maximum
heat production from shivering is ~425 kcal/h and this is not a
functional state; one is simply a massively shivering block of
tissue, unable to walk out of the cold chamber unassisted. Even
if physiological temperature regulation can keep humans in the
compensable cold zone (i.e., where the demand for heat production
the capacity for heat loss) shivering is never comfortable.
Hot Climate Design
g
Human temperature regulation is best in hot climates. Note that
an average adult man has about 1.8 m2 (~20 ft2) of heat transfer
surface (i.e., his skin); normally this is at ~6% humidity since it
is a semipermeable membrane surrounding the body core, which
is about 72% water. As in the cold, the ﬁ rst line of defense is to
alter the circulatory blood ﬂ ow to the skin. However, in the heat
the ﬂ ow is increased, to raise skin temperature and increase heat
loss to the environment or, if the air temperature is already above
skin temperature, reduce the heat gain from the environment. At
rest in a comfortable environment (i.e., 72°F to 78°F, with the
other ﬁ ve key factors as speciﬁ ed for comfort at this range), the
normal circulatory heat ﬂ ow (q) to the skin is 72 kcal/m2·h.
With moderate vasodilation (dilation of the skin blood vessels),
q increases to 230 kcal/m2·h; when the demand for heat loss is at
a maximum, q jumps to 440 kcal/m2·h. This increased circulatory
heat ﬂ ow to the skin obviously increases skin temperature (Ts) and
as Ts rises above ~95°F, sweating, the second line of defense kicks

Note that sweat production does not provide any cooling unless
sweat can be evaporated, but each gram of sweat evaporated can
remove 0.58 kcal from the skin.
As long as the demand for sweat evaporation can be matched
to the capacity for sweat evaporation allowed by:
1. The clothing moisture permeability index ratio (I(I( m/clo);
2. The vapor pressure gradient between the skin and ambi-

then the individual is in the sweat compensable zone of
temperature regulation.
Having classiﬁ ed the human HVAC system into these ﬁ ve
zones, it may be useful to relate these to the classic PMV scale,
as well as to the percent skin wetness (i.e., relative humidity),
expressing the latter as the Erequired

max ratio—the required

protective clothing system with a speciﬁ ed moisture perme-

This is done in Figure 2, which shows the role of clothing,
and other factors in separating the compensable hot and cold
zones from the incompensable ones, as well as the expected
PMV and percent skin wetness in the heat or metabolic heat
production in the cold, as shown below.
A useful basis for understanding why heat accumulates in
the body, producing a continuum of heat illness, is suggested
by three simple equations.
The ﬁ rst implies that cardiac output of blood, i.e., the volume of
blood (L/min.) pumped by the heart, at rest or work in any envi-
ronment, is simply a function of heart rate and stroke volume.
1. Cardiac output (C.O.) = heart rate stroke volume
The second equation states that the vol-
ume of oxygen (VO2, in L/min.) supplied
to the various body organs (i.e., brain,
muscles, skin, etc.) is simply a function of
the cardiac output (C.O.) times the differ-
ence between the oxygen concentration in
the arterial blood (ArO2) as it is circulated
to these organs, and the oxygen in the ve-
nous blood (VeO2) as it leaves them.
2. VO2 Delivery to Organs = C.O. 
(Arterial-Venous) Oxygen
The third equation states that the maxi-
mum amount of heat (kcal/min.) that can
be transferred from the body’s core (i.e.,
muscles and other organs) to the skin is a
function of the cardiac output (C.O.) times
the difference between the temperature of
the body’s core (the primary site of heat
production during work) and the temperature of the skin. Al-
most all the heat produced by the body must be delivered to the
skin before it can be lost through clothing to the environment
by convective, radiant and evaporative heat transfer.
3. Maximum Heat Delivery to Skin = C.O.  (Tcore
– Tskin
T
)
These three equations suggest competition for cardiac
output to:
a. Transport oxygen between the muscles (and other organs
in the body’s core) and the brain; and
b. Transport heat from the body’s core to the skin.
A fourth equation (suggested in Endnote 4) establishes the
maximum total heat transfer (kcal/h) at any environmental air
temperature (Tair

(36°C), fully sweating (vapor pressure [VP] = 44 mmHg) skin
to the environment:
a. Through clothing insulation (in clo
units) as radiative and convective heat
loss; and
b. Through clothing resistance to evapo-
rative heat loss (I(I( m/clo) by evaporation.

Protective Clothing Systems
The ﬁ rst component of modern chemi-
cal-biological (CB) protective systems, a
simple oro-nasal mask protecting the respi-
ratory system, was developed shortly after
introduction of chemical warfare on April
22, 1915. A simple face mask was devel-
oped to protect the lungs and eyes against
respiratory agents, but more persistent and
lethal agents that attacked the skin (e.g., mustard gas) were
developed. These required whole-body protection, so a protec-
tive clothing system consisting of a set of chlorinated-carbon
impregnated long underwear worn under an impregnated outer
uniform, with impregnated cotton gauntlet gloves and socks was
developed. With an improved canister gas mask, a plastic hood
and plastic over-boots, shown in Figure 3, this remained the U.S.
standard protective CB clothing until the early 1960s.
It provided protection for limited periods of time (break-
through time) against chemical agents. However, the two lay-
ers were so insulating (high clo) and took so long for sweat to

ASHRAE Journal ashrae.org
February 2005
break through to the surface (low Im until sweat soaked, and then
possibly provided reduced protection) that tolerance time for
work was limited. The limits are shown in Figure 4 as predicted
tolerance time to 50% risk of heat casualties (i.e., body heat
storage 2.25 kcal/kg) as a function of the ambient wet-bulb
globe temperature (WBGT). This environmental index, cal-
culated as 0.7 the natural wet bulb temperature (i.e., a wet
bulb ventilated only by ambient air movement) + 0.2 black
globe temperature + 0.1  air temperature generally is used for
predicting human tolerance times in hot environments. Since
the wearer is not being ventilated like a psychrometric wet-bulb
thermometer, use of the natural wet bulb is essential, and those
weightings reﬂ ect the relative effects of evaporative, radiation
and convective heat transfer on the human body.
A second CB protective clothing approach was developed: a
one- or two-piece garment fabricated as a single layer of foam
with embedded activated charcoal, shown in Figure 5. This
JSLIST garment is the latest charcoal-in-foam system. This
approach was much more comfortable. It had lower insulation
(i.e., lower clo, especially with air motion), higher moisture
permeability (I(I( m) and, thus, a much higher (I(I( m/clo) which meant
the wearer could get a greater percentage of the evaporative
cooling allowed in an environment. It also sweat wet-out the
surface much earlier, further enhancing evaporative cooling
capacity. However, the original 1960s versions eventually were
found to offer much less CBR protection and despite continu-
ing attempts to develop versions with decreased vapor, liquid
and particulate permeability and increased breakthrough time,
even this latest model JSLIST garment is rated only as Level
C protection. Note the tape used to seal up the waist, wrist
and ankle, overlapped openings.
Figure 6 shows a Level B protective system. This one-piece,
6 6
totally air- and moisture-impermeable Level B suit has elastic
wrist, face and ankle openings. The Level B system requires
that the respiratory mask, gloves and rubber boots be sealed
to the garment with tape at these apertures to prevent possible
inﬁ ltration of contaminated air from the environment. While a
Level B suit is less “hobbling” because of the reduced rigidity at
these junctions and because it allows a self-contained breathing
apparatus (SCBA with typical weight ~ 33 lb) to be worn as a
backpack outside the suit, it has the same insulation and total
lack of moisture and air permeability as the totally encapsulat-
ing Level A systems that have solidly connected butyl gloves
and booties, as shown in Figure 7.
Figure 7 shows a totally encapsulating Level A system with
7 7
its intrinsic butyl gloves and booties (covered by boots) and full
head cover with a large clear ﬁ eld of view. A Velcro-sealed ﬂ ap,
covering the gas-proof zipper closure for this front-entered suit,
barely can be detected running from the right shoulder down
toward the left hand. A closed rebreathing system is worn as
a backpack inside the voluminous Level A garment. While
providing maximum possible CBR protection the weight and
hobbling effect of this Level A system is enough to increase the
metabolic heat production of its wearer by almost 50% even
when seated at rest.
Obviously, without some form of efﬁ cient auxiliary cooling
(i.e., a system whose weight does not increase the heat produced
by the wearer by a large percent of the heat removal capacity of
the cooling system), available safe work time is generally <1
hour for moderate or heavier work demands. This completes
the argument that comfortable CBR protection is an oxymoron
and remains so for the foreseeable future.
Endnotes
1. One clo unit of insulation is directly equivalent to an R
value of 0.877, corresponding to a combined radiation and
convection heat transfer of 5.55 kcal/m2·h per °C difference
between human skin and ambient air temperatures; it represents
the insulation provided by each 0.25 in. thickness of clothing
of almost any ﬁ ber or construction, since the thickness of the
trapped air is the dominant factor.
2. The permeability index (I(I( m) is the ratio of the evaporative
heat loss from the skin surface through the clothing fabrics and
associated trapped + surface air layers to the maximum evaporative
heat loss of a psychrometric (i.e., maximally ventilated) wet-bulb
thermometer in the given ambient environment. The clothing
permeability index ratio (I(I( m/clo) deﬁ nes the maximum evaporative
cooling available to the wearer in any given environment speciﬁ ed
by Ta, WV, RH and MRT as: 5.55 Im/clo kcal/m2·h 2.2 per
mm difference between human skin (sPs) and ambient air vapor
pressure (aPa), where 2.2 = the psychrometric constant (Lewis
#),  = relative humidity, and P = saturated vapor pressure. The
P
Im/clo of the usual everyday clothing worn indoors is ~0.45.
3. Usually accepted as ~1 L/h, although one very severe
study (seated inside a closed tank wearing fully encap-
sulating protective clothing) measured 3.2 L of sweat

About the Authors
G.N. Dunn is a research associate and I.P. Knight
is senior lecturer at the Welsh School of Architec-
ture in Cardiff, Wales. E.R. Hitchin is technical
director at Building Research Establishment in
Watford, England.
By G.N. Dunn, I.P. Knight and E.R. Hitchin
Measuring System Efﬁ ciencies of
and
In a consumer-led, knowledge-based
economy, a conﬂ ict exists between the
desire to restrict the use of air condition-
ing to reduce environmental impact and
the market demand for air conditioning
on the grounds of enhanced productiv-
ity. In practice, to resolve this conﬂ ict
we must explore new ways to reduce
energy use and emissions resulting
from air conditioning. The way forward
hile energy consumption and atmospheric carbon emissions from most building-related services
are falling, in the United Kingdom those associated with air conditioning (A/C) are growing as
is likely to involve the use of cleaner
energy, improved integration of building
and services design and promotion of
highly energy efﬁ cient A/C systems. To
achieve the latter goal, clear guidance is
required on the appropriate use of A/C
and which systems are the most energy
efﬁ cient in practice.
The research presented in this article,
along with other work being carried
out at the Welsh School of Architec-
ture,5 aims to improve understanding of
the actual energy performance of
A/C systems.
W
more buildings become air conditioned1 due to increasing occupant expectations of thermal comfort.2
Future projections of UK market trends suggest increased use of A/C will continue, resulting in increased
national energy demand and its associated carbon emissions.3 This growth in carbon emissions conﬂ icts
with national commitments to reduce greenhouse gas emissions under the Kyoto Protocol and the UK
government’s additional goal to reduce emissions by 60% before 2050.4
The following article was published in ASHRAE Journal, February 2005. © Copyright 2005 American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and
Air-Conditioning Engineers, Inc. It is presented for educational purposes only. This article may not be copied and/or distributed electronically
or in paper form without permission of ASHRAE.
February 2005
ASHRAE Journal
27
Research Overview
This article presents research ﬁ ndings of a ﬁ eld monitoring
study, which has determined the energy efﬁ ciency of fan coil and
packaged direct expansion (DX) air-conditioning systems in
use within UK office buildings. The method employed
determined the in-use efﬁ ciency of each system through the
measurement of their electrical energy consumption and
thermal cooling output.
The work considered the efﬁ ciency of the whole system, in-
cluding all equipment loads associated with the delivery of cool-
ing to the occupied space and not just the chiller and refrigeration
aspects. This enabled direct comparison of the energy efﬁ ciency
of the different DX and fan coil systems studied. In addition, it
provided detailed proﬁ les of system energy consumption, cool-
ing output and the frequency that various cooling loads were
encountered, as well as the breakdown of energy consumption
by subsystem component for non-packaged systems.
These results are compared with estimates derived from
comprehensive building and system simulations. Although not
commonplace, such simulations are easier to carry out than ﬁ eld
measurements but, of course, represent simpliﬁ ed situations.
The simulations cover a wider range of systems than have been
monitored and so help to place the results in a wider context.
The ﬁ eld monitoring measured the in-use efﬁ ciency of three
liquid chillers with fan coil systems and a packaged split direct
expansion system between May 2002 and July 2003. The sys-
tems are described in more detail in Table 1.
The ﬁ eld work monitored the following parameters:
• Electrical energy consumed by each A/C system (kWe
We
W)
recorded at one-minute intervals using energy meters and
data loggers.
• The system electrical energy consumption (kWe
We
W )
broken down by major subsystem components, including
chillers, pumps, fan coils, reheat and controls to differenti-
ate between refrigeration and system energy performance
and further understand the main end-uses of the energy
consumed by each system. For the packaged DX system,
this was impractical and inappropriate as it is a single
packaged system.
• The cooling output in terms of thermal energy (kWth) of
the system for each interval of electrical energy consump-
tion data. For the fan coil systems, the cooling output was
calculated from the temperature differential and water ﬂ ow
rate across the evaporator for each liquid chiller. The cool-
ing output of the packaged DX split system was calculated
from measurements of the airﬂ ow rate and the difference in
air temperature and humidity across the supply and return
airﬂ ow of the internal cassette unit, measured using an array
of sensors and data-loggers.
• Ambient temperature and RH at the building.
To make comparisons between the different system types
and fully account for all the loads associated with each system,
the following expressions of performance have been deﬁ ned
to enable comparison to published rated efﬁ ciencies and the
performance of different systems.
System Efﬁ ciency: The efﬁ ciency with which the system
serves the cooling load, accounting for all the loads of the entire
system, including distribution pumps, fan coils and reheat.
Refrigeration Efﬁ ciency: The efﬁ ciency with which the re-
frigeration cycle achieves the cooling load, but only accounting
for the energy consumption of the refrigeration plant, i.e., com-
pressors and heat rejection equipment. The energy consumed
by distribution pumps, fan coils or reheat is not included.
Measured Time of Operation
This section discusses the system operational hours and
cooling load proﬁ les that the systems experienced during the
monitoring. Over the monitoring period, the four systems
operated between 14% and 97% of the time, a wide range of
operational hours, but not unexpected when considering the
wide differences in the loads served, occupancy and controls
regimes at the different sites. Table 2 further details the per-
centage of time each system was operational by season. One-
hundred percent means the plant operated 24 hours a day.
Systems 2 and 3 are probably serving higher loads over
longer periods of time, explaining their higher hours of op-
eration and, in particular, System 2’s extended hours of use.
System 2’s operation was well beyond the normal occupancy
for this building and reﬂ ects the substantial process cooling
demands from a large information technology (IT) center. The
This article was ﬁ rst presented at the Building Performance Congress in
April 2004.
28
ASHRAE Journal ashrae.org
February 2005
increased cooling hours of this system in winter implies the
system might be conﬂ icting with the separate heating system
in a few areas of the site.
Only System 4 used localized user controls, which coupled
with the relatively well-informed occupants resulted in greatly
reduced hours of operation compared to the other systems, on
average operating only 14% of the time. This had a signiﬁ cant
impact on the energy consumption of this system, though has
no impact on the instantaneous efﬁ ciencies discussed later in
the article.
Measured Frequency of System Load
This section discusses the loads to which each system was
subjected to over the monitoring period in relation to their
cooling capacity. Figures 1 to 4 show the percentage of time
various cooling loads were placed on each system during their
operation over the monitoring period, expressed as a percent-
age of the system’s full-load capacity. These frequency proﬁ les
of part-load operation are related, therefore, to the cooling
loads placed on the system, when the various loads occur, and
the relationship between the cooling capacity of the system
and the load served, i.e., the sizing of the chiller plant.
Figures 1 to 4 show the wide range of cooling loads to which
the monitored systems were subjected, and also highlight how
infrequently the chillers are required to operate at or even
near their rated full-load capacity. On average these systems
operated between 8% and 44% of their full-load capacity as
detailed in Table 3.

ASHRAE Journal
29
In Figure 2, the increased frequency of loads at 10%to 15% of
the system’s full load shows the importance of this component
of the load to the overall system performance.
Further investigation of the data (not shown) revealed that
the majority of these loads occurred during the time when the
building was not occupied, which affected the average system
load shown in Table 3.
Figure 4 illustrates the effect of local occupant control on
the system part-load proﬁ le, in which the A/C system was
switched on only when required, eliminating the majority of
part-load operation at levels below 25% of the system’s full-
load capacity.
Signiﬁ cantly these ﬁ gures also highlight the occurrence of
plant oversizing, since Systems 1 and 3 had at least twice, and
in the case of System 3, possibly even three times, the cooling
capacity they required over the monitoring period since they
virtually never operated above 30% to 40% of their full load.
System Energy Consumption
This section discusses the energy consumption of the A/C
systems in terms of their annual energy consumption compared
to national benchmarks4 and the breakdown of energy consumed
by the various subsystems where obtained.
The measured total annual energy consumption of each of
the systems is detailed in Table 4, including each system’s
performance compared to the current UK benchmarks of
A/C system energy consumption6 in UK ofﬁ ces.\* The energy
consumption of System 2 at 148 kWh/m2 was the only system
to exceed the typical practice energy consumption benchmark
and, therefore, would be considered a high-energy consum-
ing site. However, as previously mentioned this was expected
due to the high process loads and extended hours of opera-
tion at this site.
The energy consumption of Systems 1 and 4 were 18%
below and 1% above the national good practice benchmark,
respectively. Therefore, both would be considered low energy
consuming sites. System 3, on the other hand, would be consid-
ered a moderate energy-consuming site since its annual energy
consumption of 58 kWh/m2 places it 36% below typical practice
but 31% above good practice.
Table 4 shows the breakdown of energy consumption by
subsystem components for the non-packaged systems. Signiﬁ -
cantly, the non-refrigeration components of all of the fan coil
systems consume around 60% of the total energy consumption
of the systems. This is what substantially reduces the overall
efﬁ ciency of the entire A/C system when compared to published
laboratory chiller test data for individual units.

ASHRAE Journal ashrae.org
February 2005
Refrigeration, System & System Part-Load Efﬁ ciency
This section discusses the measured daily efficiency
of the refrigeration aspects of each system, the overall
system efﬁ ciency once all of the ancillary loads are taken into
account, and the typical part-load performance of each system
during the monitoring period. This analysis has been conduct-
ed in two different ways to make a fair comparison between
the different system types and to identify the primary issues
that affected the efficiency of the systems during the
monitoring.
In the ﬁ rst instance, the average daily efﬁ ciency of both the
whole A/C system and refrigeration cycles of each system
has been calculated, using the total system input and output
energy over the operational period of an entire day. This
method accounts for all of the energy provided by the system
including that provided during periods of system thermal
inertia, where the system continues to provide cooling even
though the compressors have ceased to operate.
In the second instance, the calculated instantaneous
efﬁ ciency data has been manually cleaned to remove any
data where cooling occurs four minutes or more after
the compressor has ceased to operate. This method pro-
vides data relevant to the operational periods of the refrig-
eration plant and has been used to assess the relationship
of energy efﬁ ciency to system load averaged over 15 minute
intervals.
Figure 5 shows the average daily cooling efﬁ ciency ratio
(CER) for System 1 plotted against the average outdoor tem-
perature for both the refrigeration components and the entire
fan coil system.
The refrigeration CER ranged from 2.0 to 4.5, which
compares favorably to the chiller’s rated cooling efﬁ ciency
of 2.48 EER (Energy Efﬁ ciency Ratio). However, once the
other system loads are included the system CER drops sub-
stantially to between 0.5 and 2.0 with the overall average
peak being around 1.6 at an average external temperature of
22°C (72°F). The ﬁ gure also shows that a relationship exists
between external temperature and the refrigeration efﬁ ciency
and, therefore, also to the overall system efﬁ ciency. Figure 5
shows that the efﬁ ciencies drop off during periods of lower
and higher outdoor temperatures.
Figure 6 also shows that there is a clear variation of ef-
6
ﬁ ciency with percentage of system load, with higher system
loads resulting in higher system efﬁ ciencies. When we com-
pare this relationship to the average system load of 21% of
full cooling load (Table 3), it suggests that the typical system
efﬁ ciency over the monitoring period for this system was
between 1.0 and 1.4 CER.
Table 6: Summary of simulated A/C energy-efﬁ ciency performance.

Similar relationships between ambient outdoor tempera-
tures, system cooling load and A/C system energy efﬁ ciency
were observed in the other three systems. The overall ﬁ ndings
are summarized in Table 5.
Comparison of the ﬁ ndings from the four systems indicates
the following in terms of their relative system performance:
• The measured energy efficiency of the refrigeration
cycles in all four systems was broadly in line with their rated
efﬁ ciencies.
• The water-cooled chiller used in System 2 had the most
efﬁ cient refrigeration cycle, but the effect of this higher ef-
ﬁ ciency on the overall system efﬁ ciency was lost due to very
high ancillary energy consumption.
• The typical system cooling efficiency ratio of the
fan coil systems, weighted for their average system cooling
load, was, on average, 1.1 CER at 10% to 20% full-load
once the energy consumption of the entire system was taken
into account.
• The typical energy efﬁ ciency ratio of the DX split system,
weighted for its average system cooling load, was on average
1.5 CER at 44% of full-load or 40% higher than the average
fan coil systems.
• The peak daily system cooling efﬁ ciencies were mea-
sured to be between 1.4 and 1.7 for the fan coil systems and
3.4 for the DX system, on average 116% higher than the fan
coil systems.
System Efﬁ ciency Modeling
This section compares the ﬁ eld monitoring results with
ﬁ gures from independent building simulation research work
looking at seasonal efﬁ ciency of air conditioning systems
in UK ofﬁ ce buildings.7 The modeled seasonal efﬁ ciency
results were derived from detailed building and A/C sys-
tem simulations for a number of ofﬁ ce buildings including
standard benchmark designs,6 using the DOE28 and TAS9
building simulation software. In terms of comparisons, the
buildings modeled were not those monitored, nor was the
weather the same, although they were ofﬁ ce buildings located
in the southeast part of the UK. The simulation results are
summarized and compared to the typical measured system
efﬁ cacies in Table 5.
Some of the differences in Table 6 are undoubtedly due to
6 6
differences of deﬁ nition between the DOE2 simulations, where
fan energy has been apportioned between heating and cooling
relative to the proportion of the loads; the TAS simulation
data, which includes all fan energy loads; and the ﬁ eld data,
which includes all fan energy loads where this is part of the
method for delivery of cooling.
The measured range of cooling efﬁ ciencies was 0.3 to 1.6
CER for the fan coil systems and 1.3 to 1.7 CER for the DX
split system; the simulated seasonal efﬁ ciencies ranged from
0.82 to 1.16 and 1.15 to 1.95 for the fan coil and split DX
systems, respectively. The similarity of the efﬁ ciency ranges
indicates that the simulation results are broadly representative
of actual energy performance of UK A/C systems in practice
for the two system types being compared.
Assuming the simulation results accurately represent the
energy performance of A/C systems in practice, then the
simulation results shown in Table 6 for the other system
6
types, suggest that the various chilled ceiling conﬁ gurations
and DX air-conditioning systems offer signiﬁ cant energy
efﬁ ciency advantages over other more traditional centralized
and partially centralized system types.
Conclusions
The monitored energy efﬁ ciency of the fan coil and DX
split systems showed that refrigeration energy efﬁ ciency
varies with outdoor temperature and the load placed on the
system. Logically, the outdoor temperature and the system
load are interrelated (but not perfectly correlated) and, there-
fore, must be considered together when assessing the actual
operational efﬁ ciency of refrigeration plant and air-condition-
ing systems.
This relationship between system load and ambient tem-
perature that affects the refrigeration efﬁ ciency resulted in the
optimum energy efﬁ ciency occurring at ambient temperatures
between 20°C to 25°C (68°F to 77°F). At outdoor temperatures
higher than optimum, the efﬁ ciency drops even though system
loads are generally higher, presumably due to the increased
temperature lift required of the refrigeration cycle. Similarly,
at lower outdoor temperatures the system efﬁ ciency also
drops as system loads are generally much lower, resulting in
reduced system efﬁ ciency, as the constant energy demands
of the system such as fans, pumps and controls become pro-
portionally higher compared to the total energy consumption
of the whole system.
The measured energy efﬁ ciency of the refrigeration cycles
in all four systems studied was broadly in line with their
rated efﬁ ciencies. The water-cooled chiller in System 2 had
the most efﬁ cient refrigeration cycle as expected, but the
effect of this higher efﬁ ciency on the overall system efﬁ -
ciency was lost due to very high ancillary energy consumption
per capacity.
Comparison of the measured energy efﬁ ciency of each sys-
tem shows that the DX system was generally more efﬁ cient
over the monitoring period than the three fan coil systems
once energy used by the entire system, including fan coils,
pumps and reheat, was considered. Based on typical op-
erating efﬁ ciencies weighted by average system load, the
DX split system was around 40% more efﬁ cient than the fan
coil systems.
The energy performance of the DX system was further
improved by effective local controls, including time clocks
and easy occupant interfaces that contributed not only the
32
ASHRAE Journal
February 2005
higher efﬁ ciency while running but also
in significantly lower annual energy
consumption through reduced opera-
tion time.
This monitoring also has reinforced
the importance of appropriate plant siz-
ing and its effect on energy efﬁ ciency,
since a clear relationship between
system load and energy efﬁ ciency was
observed in all of the systems studied,
and two of the four systems had at least
twice the cooling capacity they required,
i.e., never operating above 40% to 50%
of their full load.
It is not possible to assess the im-
provement in energy efficiency that
could occur were the chillers sized
more appropriately, as we are unable to
say what effect control issues were
having on the energy performance
without further investigation, because of
the uncertainty of when the reheat
occurred.
Since the measured energy perfor-
mance of the systems studied here
were in line with the seasonal efﬁ cien-
cies predicted through building simula-
tion, greater conﬁ dence can be assigned
to such simulation results in the future.
Assuming these simulation results
actually represent the performance
of other system types, it also suggests
that the generic chilled ceiling and
DX system types offer signiﬁ cant en-
ergy efﬁ ciency advantages over other
system types.
Further investigation is necessary to
determine the actual in-use efﬁ ciency of
the other system types including all-air,
chilled ceiling and variable refrigerant
ﬂ ow/variable refrigerant volume sys-
tems, as well as the energy performance
implications of control issues including
the use of electric reheat batteries and
plant system sizing. Once this infor-
mation has been obtained, informed
decisions can be made about choice
of system type, controls and sizing for
given situations. From the data shown
here and the larger energy consumption
study, the potential energy savings are
very signiﬁ cant.
Acknowledgments
The Welsh School of Architecture
wish to acknowledge the funding for this
research from the Carbon Trust’s Action
Energy Programme and the technical as-
sistance provided by Toshiba Carrier Air
Conditioning UK Ltd.

About the Author
James B. (Burt) Rishel, P.E., is owner of Pumping
Solutions, Cincinnati.
By James B. (Burt) Rishel, P.E., Fellow/Life Member ASHRAE
Simplifying
Contemporary
HVAC Piping
Without manual adjustment, balance
valves and multiple duty valves are no
longer needed. Also, precise control of
pump speed has proved that piping ar-
rangements used to control pump ﬂ ow
and pressure are unnecessary. What is
left are variable speed pumps and coil
control valves that provide the pressures
and ﬂ ows required in any part of a water
system under all load conditions. In the
following ﬁ gures, the manual isolation
valves are not shown nor are the air
separators or expansion tanks. All of
these items are substantially the same in
all ﬁ gures.
Let us start with a simple, single zone
chilled water system that would have had
constant speed pumps with total manual
adjustment, Figure 1. This was the con-
ﬁ guration regardless of any cooling load
variation on it. Figure 1 was a ﬁ ne system
circa 1960, before the days of affordable
variable frequency drives. If you have
T
he advent of digital control and variable speed pumping has
decreased the amount of equipment needed to control water
ﬂ ow in chilled and hot water distribution systems. This applies to most
hot and chilled water systems that have variable heating and cooling
loads. Gone are balance valves, multiple duty valves, primary/secondary
pumping, cross-over bridges and other energy-consuming piping ac-
cessories. Almost all that is left are the pumps, piping, isolation valves,
and coils with control valves. Adaptive control provides a continually
adjusting program that completely eliminates manual operation.
The following article was published in ASHRAE Journal, February 2005. © Copyright 2005 American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and
Air-Conditioning Engineers, Inc. It is presented for educational purposes only. This article may not be copied and/or distributed electronically
or in paper form without permission of ASHRAE.
February 2005
ASHRAE Journal
17
a constant load, such as in a computer
center with no external exposure, it is
still worth consideration.
Today, this system with variable ﬂ ow
would most likely have only variable pri-
mary pumps with no balancing whatsoever
(Figure 2
(F
(
). The coil control valves would
be of a quality that would prevent overﬂ ow
of any coil, regardless of its position in the
water system. Hundreds of systems such
as this are in operation. No need exists to
control the ﬂ ow of the pumps manually,
since the pump speed control can be set
for any desirable maximum speed.
The advent of variable primary pump-
ing began around 1994. Initially, chiller
manufacturers had to be conservative
with the allowable minimum ﬂ ow. It was
generally limited to a minimum evapora-
tor tube velocity of 3 fps (0.9 m/s).
Today, some chiller manufacturers
have reduced this limit to as low as 1.5
fps (0.5 m/s) at part load on the chiller.
Ultimately, it is believed that this ac-
ceptable minimum ﬂ ow question will
be solved by the chiller manufacturers
themselves by equipping each chiller
with an integral circulator and reducing
the minimum ﬂ ow requirement through
improved refrigerant control.
Due to this variation in minimum tube
velocity requirement for different chiller
manufacturers, it is imperative that each
project have the minimum ﬂ ow veriﬁ ed
to ensure compliance with the chiller
manufacturer’s requirements.
Many HVAC systems are much more
complicated than the simple system of
Figures 1 and 2. Figure 3 describes a
traditional primary/secondary, constant
flow, chilled water system with four
zones. It has constant speed pumps
with totally manual adjustment. All of
the pumps would have been equipped
with balance valves and check valves as
well as isolation valves. The cross-over
bridges would have had return control
valves that decoupled the secondary and
tertiary (zone) pumps.
It is apparent that considerable energy
waste will be in this manually balanced
system if it were applied to a variable
load application. The energy losses
would be appreciable in the cross-over
bridge valves in the building zones. If the
zones are far apart, much of the energy of
the secondary pumps is lost in the zone
valves for the zones near the chiller plant.
Along with the energy loss through the
bridge/return valves, the balance valves
on all of the pumps and cooling coils
typically would be throttled, resulting in
energy waste. This indicates that great
energy waste occurs if we try to ﬁ t a
constant speed system to a variable load
installation.
Conversions of constant speed systems
to variable speed applications without the
need for manual balancing have resulted
in signiﬁ cant energy savings for the vari-
able ﬂ ow system.
This was demonstrated in the retroﬁ t
of a hotel in Southern California.1 In this
case, the cooling production and distri-
bution equipment was downsized from
Gone are balance valves, multiple duty valves,
primary/secondary pumping, cross-over bridges
and other energy-consuming piping accessories.
Almost all that is left are the pumps, piping,
isolation valves, and coils with control valves.
18
ASHRAE Journal ashrae.org
February 2005

Valve

Figure 2: Contemporary variable ﬂ ow system.
FT
Chiller
Chiller
Check
Valve
the original chiller plant, and it deﬁ ned
the ability to reduce the capacity of
the pumping equipment, chillers, and
cooling towers when changing to vari-
able ﬂ ow. After conversion to variable
primary pumping using a smaller chiller
plant, the author of the article that
described the project stated: “the data
show that the VPF [variable primary
ﬂ ow] chiller plant saved approximately
68% of the pre-retrofit base energy
consumption.”
Several contemporary procedures
exist for pumping a four-zone system
such as that shown in Figure 3. The
distance between the zones is used to
determine the best procedure for pump-
ing this system. If the loads are close
together in one building, only one set
of pumps is required (Figure 4
(F
(
). This
represents a typical variable primary
pumping system. The short distance
to the farthest load does not impose a
high distribution piping system friction on the load nearest to
the chiller plant. If each zone is a building and a considerable
distance exists between the buildings, distributed pumping
should be considered to avoid excessive pump head in the
nearby buildings (Figure 5
(F
(
).
The only chilled water pumps required in the chiller plant
itself are the minimum ﬂ ow circulators required to protect the
chillers. This situation requires the use of an automatically
controlled isolation valve on each chiller return piping to stop
ﬂ ow in that chiller when it is not supplying cooling to the chilled
water system. Without these isolation valves, uncooled water
would pass through an inoperable chiller and mix with cooled
water from operating chillers. This would elevate the supply
water temperature above the desired temperature.
A friction loss of 8 to 14 ft (24 to 42 kPa) or more through
pump ﬁ ttings such as suction strainers, check valves, suction
reducers, discharge increasers, isolation valves and piping
is saved whenever a pumping system is eliminated. Quality
design limits this loss to 8 ft (24 kPa) while poor designs will
reveal losses as high as 14 ft (42 kPa) or more of pump head.
In this case, variable primary pumping would, therefore, save as
much as 16 to 28 ft (48 to 84 kPa) of pump head by eliminating
secondary and tertiary pumping installations.
Minimum flow circulators have a relatively low motor
horsepower requirement. Assume that a chiller plant has 2,000
ton (7034 kW) chillers, each with a design ﬂ ow of 4,000 gpm
(252 L/s). Additionally, assume that the chiller evaporators
were selected at 7 fps (2 m/s) with a minimum allowable ﬂ ow
at part-load operation of 1.5 fps (0.45). The minimum ﬂ ow
would be 1.5/7 of 4,000 gpm or 860 gpm (252 L/s or 544 L/s).
If the friction loss through the evaporator were 21 ft at 4,000
gpm (63 kPa at 252 L/s), the design head for the minimum ﬂ ow
pumps would be (1.5/7)2 · 21 or 1 ft (3 kPa) plus the piping and
pump ﬁ tting loss.

there is adequate pressure provided by the secondary pumps,
this will be indicated by a rise in the differential pressure at
the building differential pressure transmitters, DP2. After
a time delay, the building pumps would be stopped and
the building differential pressure transmitters would control
the secondary pumps. Likewise, when the building load
increases and the building pumps are needed, they will
restart automatically. All of these piping and control proce-
Assume the piping and ﬁ tting loss to
be 16 ft (48 kPa), so the design head for
the minimum ﬂ ow pumps would be 17 ft
(51 kPa). If the pumps have an efﬁ ciency
of 80%, the brake horsepower would be
4.6 hp (3.4 kW) and the pumps would
be equipped with 5 hp (3.7 kW) motors.
The pumps would be variable speed, so
the actual energy consumption would
be quite low. Since the loss through the
evaporator is so low that it may not be
represented by a smooth curve, it should
be veriﬁ ed by the chiller manufacturer.
Control of these circulators should be
accomplished either by a chiller plant
ﬂ ow control as shown in Figure 5, or
it can be by differential pressure trans-
mitters connected across the chiller
evaporators (Figure 6).
Recognizing the inefﬁ ciency of many
existing chilled water systems, it is pos-
sible to retroﬁ t a constant ﬂ ow system
of Figure 3 into a more efﬁ cient system
through the use of the existing pumps
and piping (Figure 7).
• All of the pumps would be converted
to variable speed.
• The minimum flow through the
chillers would be controlled by a new
bypass valve.
• The speed of the chiller pumps
would be controlled by a new differential
pressure transmitter, DP1, whose setting
would be determined by the controller
for all of the pumps.
• The secondary pumps would
be controlled by the differential trans-
mitter, DP2, at the far end of the chilled
water loop.
This would cost less than changing the
pumps and piping to a variable primary
pumping system shown in Figure 4.
However, a consulting engineer could
make a careful evaluation to determine
if it is economically feasible to downsize the pumps, chillers,
and cooling towers as in the previously mentioned article.
The tertiary pumps in the buildings would be controlled
by differential pressure transmitters, DP2, at the correct lo-
cation in each building, possibly at the top of each zone as
shown. A bypass check valve would be located around each
of the building pumps, and the return valves with all of their
energy waste would be eliminated. Under light load, when

DP
All of the previous ﬁ gures show the chillers and their pumps
assembled in tandem. It may be more economical to provide
these pumps headered as shown in Figure 8. This could provide
better programming of the pumps and offer the opportunity to
overﬂ ow the evaporators in event of low chilled water return
temperatures. Recognizing that chiller output is ﬂ ow times the
differential temperature, the chiller output can be sustained by
increasing the ﬂ ow during the existence of reduced differen-
tial temperatures. This has become common due to the many
instances of low return temperatures in chilled water systems.
All chillers have a maximum allowable velocity such as 10 fps
(3 m/s) in the evaporator. The chiller manufacturer should be
consulted before overﬂ ow is attempted.
ASHRAE recognizes that the parasitic load of pumps and
cooling towers may equal or exceed the energy consumption
of the chillers themselves at light loads. Efforts are being made
to develop a guideline to help determine the total energy con-
sumption of a chiller plant, not just the kW/ton of the chillers
themselves.
Another signiﬁ cant improvement in HVAC water-distribution
systems is the realization that the quality of coil control valves
must be such that they will withstand the broad differential
pressures that have always existed in these systems. In the past,
many of these coil valves were of a commercial grade that could
not withstand the differential pressures imposed upon them.
Today, the use of industrial quality valves as well as pressure
independent valves has improved the differential temperature
and dramatically reduced the ﬂ ow in these systems. This mat-
dures are in operation on many chilled
water systems.
A number of the ﬁ gures in this article
illustrate the use of differential pressure
transmitters as the means of controlling
pump speed. So far, this is a proven method during the past 25
years. Contemporary digital electronics indicate the possibility
of mass ﬂ ow as a means of pump speed control. Unfortunately,
this procedure also requires physical location of each individual
cooling load because friction to a speciﬁ c load is determined
by the distance of that load from the pumping system. The
total calculation then becomes quite complicated. So far, the
author has not seen total pump control software that recognizes
this fact.
Another explanation should be made concerning the piping
diagrams in this article. All of the ﬁ gures describe equal fric-
tion losses in the supply and return mains. Such is not always
the case on large systems. Often, the return piping has different
circuiting and, therefore, unequal friction losses. The return
piping should be evaluated as carefully as the supply piping.
One advantage of using cross-over bridges and tertiary
pumps is the ability to blend warm return water with cold
supply water to operate the zones at higher temperatures than
those developed in the chiller plant. With the quality of today’s
piping insulation, however, little need exists for such blending.
If a research or process facility requires colder chilled water,
it is usually better to provide a booster chiller for that facility
rather than absorb the energy penalty produced by generating
the entire cooling load at a reduced temperature. For example,
if a research facility requires 40°F (4°F) chilled water, the
booster chiller could reduce the 45°F (7°F) distribution water
temperature. Obviously, this is an engineering design problem
requiring evaluation for each speciﬁ c case.

ter of valve authority and quality should
be reviewed thoroughly in a separate
technical paper.
Another development that has en-
hanced the efﬁ ciency of these systems
has been that of a running limit on
contemporary variable frequency drives.
This prevents the pump from overload-
ing the drive. This means that the drive
and motor can be sized at the design
condition rather than at the pump run-
out condition.
For example, assume that a chilled
water pump requires 29 bhp (22 kW)
at the design condition and 35 bhp (26
kW) at the run-out condition or the non-
overloading point on the pump curve.
With older drives, it would have been
necessary to equip the pump with a 40
hp (30 kW) motor and drive. With this
running limit, the pump can be furnished
with a 30 hp (22 kW) motor and drive
without concern for the horsepower re-
quirement at the pump run-out condition.
This reduces the ﬁ rst cost of equipment
and total electrical installation. It also
provides higher wire-to-shaft efﬁ ciencies
at reduced loads on the system.
A recent development has been the use
of geothermal energy for cooling and
heating. Unfortunately, there has been
some confusion about the capabilities
of these thermal sources during variable
ﬂ ow. If the thermal source, such as a well
ﬁ eld or lake, is capable of producing the
energy required at design ﬂ ow, there will

be adequate thermal capacity at reduced ﬂ ow. Misunderstanding
this has resulted in the application of complicated pumping and
piping systems (Figure 9
(F
(
).
Consulting engineers, who understand the concept of the
variation in heat ﬂ ow at reduced ﬂ ow, have developed simple
pumping and piping systems as shown in Figure 10. A number
of consulting ﬁ rms have many installations in operation with
this conﬁ guration. This demonstrates that no need exists for
separate well pumps or balancing valves on the circulating
pumps or the building heat pumps.
Summary
The most signiﬁ cant fact about all HVAC water systems is not
the system’s conﬁ guration, but what are the hours of operation at
all percentages of ﬂ ow, from minimum ﬂ ow to maximum design
ﬂ ow? Only after receiving this information, can an engineer
make use of the suggestions here on the reduction of ﬁ rst cost
and increase of overall operating efﬁ ciency.
The increase in quality of coil control valves and the
added software for the control of these valves and pumps has
resulted in simplicity and greater efﬁ ciency for most of the
HVAC industry’s chilled and hot water distribution systems.
Today’s level of ability and competency of the designers and
operators of these systems permits the use of this technology
on all variable volume, HVAC systems—not just on special
installations.
